Vision-based methods such as PALMS enable infrastructure-free localization using only a floor plan and a stationary scan, but are limited by the short range of smartphone LiDAR and ambiguity in indoor layouts. We propose PALMS+, a modular, image-based system that addresses these challenges by reconstructing scale-aligned 3D point clouds from posed RGB images using a foundation monocular depth estimation model (Depth Pro), followed by geometric layout matching via convolution with the floor plan. PALMS+ outputs a posterior over the location and orientation, usable for direct or sequential localization. Evaluated on the Structured3D and a custom campus dataset consisting of 80 observations across four large campus buildings, PALMS+ outperforms PALMS and F<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>Loc in stationary localization accuracy—without requiring any training. Furthermore, when integrated with a particle filter for sequential localization on 33 real-world trajectories, PALMS+ achieved lower localization errors compared to other methods, demonstrating robustness for camera-free tracking and its potential for infrastructure-free applications. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Head-inthe-Cloud/PALMS-Plane-based-Accessible-Indoor-Localization-Using-Mobile-Smartphones.

title: An Adaptive Federated Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Explainable Deep Learning and Smart Decision-Making in Healthcare IoT Using MIMIC-IV
authors: Vinitha Vincent, Harshitha Saraswathi R S, Srikanth R, N.P. Dharani, M. UmaDevi, Sudharsan Kalyanakumar
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The fast growth of Internet of Things (IoT) in healthcare has allowed real-time patient data monitoring, but issues of data privacy, explainability, and adaptive decision-making are not yet resolved. This paper proposes AFNS-IoT, an Adaptive Federated Neuro-Symbolic IoT framework that integrates deep learning, neuro-symbolic reasoning, and reinforcement learning for intelligent and explainable healthcare decision-making. Leveraging the MIMIC-IV dataset, local IoT edge nodes are trained using federated deep learning models to preserve patient privacy, while a multi-objective weighted aggregation balances accuracy, latency, and device constraints. A neuro-symbolic fusion layer translates model outputs into interpretable symbolic rules, providing transparent decision pathways for clinical decision support. Furthermore, a reinforcement learning feedback loop adaptively refines the system based on real-world outcomes. Experimental results demonstrate that AFNS-IoT achieves the highest accuracy (0.94), F1-score (0.92), and AUC (0.95), outperforming all baseline models. Furthermore, it reduces inference latency to 140 ms, compared to 220 ms in centralized learning, while achieving superior energy efficiency (0.88). Unlike conventional deep learning and federated approaches that lack interpretability, AFNS-IoT provides very high interpretability fidelity through its neuro-symbolic fusion layer. The findings demonstrate how AFNS-IoT can be used as the next-generation framework of privacy, explainable and adaptive healthcare IoT systems.

title: Research on the Construction and Application of Design Metaverse Teaching Environment Integrating AIGC and Virtual Simulation
authors: Weiqing Guo
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The use of Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) and virtual simulation technologies has transformed the process of design education and provided new means of improving learning outcomes. This research paper seeks to examine how Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, Immersion, and Cognitive Load change Learning Performance and Behavioural Intention to Use in a design metaverse teaching environment. The quantitative survey was used, and 300 students were used to gather data. The findings demonstrate that there is a high degree of positive impact of Perceived Usefulness and Immersion on Learning Performance, and Cognitive Load on Behavioural Intention to Use. The results of the study indicate that Perceived Usefulness and Immersion are crucial in driving the learning performance, and Cognitive Load has a role to play in determining the desire of the students to continue using the platform. The conclusion outlines the relevance of usability and immersive experiences to improve the outcomes of learning. Some of the suggestions would be to incorporate AIGC and immersive technologies into the curriculum to enhance student engagement. The implications of the study indicate that this kind of environment may contribute greatly to achieving effective learning and adoption in the long run. The shortcomings, however, are that the sample used is homogeneous, and this might not represent the greater educational reality.

title: Variance-Weighted Random Forests for Robust Keystroke Dynamics Authentication
authors: Subhadeep Sasmal, Rajashree Konwar, Indrajit Banerjee
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Password-based authentication is becoming increasingly vulnerable to brute-force, phishing, and social engineering attacks, which makes stronger secondary security methods necessary. Keystroke dynamics, a behavioral biometric based on typing patterns, provides a simple and low-cost solution. In this work, we propose Variance-Weighted Random Forest (VWRF), a enhanced Random Forest model that uses how much each feature changes across sessions to estimate its stability and that is used for both feature selection and ensemble voting. By giving more importance to stable features and reducing the influence of session-sensitive features, our method becomes more robust to cross-session variation. We evaluated the method on the CMU DSL-StrongPassword dataset and showed that our method achieves very high performance (AUC: 0.9997) while also producing more consistent results across users. Levene's test shows a 37.5% reduction in the standard deviation of AUC (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$p<0.001$</tex>) compared to standard Random Forest, which confirms the improvement in stability. At the same time, VWRF keeps the simplicity, efficiency, and interpretability of standard Random Forest, making it suitable for practical behavioral authentication systems.

title: Reviving Artificial Emotional Intelligence: Deep-Learning Frameworks For Context-Aware Multi-Sentiment Understanding
authors: Bodduna Sriveni, Pramod Kumar P, Satish Muppidi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Multi-sentiment analysis is a more modern and more sophisticated branch of Natural Language Processing that is no longer binary or even ternary in its ability to tell a positive, negative or neutral text. It is more engaged with establishing a number of coexisting conditions of emotions of a shared text that contains the concept of the overlapping, delicate and context- specific nature of human emotions. As the social media and online forums continue to grow exponentially, and more users are producing reviews, huge quantities of information of unknown proportions of complex emotional data have come into view. The processing of this data to derive valuable information creates the need to have reasonable computational means adapted to understand the potential emotional quotient of language. Deep learning (DL) has revolutionized the field of study since the processing of hierarchical and contextual characteristics of raw text can automatically be accomplished. Neural Networks like CNN, RNN and LSTM as well as more modern attention driven transformer networks like BERT, RoBERTa and GPT have proven extremely successful in acquiring deep semantic representations and context dependencies. Nevertheless, even with these developments, there are still some challenges. These the limited availability of completely annotated multi-emotion datasets, challenges in relation-modelling inter-sentiment, expensive computation, interpretability and the ethical issues of bias and privacy of data. The present paper is the detailed introduction of the latest developments in the area of deep learning-based methods of multi sentiment analysis with a focus on hybrid models and multimodal models incorporating text, audio, and visual information. It shows research criticities including lack of contextual knowledge, inadequate model generalization and lack of interpretability and outlines the necessity of scalable, transparent, domain-adaptable deep learning structures. The eventual aim of the research is to stimulate the moving of models capable of perceiving and figuring out complicated human feelings and emotions, and to revive the emotional intelligence of the artificial systems in various fields, including business analytics, healthcare, mental health testing, and social media surveillance.

title: Words Around Me -Al-Powered Interactive Multilingual Learning
authors: Divya Gudibandla, Anam Mehvish, Nabeela Alam, Koduru Shrenika
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: The majority of online learning applications do not yet take into account the way a young child speak and communicate, even though language learning at an early age is essential in cognitive and social growth. Our application will be kid-friendly and tailored specifically to children between the age of 3 and 10 with speech training, object recognition, flashcards, quizzes, and progress reports. It has two primary foundations: speech recognizer that is child-optimized to identify and correct mistakes in pronunciation and an interactive multilingual tutor that will adjust lessons based on conversation and picture-based prompts. Children play by describing real-world items and trying to speak in a natural way and the system adapts dynamically to their personal development. The vocabulary growth, fluency, and interaction can be tracked using a special dashboard by parents. On the initial tests, our strategy was better than the conventional language applications children achieved better vocabulary retention and a quantifiable increase in pronunciation. The findings underscore the manner in which the speech technology that is sensitive to errors, and adaptive instruction strategies can ensure effective, interesting, and powerful early language learning.

title: SafeguardGS: 3D Gaussian Primitive Pruning While Avoiding Catastrophic Scene Destruction
authors: Yongjae Lee, Zhaoliang Zhang, Deliang Fan
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has advanced novel view synthesis, but its densification process leads to an excessive number of Gaussian primitives, which negatively impact rendering speed and memory usage. Although many 3DGS pruning techniques have been proposed to address this issue, a comprehensive analysis is still lacking. In this paper, we are the first to categorize 3DGS pruning techniques into two approaches: scene-level importance-threshold-based pruning and pixel-level importance-rank-based pruning, defined by their scope of importance calculation (scene-level vs. pixel-level) and their pruning criteria (threshold vs. rank). Our studies reveal that while the former leads to disastrous quality drops under extreme Gaussian primitive decimation, the latter not only sustains high rendering quality but also provides a natural pruning boundary, i.e., a safeguard for Gaussian pruning. We further propose multiple pruning score functions. From our extensive studies on various pruning score functions, we discover that color similarity with blending weight is the most effective factor for identifying insignificant primitives. In our experiments, our proposed method, SafeguardGS, with the selected score function achieves the highest PSNR-per-primitive performance under extreme pruning setting, retaining only about 10% of the primitives from the original 3DGS scene, i.e., 10× compression ratio.

title: HalluciSafe: Entailment-Driven Hallucination Suppression in LLM-Assisted Manuscripts
authors: Abirami A, S. Ponmaniraj
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The paper presents a hallucination suppression framework, HalluciSafe, that is an entailment-based system that upholds the suppression of hallucinations during the generation of academic manuscripts with the help of large language models (LLM). The system is applied in the form of the multi-stage pipeline, incorporating prompt-level risk control, claim-level verification, and human-in-the-loop correction. The LLM-generated manuscript drafts are initially broken down into factual claims as atoms with dependency-based claim extraction deployed during implementation. All of the claims are checked by a transformer-based Natural Language Inference model using human-written text and a reference corpus. It uses a Prompt Risk Scoring Algorithm to actively limit risky and unconstrained prompts prior to generation and avoids hallucination-prone results. Neutral that are contradicted claims are candidate to confidence masking and are refined, through author intervention over a structured refinement process. HalluciSafe experimental evaluation on the LLM-generated academic drafts indicates that the hallucination detection accuracy of HalluciSafe is 91.0%, surpassing by more than 11% the F1-score of only using NLI. Early risk control further lowers the incidence of hallucinations by over 60% of the high-risk prompts. The findings validate the fact that HalluciSafe is effective in reducing hallucination to a proactive and curative control process, which facilitates credible and dependable scholarship writing supported by the help of the LLM.

title: Key Parameters Optimization of DC Short-Circuit Current in Hybrid MMCs for GW Level UHVDC Transmission Systems
authors: Xin Yuan, Junda Qin, Nan Deng, Qing Wang
publish: 2025 4th International Conference on Renewable Energy and Electrical Technology (ICREET)
abstract: The hybrid modular multilevel converter (MMC), which is composed of half-bridge submodules (HBSMs) and fullbridge submodules (FBSMs), has become a significant research focus in flexible high-voltage direct current (HVDC) transmission. This is due to its unique blocking-free DC fault ride-through capability. First, taking the ± 800 kV Gansu-Zhejiang UHVDC project as a representative case, this study describes the topology and operational principles of hybrid MMCs in the project. Next, the impact of both the FBSM proportion ratio and DC reactor parameters on short-circuit currents during system faults is analyzed. Finally, a PSCAD/EMTDC model based on GansuZhejiang project is built. Simulation results conclusively demonstrate that optimized FBSM/HBSM proportion ratios and appropriately selected reactor parameters can significantly limit short-circuit currents.

title: TranSight: Dual-Attention Spatio-Temporal Network for Anomaly Detection in Intelligent Transportation Surveillance
authors: Rakesh Kumar, Arvind, Manish Kumar Sharma, Sumeet Saurav, Sanjay Singh
abstract: Video anomaly detection (VAD) is crucial in intelligent transportation surveillance, enabling the automated identification of irregular or unsafe behaviours in complex traffic environments. Challenges such as illumination changes, occlusions, and scarce labelled data hinder the capture of finegrained spatio-temporal patterns. This paper presents TranSight, an unsupervised attention-guided spatio-temporal learning framework for anomaly detection in transportation videos. TranSight employs a multi-scale Wider-ResNet encoder for hierarchical spatial representation, a Bi-directional Temporal Shift Module (Bi-TSM) for efficient motion encoding, and a Temporal Transformer to model long-range dependencies. A dual-attention refinement scheme, combining Residual Channel Attention Blocks (RCABs) and a Spatial-Temporal Attention (STA) module, enhances discriminative motion cues while suppressing background redundancy. The framework is optimized using a multi-loss strategy integrating pixel-level, gradient-based, perceptual, and contrastive objectives, balancing reconstruction fidelity with anomaly sensitivity. On the UCSD Ped2 dataset, TranSight achieves a video-level AUC of 98.0%, a global frame-label AUC of 30 ± 1.47, an anomaly ratio of 82.20%, and an overall AUC of 97.99%, outperforming transformer-based baselines with high robustness, interpretability, and efficiency for real-time intelligent transportation analytics.

title: ViGG: Robust RGB-D Point Cloud Registration using Visual-Geometric Mutual Guidance
authors: Congjia Chen, Shen Yan, Yufu Qu
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Point cloud registration is a fundamental task in 3D vision. Most existing methods only use geometric information for registration. Recently proposed RGB-D registration methods primarily focus on feature fusion or improving feature learning, which limits their ability to exploit image information and hinders their practical applicability. In this paper, we propose ViGG, a robust RGB-D registration method using mutual guidance. First, we solve clique alignment in a visual-geometric combination form, employing a geometric guidance design to suppress ambiguous cliques. Second, to mitigate accuracy degradation caused by noise in visual matches, we propose a visual-guided geometric matching method that utilizes visual priors to determine the search space, enabling the extraction of high-quality, noise-insensitive correspondences. This mutual guidance strategy brings our method superior robustness, making it applicable for various RGB-D registration tasks. The experiments on 3DMatch, ScanNet and KITTI datasets show that our method outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods in both learning-free and learning-based settings. Code is available at https://github.com/ccjccjccj/ViGG.

title: Handwritten Compound Odia Character Recognition using Deep Learning Architectures
authors: Subham Sahoo, Umakanta Majhi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The Odia language is spoken by more than 50 million+ people and it has a very rich literary heritage that is recorded in handwritten manuscripts and centuries of historical records. These documents have, however, been hard to digitize. Digitizing these materials remains challenging due to the complex structures of Odia characters and substantial handwriting variation across writers. We investigate compound Odia handwritten character recognition using deep learning. Our custom dataset consists of 5,760 handwritten samples across 64 compound character classes, collected systematically using standardized grid-based sheets. A comprehensive preprocessing pipeline was implemented, including geometric correction, skew correction, perspective transformation, and adaptive thresholding to extract clean character images. We evaluated five deep learning architectures: VGG-16, ResNet-18, DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B0, and MobileNet-V2 under two scenarios: standard testing and cross-writer generalization. DenseNet-121 achieved the highest standard test accuracy (96.64%). However, cross-writer experiments—testing on completely unseen writers—revealed EfficientNet-B0 demonstrated superior generalization (92.29% ± 2.16%) compared to DenseNet-121 (88.93% ± 5.06%).Performance differences are not statistically significant, according to statistical analysis using Mc-Nemar’s test and the paired t-test (p=0.503), which suggests similar cross-writer robustness. These results show that lightweight architectures like EfficientNet-B0 and MobileNet-V2 achieve competitive performance with significantly lower computational cost, making them appropriate for deployment in government administration, educational institutions, and archival preservation projects that have limited resources.

title: An Attention-Guided Multi-Scale Network for Brain Tumor Segmentation
authors: Tushar Chaurasia, Pranshu Jena, Umesh C. Pati
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The identification of the tumorous region in brain scans has been challenging due to the scarcity of quantitative data for evaluation, treatment planning, and monitoring disease progression. Manual detection of tumor regions has been a difficult task in brain tumor segmentation due to intricate boundaries and the varied characteristics of tumors. Conventional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) struggle to capture in-depth tumor features and the nuanced spatial relationships between tumor and non-tumor regions. Hence, this work effectively addresses the drawbacks of existing state-of-the-art frameworks by proposing an innovative, lightweight segmentation framework that employs the U-Net architecture integrated with a fine-tuned ResNet-34 backbone and the squeeze-and-excitation architecture. The focus on the tumor region is enhanced by the multi-head attention module, resulting in improved segmentation performance. The BraTS 2020 dataset is rigorously tested using the proposed framework, yielding a Dice score of 0.8247, which indicates competitive performance when compared to state-of-the-art models.

title: A Lightweight Transfer Learning Framework for Deepfake Audio Detection using Mel- Spectrograms
authors: Thuraka Sai Jeevan, K. Jai Ram, R. Rajmohan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The latest development of speech synthesis models and voice conversion models has contributed greatly to the realism of audio deepfakes, posing a grave threat associated with identity fraud, fake news, and computer security. To overcome this difficulty, this paper suggests an audio deepfake detector framework based on deep learning that involves Mel-spectrogram representations and a small convolutional neural network based on transfer learning. The suggested system converts audio signals into time-frequency representation and utilizes a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 model to perform binary classification of fake and legitimate audio samples. An audio preprocessing, model inference, and result visualization application architecture based on Streamlit is a monolithic application architecture. As experimental testing on the York University audio deepfake dataset shows, with an accuracy of 91.92, computationally efficient CNN models can indeed be used in real-time to detect audio deepfakes.

title: Dynamic Programming Optimization of LLM Cascade Ordering for Extracting Sleep Parameters from Mean Sleep Latency Tests
authors: Arash Maghsoudi, Nithin Weerasinghe, Amir Sharafkhaneh, Javad Razjouyan
publish: 2025 Conference on AI, Science, Engineering, and Technology (AIxSET)
abstract: The objective of this study is to develop and evaluate a scalable natural-language-processing (NLP) pipeline that accurately extracts mean sleep latency (MSL) from Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT) reports in Veterans Health Administration (VHA) records, while minimizing hallucinations and computational cost. We analyzed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 3 3}$</tex> million VHA clinical notes containing the terms MSLT or multiple sleep latency (1999 - 2025). A logistic-regression classifier using bigram features first identified genuine MSLT notes. Verified notes were processed with a 4-bit-quantized Llama-3.1-8B large language model (LLM); prompts were clinician-guided, and extractions were run at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.2-1.8$</tex> temperature settings (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\Delta=0.1$</tex>). To cut missrates without excessive compute, we framed temperature ordering as a dynamic-programming (DP) optimization that, under a predefined cost budget, chooses the cascade of LLM runs that maximizes overall accuracy. Manual annotation of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1, 8 2 5}$</tex> randomly sampled notes (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 4 6}$</tex> MSLT, 979 non-MSLT) served as ground truth. The note-identifier achieved mean accuracy <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=94.3 {\%}$</tex> and AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=0.981$</tex> (5-fold cross-validation). Filtering reduced hallucinations from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0. 8 \%}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5. 5 \%}$</tex> and misses from 12.8% to 2.4%, raising single-pass extraction accuracy from 73.3% to 87.0%. The DP-optimized cascade further lowered the miss-rate while respecting the cost budget, outperforming any individual-temperature run across all metrics. Clinically, using the 8 -minute MSL cut-off, the final system achieved a sensitivity of 98% and a positive predictive value of 97%.

title: A Hybrid Lightweight AI Framework for Skill Gap Analysis in HR Analytics
authors: Nazia Tasleem, Mohammed Nadeem Ansari
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Identifying skill gaps is a necessary condition of data-driven workforce development, but today, most of the current AI-based HR systems are either proprietary or computationally expensive deep-learning algorithms, which are less likely to be transparent and practically applicable in the real world. This paper is a hybrid lightweight AI framework, that is, the skill-gap analysis, which refers to both lexical and semantic data obtained in a resume and job description. The proposed model is a hybrid between TF-IDF and MiniLM sentence embeddings, which form a 684-dimensional hybrid feature representation of the candidate-job alignment to model the interpretable features. Evaluation of the discriminative capability of the hybrid feature space is done by the use of a Logistic Regression classifier and is mainly to measure feature separability as opposed to predictive performance. Besides this, there is the introduction of a transparent Skill Gap Index (SGI), which is the average relative absolute difference between the candidate features and job feature vectors, in order to measure relative skill mismatches. The stable SGI behavior and constant ranking of candidate suitability is shown in experiments that are done on representative pairs of resumes and job. The findings suggest that without causing a large computational burden, hybrid lexical-semantic representations are able to improve the quality of representations. In general, the suggested framework offers a scalable, interpretable, and reproducible basis of the skill-gap analysis in HR analytics, especially in settings with a limited amount of computational resources.

title: UAV-assisted MinMax Entropy and Artificial Intelligence-based Semi Supervised Domain Adaptation Approach for Rice Disease Detection
authors: Harnoor Kaur Khehra, Baljit Singh Khehra, Arjan Singh
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: In this paper, a semi-supervised domain adaptation approach is proposed that leverages a MinMax entropy strategy to address domain shift in rice disease detection. The method integrates UAV technology and consumer electronics, enabling scalable, field-level disease monitoring and detection. UAVs facilitate rapid data collection across diverse environments, while consumer electronics enhance accessibility and deployment of detection models. Several CNN architectures, including general CNN models and pretrained architectures like VGG16, ResNet50, and AlexNet, were evaluated for their ability to adapt from a labeled source domain to a target domain with limited labels. The proposed approach minimizes cross-entropy loss in the source domain while maximizing entropy loss in the target domain, ensuring better generalization. Through k-fold cross-validation, the performance of the models was assessed based on training, validation, and test losses, as well as validation and test accuracies. Experimental results highlight the efficiency of the proposed method in improving domain adaptability and enhancing the reliability of rice leaf disease detection, offering a scalable solution for modern agricultural challenges.

title: Language Integration in Fine-Tuning Multimodal Large Language Models for Image-Based Regression
authors: Roy H. Jennings, Genady Paikin, Roy Shaul, Evgeny Soloveichik
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show promise for image-based regression tasks, but current approaches face key limitations. Recent methods fine-tune MLLMs using preset output vocabularies and generic task-level prompts (e.g., "How would you rate this image?"), assuming this mimics human rating behavior. Our analysis reveals that these approaches provide no benefit over image-only training. Models using preset vocabularies and generic prompts perform equivalently to image-only models, failing to leverage semantic understanding from textual input. We propose Regression via Transformer-Based Classification (RvTC), which replaces vocabulary-constrained classification with a flexible bin-based approach. Unlike approaches that address discretization errors through complex distributional modeling, RvTC eliminates manual vocabulary crafting through straightforward bin increase, achieving state-of-the-art performance on four image assessment datasets using only images. More importantly, we demonstrate that data-specific prompts dramatically improve performance. Unlike generic task descriptions, prompts containing semantic information about specific images enable MLLMs to leverage cross-modal understanding. On the AVA dataset, adding challenge titles to prompts substantially improves our already state-of-the-art image-only baseline. We demonstrate through empirical evidence from the AVA and AGIQA-3k datasets that MLLMs benefit from semantic prompt information, surpassing mere statistical biases. We validate RvTC across two different MLLM architectures, demonstrating consistent improvements and method generalizability. <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: HyMamba-G: Hyperbolic Mamba-Based Clinical Gait Analysis for Efficient Occlusion-Robust Neurodegenerative Disease Screening
authors: Muduthanapelli Kiran, Vijaya Chandra Jadala
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Neuro-degenerative diseases like Parkinson disease should be detected at an early stage but sometimes, some minor motor defects in gait may go unnoticed by the normal means of diagnosis. Even though gait analysis can be performed through video-based methods, the current deep learning models are often susceptible to occlusions, computationally expensive, and ineffective in clinical environments. The presented paper presents a new gait analysis framework, HyMamba-G (Hyperbolic Mamba for Gait-based clinical analysis), which combines the hyperbolic geometric modeling with the efficient Mamba-based state-space architectures. The proposed approach has the advantage of incorporating skeletal representation into the hyperbolic space making it effective to capture the hierarchical nature of the human body focusing on distal joints like the ankles where initial pathological tremors tend to arise. Simultaneously, bidirectional Mamba architecture is a model of temporal gait dynamics with linear computational complexity making it able to efficiently process long sequences. A series of experiments carried out on a group of 149 participants shows that HyMamba-G has an accuracy of 84.6 and a sensitivity of 86.2 and it also remains strong when used in conditions of joint occlusion and frame drop. Moreover, the suggested framework has a frame rate of 88 frames per second, which is much more efficient when compared to Transformer-based baselines, which means that it can be used to conduct gait-based disease screening in real-time and in an automated manner.

title: Predictive Health Monitoring of Solenoid Coils Under Humid Aging Conditions
authors: Thomas Roulleau, Killian Corbel, Hani Al Hajjar, Frédéric Lamarque
abstract: This study presents an experimental approach to predict failure of alternative current solenoid coils, by linking internal temperature variation with the wire insulation degradation state due to the water ingress. (Abstract)

title: ConvKAN-Mixers : A New Deep Learning Architecture for Image Classification
authors: Swajeet Chikhali, Suleman Agasimani, J Nithish, Saideep Nayak, Uday Kulkarni, Arundati Aralimatti
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Interpretable deep learning models similar to traditional neural networks, including Kolmogorov Arnold Networks (KANs), and mixer models based on KANs, were proposed recently. Unfortunately, contemporary KAN, KAN-Mixers, MLPbased, and transformer-based models reveal poor scalability and reduced performance when performing image classification tasks on a larger scale. This work introduces ConvKAN-Mixers, an innovative hybrid architecture combining KAN-like functional transforms, token mixing layers, and convolutional patch embeddings. To create a valid basis for comparing, all baseline models (MLP, MLP-Mixers, KAN, KAN-Mixers, SineKAN, TSKKAN, ViT-Tiny, ResNet-18) were recreated from scratch and trained under the same experimental conditions, using AdamW optimizer, cosine learning rate scheduling, and uniform data augmentation with the ConvKAN-Mixers model. The proposed ConvKAN-Mixers model is demonstrated to be better than all eight baselines experimentally on four benchmark image classification datasets: CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, FashionMNIST, and Tiny-ImageNet. It achieves the best accuracies on each dataset (90.31 % (CIFAR-10), 65.79 % (CIFAR-100), 94.24 % (FashionMNIST), 49.64 % (Tiny-ImageNet)) and outperforms previous KAN-based approaches in scalability and feature extraction capabilities.

title: Distributed Ledger Technology in Healthcare: Advancing Governance Architectures and Performance Engineering in Decentralized Ecosystems
authors: Sunil Kumar R M, Raghavendra M Devadas, Mrutyunjaya M S, Praveen Gujjar J, Sowmya T, Vani Hiremani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: Healthcare data infrastructures remain fragmented, insecure, and characterized by low trust, hindering interoperable, patient-centric care. Centralized architectures struggle with multi-stakeholder governance, fine-grained accountability, and performance at scale under real-world workloads. Distributed ledger technology (DLT) promises immutability, distributed trust, auditability, and strong provenance, yet most healthcare implementations focus on narrow use cases, ad-hoc governance choices, or limited performance benchmarking. This paper designs and empirically evaluates an integrated governance architecture and performance engineering framework for healthcare DLT ecosystems. The approach combines (i) a theoretical synthesis of DLT governance and health data governance, (ii) architecture and protocol design for permissioned and hybrid platforms (e.g., Corda, Hyperledger Fabric, Ethereum-based frameworks, edge–blockchain hybrids), and (iii) testbed implementations with multi-scenario benchmarking across local, cloud, and edge-integrated deployments. Results from representative use cases—EHR interoperability, m-health/IoT streaming, and self-sovereign identity and consent—show that DLT-enabled governance can strengthen integrity, accountability, and patient control while achieving acceptable throughput–latency envelopes compared with centralized baselines, subject to explicit trade-offs between decentralization, audit granularity, and scalability. The paper distils design guidelines and reusable governance–performance patterns for large-scale, interoperable DLT health ecosystems, clarifying when to Favor platform choices, consensus mechanisms, and data-management strategies given domain-specific requirements.

title: Learning Mask-Aware Offsets: Two-branch Deformable Attention Networks for Inpainting with Masked Region Avoidance
authors: Hyeongseok Oh, Joonki Paik
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Image inpainting restores missing regions in a visually plausible way, yet existing methods struggle with irregular holes and complex structures due to fixed kernels and static attention. In this paper, we propose Mask-Aware Deformable Inpainting Network (MADIN), which incorporates mask information for position-aware control. The proposed model employs a Two-branch Offset Estimator that jointly utilizes query features and mask signals to reliably predict reference positions even within masked regions. In addition, Adaptive Offset Range Scaling adjusts offset magnitude based on masking ratio to capture broader context when needed. Experiments on CelebA-HQ and Places2 show that MADIN achieves state-of-the-art results in PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, and FID, while remaining lightweight and efficient. The code will be released at https://github.com/ohhhyeongsn/MADIN.

title: DreamMakeup: Face Makeup Customization using Latent Diffusion Models
authors: Geon Yeong Park, Inhwa Han, Serin Yang, Yeobin Hong, Seongmin Jeong, Heechan Jeon, Myeongjin Goh, Sung Won Yi, Jin Nam, Jong Chul Ye
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: The exponential growth of the global makeup market has paralleled advancements in virtual makeup simulation technology. Despite the progress led by GANs, their application still encounters significant challenges, including training instability and limited customization capabilities. Addressing these challenges, we introduce DreamMakup – a novel training-free Diffusion model based Makeup Customization method, leveraging the inherent advantages of diffusion models for superior controllability and precise real-image editing. DreamMakeup employs early-stopped DDIM inversion to preserve the facial structure and identity while enabling extensive customization through various conditioning inputs such as reference images, specific RGB colors, and textual descriptions. Our model demonstrates notable improvements over existing GAN-based and recent diffusion-based frameworks – improved customization, color-matching capabil ities, identity preservation and compatibility with textual descriptions or LLMs with affordable computational costs.

title: Feature-Disentangling RGB-NIR Fusion Network for Remote Driver Physiological Measurement
authors: Tayssir Bouraffa, Ziyuan Wang, Daniel Strüber
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) is a crucial technique for non-contact heart rate (HR) estimation using facial videos, gaining significance in driver monitoring systems where contact-based measurements are impractical. Existing rPPG methods often rely on either RGB or NIR data, each susceptible to limitations under motion artifacts and varying illumination in real-world driving scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce a novel RGB-NIR fusion model tailored for robust rPPG and HR estimation in dynamic vehicle environments. Our approach features two main contributions, an NIR-specific decoder that facilitates effective cross-modal knowledge transfer from RGB to NIR, enhancing model adaptability, and a dual autoencoder architecture for efficient feature disentanglement and reconstruction, mitigating noise from driver motion and changing lighting conditions. Comprehensive evaluations, including inter- and cross-dataset testing and ablation studies across various driving and garage conditions, demonstrate that our model achieves superior performance on the MR-NIRP car dataset, showcasing significant robustness in complex vehicular environments.

title: Integrating Satellite Imagery and IoT Field-Based Observations for In-Situ Soil Moisture Analysis
authors: Sravani Pathivada, Susmitha Pallipe, Veeramallu Satya Sahithi, Akhila Rayala, Deepika Paturu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The precision and real-time monitoring of the soil moisture is the key to optimizing agricultural output and sustainable management of water resources. Traditional methods of measuring soil moisture using the gravimetric analysis is accurate but not scalable and remote sensing methods are scalable but not accurate. In this paper, a two-layer model is presented, which combines the soil moisture measurement with the help of the IoT and the Normalized Soil Moisture Index (NSMI) that is calculated using the data obtained by the sentinel-2A satellite. This IoT part of the model employs the Arduino microcontroller capable of linking the soil moisture sensor with the SIM900A GSM module that sends the data to ThingSpeak cloud service. The other variable of the model uses Google Earth Engine application (GEE) which is a cloud computing system that gathers information at the data on the soil moisture map which has been generated using the NSMI of the sentinel-2A satellite images. The comparison between the accuracy of the soil moisture level and the soil moisture level was made using the data obtained with the help of the IoT sensor at the proximity of the Poranki area of the Vijayawada area of the Republic of India and the data provided by the NSMI of sentinel-2A satellite data. The system generated a high degree of accuracy of R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9888.

title: Predicting Workplace Depressive Symptoms based on Work Limitation Questionnaire and Wearable-Derived Indicators
authors: Hiroaki Suzuki, Takemi Matsui, Nobutoshi Kariya, Yusuke Obara, Shohei Sato
abstract: Early detection of depression in the workplace is essential to enable timely support and mitigate productivity loss. The 25-item Work Limitations Questionnaire (WLQ-25), widely used by employers, correlates with depressive symptoms but is insufficient on its own for screening. We developed practical models that integrate self-report with wearable-derived physiology. Twenty office workers completed the WLQ-25 and the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9; reference standard) and wore a wrist device for 24 h to obtain total sleep time (TST) and mean heart rate during the first 60 min after waking (HR post-waking). Multiple linear regression showed that adding TST and HR post-waking to the 8-item WLQ short form (WLQ-8) substantially improved PHQ-9 estimation (adjusted R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.68) compared with WLQ-8 alone (0.38). A model that used WLQ-25 instead of WLQ-8 achieved adjusted R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.75 (ΔR<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.07 versus the WLQ-8 model; nested F-test p = 0.0498). Higher WLQ scores, longer TST, and lower HR post-waking were associated with higher PHQ-9 scores. These patterns may reflect reduced morning activity and blunted autonomic reactivity. The proposed approach—combining a brief 8-item questionnaire with simple physiological indicators—offers a feasible primary screening tool for occupational health programs.

title: Config-Driven Promptable Semantic Segmentation for Satellite Land Cover Mapping with PyTorch
authors: Dharshan S, Dhanush S, Dhanush Shankar, Dinesh Kumar G, B Uma Devi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Semantic segmentation is a core component of remote sensing, especially in land cover mapping, that can be used to monitor the environment and manage resources. The paper presents a prompt enabled and configuration based segmentation system based on convolutional neural network models, which are configured to classify pixels of satellite images into buildings, vegetation, roads, and bodies of water, among others. This integrated approach ensures consistent performance across varying spatial resolutions and diverse remote sensing conditions. An open AutoML like configuration file format allows the user to directly choose model architectures, optimizers, and learning parameters in the configuration file, thus increasing flexibility and enabling experimental tailoring. The immediate segmentation option also enhances flexibility since visualization of solely chosen land cover classes is available. The modular framework facilitates supervised, semi supervised, and prompt mediated pipelines, making it apt for unique class and multi class scenarios. The experimental outcomes on the dataset of the LandCover.ai show high performance with an accuracy score of ninety two to ninety seven percent. The setup time was reduced by about fifty percent, and timely customization enhanced specific class measures by as much as five percent. Overall, the system provides an effective, configurable, and repeatable land cover mapping system.
