title: Analysing Sentiments by Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning Approaches on Dogri Language
authors: Ritika Bandhral, Sandeep Dubey, Shubhnandan Singh Jamwal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: In the digital era, there is very less work on sentiment analysis specially on low resourced Indian languages. Dogri is one such recognized language which still remains less explored by the researchers. It is a morphologically rich language and is spoken by people across J&K and Himachal Pradesh and in some parts of Punjab. Deep learning and neural networks are generally used for NLP applications like detecting emotions, hate speech, text summarization and prediction etc. In this paper, we have used some common machine learning approaches like SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest and Naïve Bayes for analysing sentiments. Deep learning approaches which are used in the current research are CNN, RNN and LSTM for analysing the sentiments in Dogri text. After the experimental observation on 1500 sentences, it is observed that the machine learning approaches and deep learning approaches are giving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 2 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 2 \%}$</tex> accuracy respectively.

title: GPRS-Net: Global Statistical Prior Guided Relation-Aware Symmetric Point Cloud Completion
authors: Zelin Li, Shuanfeng Zhao, Zhengheng Li
abstract: Point cloud completion aims to recover geometrically complete and topologically consistent 3D shapes from partial observations. However, existing methods still struggle with thin structures, sharp features, and strongly anisotropic regions, where local discontinuities, point scattering, and blurred boundaries are likely to occur, especially under weak symmetry and complex topology. This issue largely stems from the lack of reliable global shape constraints during symmetry guided coarse skeleton generation, while isotropic neighborhood aggregation tends to introduce cross part interference and feature confusion in anisotropic areas. To address this problem, we propose a Global Statistical Prior guided Relation Aware Symmetric Network (GPRS Net) for point cloud completion. The proposed method first introduces a directional projection statistics module to extract global statistical priors from the depth distributions of multidirectional projections of the input point cloud. These priors are fused with keypoint features to guide per keypoint transformation and translation regression, producing a structurally consistent symmetric coarse skeleton. A relation aware edge aggregation encoder is then designed to jointly model scale differences and directional deviations on KNN graphs, enabling adaptive edge feature recalibration and aggregation for stronger local geometric representation. Finally, a two stage progressive refinement backbone is adopted to progressively recover fine details while preserving overall structural consistency. Experiments on PCN and MVP demonstrate consistent improvements, as well as better structural stability and detail continuity in challenging cases.

title: Companion of the 45th Symposium on Principles of Database Systems
authors: N/A
abstract: None

title: Fine-grained Complexity of Database Queries
authors: Karl Bringmann
publish: Companion of the 45th Symposium on Principles of Database Systems
abstract: None

title: DCAChain: A Decentralized Cross-Domain Access Control Architecture with Key Abuse Resistance for the Metaverse
authors: Chien-Ming Chen, Bohao Xiang, Saru Kumari
abstract: The rapid convergence of the Internet of Things and the metaverse has intensified cross-domain data exchange and digital asset sharing, raising new challenges in access control, key abuse prevention, and interoperability across heterogeneous domains. Existing blockchain-based solutions still rely on centralized authorities or isolated single-chain architectures, which limit scalability and flexible cross-domain coordination. To address these issues, this paper proposes DCAChain, a decentralized cross-domain access-control architecture for the metaverse. DCAChain integrates a relay chain–parachain model with hashed timelock contracts to enable verifiable cross-domain coordination, and builds upon accountable CP-ABE/NIZK-based authorization to resist key abuse without trusted intermediaries. The framework further supports dynamic attribute lifecycle management, including expiration, revocation, and key update, and introduces smart-contract-based policy reuse and attribute-set deduplication to reduce repeated encryption redundancy in asynchronous multi-user scenarios. Experimental results on Ethereum and Hyperledger Sawtooth show that DCAChain achieves lower operational cost, reduced latency, and improved scalability compared with representative frameworks. These results indicate that DCAChain is a practical solution for privacy-preserving cross-domain access control in metaverse environments.

title: Folding the Vision: Towards Efficient Global Context Representation on Edge Devices
authors: Ang Li, Shouxiang Ni
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Traditional efficient CNNs face limitations in capturing global dependencies due to local receptive fields, while ViTs suffer from quadratic complexity. Recent works attempt to combine convolution and attention mechanisms, yet still struggle with computational overhead that limits inference speed on mobile devices. In this work, we propose FoldNet, a pure convolutional architecture that achieves global context representation through hardware-efficient operations. First, we propose the Fold Convolution that reorganizes spatial pixels into the channel dimension, capturing global spatial attention via channel interactions, with lower computational costs compared to ViTs. Second, we design the Partial Re-parameterization Module that applies the structural re-parameterization technique to a subset of feature channels. This approach achieves nearly identical performance compared to full-channel processing while significantly minimizing redundant computations and memory accesses. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of FoldNet over existing state-of-the-art methods. For example, on ImageNet-1K, our Foldnet-S1 achieves 79.0% Top-1 accuracy, while only has 1.0 ms inference speed on mobile devices. Comprehensive experimental results on high-resolution downstream tasks show that FoldNet outperforms existing efficient models, maintaining a superior trade-off between inference speed and accuracy.

title: A Routh Zero-Location Testing Method for Integer Polynomials With Any Singularity Profile
authors: Yuval Bistritz, Ruth Weinberg
abstract: The paper presents an integer-preserving algorithm to determine the zero location (ZL) with respect to the imaginary axis for any polynomial with Gaussian-integer coefficients. It extends the so-called optimal integer Routh stability test, previously limited to normal conditions, to integer polynomials with any singularity profile. The primary advantage of integer arithmetic lies in its inherent error-free quality. This characteristic is particularly crucial for the general ZL case because it involves workarounds for abnormal and singular cases that are triggered by correct identification of the value 0 - an occurrence that can be obscured by rounding errors in floating-point arithmetic. Running the algorithm with integers, prevents the risk of false acceptance or rejection of these workarounds. Since in practice any non-integer polynomial can be scaled into an integer polynomial, the new algorithm paves the way for an error-free solution to the classical Routh-Hurwitz problem in its most general possible setting.

title: UltraClean: A Simple Framework to Train Robust Neural Networks against Backdoor Attacks
authors: Bingyin Zhao, Yingjie Lao
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Backdoor attacks are emerging threats to deep neural networks, which typically embed malicious behaviors into a victim model by injecting poisoned samples. Adversaries can activate the injected backdoor during inference by presenting the trigger on input images. Prior defensive methods have achieved remarkable success in countering dirty-label backdoor attacks where the labels of poisoned samples are often mislabeled. However, these approaches do not work for a recent new type of backdoor – clean-label backdoor attacks that imperceptibly modify poisoned data and hold consistent labels. More complex and powerful algorithms are demanded to defend against such stealthy attacks. In this paper, we propose UltraClean, a general framework that simplifies the identification of poisoned samples and defends against both dirty-label and clean-label backdoor attacks. Given the fact that backdoor triggers introduce adversarial noise that intensifies in feed-forward propagation, UltraClean first generates two variants of training samples using off-the-shelf denoising functions. It then measures the susceptibility of training samples leveraging the error amplification effect in DNNs, which dilates the noise difference between the original image and denoised variants. Lastly, it filters out poisoned samples based on the susceptibility to thwart the backdoor implantation. Despite its simplicity, UltraClean achieves a superior detection rate across various datasets and significantly reduces the backdoor attack success rate while maintaining a decent model accuracy on clean data, outperforming existing defensive methods by a large margin. Code is available at https://github.com/bxz9200/UltraClean.

title: Beyond Real Weights: Hypercomplex Representations for Stable Quantization
authors: Jawad Ibn Ahad, Maisha Rahman, Amrijit Biswas, Muhammad Rafsan Kabir, Robin Krambroeckers, Sifat Momen, Nabeel Mohammed, Shafin Rahman
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Multimodal language models (MLLMs) demand immense parameter capacity to align high-dimensional visual features with linguistic representations, making them computationally heavy and difficult to deploy efficiently. We introduce a progressive reparameterization strategy that physically compresses these models by progressively replacing dense feed-forward network blocks with compact Parameterized Hypercomplex Multiplication (PHM) layers. A residual interpolation schedule, together with lightweight reconstruction and knowledge distillation losses, ensures that the PHM modules inherit the functional behavior of their dense counterparts throughout training. This transition yields substantial parameter and FLOP reductions while preserving strong multimodal alignment, leading to faster inference without degrading output quality. We evaluate the approach on vision–language models (VLMs). Our approach preserves strong performance comparable with base VLMs, while delivering substantial reductions in model size and inference latency. Progressive PHM substitution thus offers an architecture-compatible path toward more efficient multimodal reasoning and complements existing low-bit quantization techniques. Codes are available at https://github.com/milab-nsu/PHM.

title: FlowMorph: Revealing an Optimizable Flow Latent Space for Controlled Image Morphing
authors: Yan Zheng, Yi Yang, Lanqing Guo, Zhangyang Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: We present FlowMorph, a simple and training-free framework for geometry-preserving and semantics-aware image interpolation. The key idea is to separate two factors inside the flow model’s latent space: an offset that captures shape and geometry, and a one-step vector that carries semantic meaning. By keeping the flow model frozen and only optimizing these two variables, FlowMorph exposes a stable and interpretable neighborhood around each image. This leads to two complementary modes. Flow-Optimizer directly fits a source image toward a target image and naturally supports multi-objective combinations, producing stable reconstructions. Flow-Interpolation mixes the offset linearly and the semantic vector spherically, generating smooth and coherent transitions between images. Across a wide range of tasks including object morphing, pose changes, and scene transitions, FlowMorph outperforms prior interpolation-based methods. Quantitative experiments show that our method achieves lower perceptual error, better image fidelity, and smoother transitions. Landmark-based analysis further confirms that FlowMorph preserves geometry more effectively. We also ablate the effect of the backward step size, showing that longer steps increase semantic expressiveness and allow interpolations that move beyond trivial shape blending, enabling richer morphs across object positions and photo layouts. FlowMorph provides an interpretable and controllable tool for high-quality image morphing without the need for additional training.

title: BlendCLIP: Bridging Synthetic and Real Domains for Zero-Shot 3D Object Classification with Multimodal Pretraining
authors: Ajinkya Khoche, Gergő László Nagy, Maciej Wozniak, Thomas Gustafsson, Patric Jensfelt
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Zero-shot 3D object classification is crucial for real-world applications like autonomous driving, however it is often hindered by a significant domain gap between the synthetic data used for training and the sparse, noisy LiDAR scans encountered in the real-world. Current methods trained solely on synthetic data fail to generalize to outdoor scenes, while those trained only on real data lack the semantic diversity to recognize rare or unseen objects. We introduce BlendCLIP, a multimodal pretraining framework that bridges this synthetic-to-real gap by strategically combining the strengths of both domains. We first propose a pipeline to generate a large-scale dataset of object-level triplets—consisting of a point cloud, image, and text description—mined directly from real-world driving data and human annotated 3D boxes. Our core contribution is a curriculum-based data mixing strategy that first grounds the model in the semantically rich synthetic CAD data before progressively adapting it to the specific characteristics of real-world scans. Our experiments show that our approach is highly label-efficient: introducing as few as 1.5% real-world samples per batch into training boosts zero-shot accuracy on the nuScenes benchmark by 27%. Consequently, our final model achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging outdoor datasets like nuScenes and TruckScenes, improving over the best prior method by 19.3% on nuScenes, while maintaining strong generalization on diverse synthetic benchmarks. Our findings demonstrate that effective domain adaptation, not full-scale real-world annotation, is the key to unlocking robust open-vocabulary 3D perception. Our code and dataset will be released upon acceptance on https://github.com/kesu1/BlendCLIP.

title: An improved architecture for part-based animal re-identification through semantic segmentation distillation
authors: Eugênio Dias Ribeiro Neto, Marc Chaumont, Gérard Subsol, Michel de Garine-Wichatitsky, Hélène Guis
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Wildlife re-identification (Re-ID) is critical for non-invasive monitoring. Yet, animal Re-ID performances remain far behind person Re-ID due to limited datasets and a greater fine-grained appearance variability between individuals. One strategy is to adopt part-based methods in order to more precisely attend to distinct anatomical regions. To adapt to animal Re-ID, we propose PAW-ViT (Part-AWare animal re-identification Vision Transformer), a ViT that replaces the standard classification token with K learnable part tokens, each specialized to a specific anatomical region of the animal. Spatial specialization is achieved via feature-based knowledge distillation by training each token’s attention to image patches to produce a semantic segmentation mask. An additional aggregation token fuses the part embeddings into a single part-aware descriptor. Trained with a multi-task loss, PAW-ViT outperforms state-of-the-art methods in animal Re-ID on ATRW (Amur tigers) and YakREID-103 (yaks), particularly in scenarios of strong viewpoint variations like the cross-camera setting.

title: Exploring Automated Recognition of Instructional Activity and Discourse from Multimodal Classroom Data
authors: Ivo Bueno, Ruikun Hou, Babette Bühler, Tim Fütterer, James Drimalla, Jonathan K. Foster, Peter Youngs, Peter Gerjets, Ulrich Trautwein, Enkelejda Kasneci
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Observation of classroom interactions can provide concrete feedback to teachers, but current methods rely on manual annotation, which is resource-intensive and hard to scale. This work explores AI-driven analysis of classroom recordings, focusing on multimodal instructional activity and discourse recognition as a foundation for actionable feedback. Using a densely annotated dataset of 164 hours of video and 68 lesson transcripts, we design parallel, modality-specific pipelines. For video, we evaluate zero-shot multimodal LLMs, fine-tuned vision–language models, and self-supervised video transformers on 24 activity labels. For transcripts, we fine-tune a transformer-based classifier with contextualized inputs and compare it against prompting-based LLMs on 19 discourse labels. To handle class imbalance and multi-label complexity, we apply per-label thresholding, context windows, and imbalance-aware loss functions. The results show that fine-tuned models consistently outperform prompting-based approaches, achieving macro-F1 scores of 0.577 for video and 0.460 for transcripts. These results demonstrate the feasibility of automated classroom analysis and establish a foundation for scalable teacher feedback systems.

title: Empowering Source-Free Domain Adaptation via MLLM-Guided Reliability-Based Curriculum Learning
authors: Dongjie Chen, Kartik Patwari, Zhengfeng Lai, Xiaoguang Zhu, Sen-Ching Cheung, Chen-Nee Chuah
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Existing SFDA methods struggle to fully use pre-trained knowledge and often rely on a single model’s predictions or handcrafted prompts, limiting robustness under domain shift. Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) offer a promising alternative: they encode rich visual-semantic knowledge and generalize well without task-specific tuning. However, their use in SFDA is hindered by instruction-following failures, inconsistent outputs, and high inference costs. We propose Reliability-based Curriculum Learning (RCL), a novel framework that distills robust supervision from multiple frozen MLLMs into a compact target model. RCL organizes adaptation as a three-stage curriculum that progressively incorporates pseudo-labels based on inter-model agreement and model confidence, enabling stable and noise-aware training. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard SFDA datasets, Office-Home, DomainNet-126, and VisDA-C, outperforming zero-shot MLLMs, their ensembles, all without accessing source data or tuning foundation models. Code is available at https://github.com/Dong-Jie-Chen/RCL.

title: Anatomically-guided masked autoencoder pre-training for aneurysm detection
authors: Alberto M. Ceballos Arroyo, Jisoo Kim, Chu-Hsuan Lin, Lei Qin, Geoffrey S. Young, Huaizu Jiang
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Intracranial aneurysms are a major cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, and detecting them manually is a complex, time-consuming task. Albeit automated solutions are desirable, the limited availability of training data makes it difficult to develop such solutions using typical supervised learning frameworks. In this work, we propose a novel pre-training strategy using more widely available unannotated head CT scan data to pre-train a 3D Vision Transformer model prior to fine-tuning for the aneurysm detection task. Specifically, we modify masked auto-encoder (MAE) pre-training in the following ways: we use a factorized self-attention mechanism to make 3D attention computationally viable, we restrict the masked patches to areas near arteries to focus on areas where aneurysms are likely to occur, and we reconstruct not only CT scan intensity values but also artery distance maps, which describe the distance between each voxel and the closest artery, thereby enhancing the backbone’s learned representations. Compared with SOTA aneurysm detection models, our approach gains +2-7% absolute Sensitivity at a false positive rate of 0.5. Code and weights are available at: https://github.com/alceballosa/aneurysm-mae

title: PADM: A Physics-aware Diffusion Model for Attenuation Correction
authors: Trung Kien Pham, Hoang Minh Vu, Anh Duc Chu, Dac Thai Nguyen, Trung Thanh Nguyen, Thao Nguyen Truong, Mai Hong Son, Thanh Trung Nguyen, Phi Le Nguyen
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Attenuation artifacts remain a significant challenge in cardiac Myocardial Perfusion Imaging (MPI) using Single-Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT), often compromising diagnostic accuracy and reducing clinical interpretability. While hybrid SPECT/CT systems mitigate these artifacts through CT-derived attenuation maps, their high cost, limited accessibility, and added radiation exposure hinder widespread clinical adoption. In this study, we propose a novel CT-free solution to attenuation correction in cardiac SPECT. Specifically, we introduce Physics-aware Attenuation Correction Diffusion Model (PADM), a diffusion-based generative method that incorporates explicit physics priors via a teacher–student distillation mechanism. This approach enables attenuation artifact correction using only Non-Attenuation-Corrected (NAC) input, while still benefiting from physics-informed supervision during training. To support this work, we also introduce CardiAC, a comprehensive dataset comprising 424 patient studies with paired NAC and Attenuation-Corrected (AC) reconstructions, alongside high-resolution CT-based attenuation maps. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PADM outperforms state-of-the-art generative models, delivering superior reconstruction fidelity across both quantitative metrics and visual assessment.

title: Fake News Detection Using LLM and RAG
authors: Praveen Gujjar J, Raghavendra M Devadas, Nikitha K, Lubna Ambreen, Sowmya T, Sunil Kumar R M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: The increasing complexity and volume of user-generated content demand intelligent systems that not only analyze sentiment effectively but also demonstrate autonomous decision-making capabilities. This research proposes an Agentic AI Design Framework for sentiment analysis, integrating the principles of agency, autonomy, and adaptability within artificial intelligence systems. Unlike traditional sentiment analysis models that rely heavily on static rules or supervised learning, the agentic framework empowers AI models to dynamically interpret context, self-optimize learning pathways, and make independent decisions based on evolving data patterns. The framework combines multi-agent systems, reinforcement learning, and context-aware natural language processing (NLP) techniques to enhance both the accuracy and the responsiveness of sentiment classification. Experimental results across diverse datasets show significant improvements in adaptability to new domains and robustness against noise compared to conventional approaches. This study positions agentic AI as a transformative step in designing next-generation sentiment analysis systems capable of autonomous growth and deeper contextual understanding, opening avenues for broader applications in social media monitoring, customer feedback analysis, and beyond.

title: MUSE: Model-based Uncertainty-aware Similarity Estimation for zero-shot 2D Object Detection and Segmentation
authors: Sungmin Cho, Sungbum Park, Insoo Oh
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: In this work, we present MUSE (Model-based Uncertainty-aware Similarity Estimation), a training-free framework for model-based zero-shot 2D object detection and segmentation. First, MUSE incorporates 2D multi-view templates from 3D unseen objects and 2D object proposals from the input query image, respectively. In the embedding stage, we propose a new feature embedding scheme which integrates class and patch embeddings. Specifically, the patch embeddings are normalized using the generalized mean pooling (GeM). In the matching stage, a joint similarity score is introduced, which integrates an absolute score and a relative score. Finally, we update the similarity score using an uncertainty-aware object prior. MUSE achieves state-of-the-art performance on the BOP Challenge 2025, ranking first in the Classic Core, H3, and Industrial tracks—without any additional training or fine-tuning. Therefore, we believe that MUSE is a promising framework for zero-shot 2D object detection and segmentation.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Post-Quantum Cryptography Integration into Public Key Infrastructure: Challenges, Algorithms, and Future Directions
authors: Godi Amulya, Siva Rama Krishna T
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Development of Quantum Computers are growing rapidly, the security of today’s cryptographic systems which are working with Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) is at serious risk. RSA and ECC algorithms are the backbone of secure communication and digital identity can be breakable by quantum algorithms such as Shor’s. Hence, a transition is required to Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC), which is designed to handle quantum attacks. This work provides a detailed review of how PQC can be integrated with the existing PKI, with a special focus on the auto-enrollment process. Auto-enrollment is an essential process but often overlooked mechanism, responsible for automatically issuing and renewing digital certificates. First, we explored how current PKI systems are exposed to the quantum threats and the readiness. The performance of the algorithms that are standardized by NIST, such as Kyber and Dilithium were discussed. We analyze the technical and operational challenges of transitioning to PQC by referencing both research studies and real-world implementations from major industry players such as Google and Microsoft. These include large key size, certificate size, compatibility issues, complex computations and need for crypto-agility. We also examine the hybrid approaches that combine classical and post-quantum methods as traditional strategies. The survey concludes by outlining best practices, deployment considerations, and open research challenges that must be addressed for a smooth, secure, and scalable shift to quantum-safe PKI. This work aims to guide researchers, engineers, and policymakers in building the next generation of cryptographic infrastructure that can stand the test-time even in a quantum world.

title: OpenLVLM-MIA: A Controlled Benchmark Revealing the Limits of Membership Inference Attacks on Large Vision-Language Models
authors: Ryoto Miyamoto, Xin Fan, Fuyuko Kido, Tsuneo Matsumoto, Hayato Yamana
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: OpenLVLM-MIA is a new benchmark that highlights fundamental challenges in evaluating membership inference attacks (MIA) against large vision-language models (LVLMs). While prior work has reported high attack success rates, our analysis suggests that these results often arise from detecting distributional bias introduced during dataset construction rather than from identifying true membership status. To address this issue, we introduce a controlled benchmark of 6,000 images where the distributions of member and non-member samples are carefully balanced, and ground-truth membership labels are provided across three distinct training stages. Experiments using OpenLVLM-MIA demonstrated that the performance of state-of-the-art MIA methods approached chance-level. OpenLVLM-MIA, designed to be transparent and unbiased benchmark, clarifies certain limitations of MIA research on LVLMs and provides a solid foundation for developing stronger privacy-preserving techniques.

title: Robust Scene Coordinate Regression via Geometrically-Consistent Global Descriptors
authors: Son Tung Nguyen, Alejandro Fontan, Michael Milford, Tobias Fischer
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Recent learning-based visual localization methods use global descriptors to disambiguate visually similar places, but existing approaches often derive these descriptors from geometric cues alone (e.g., covisibility graphs), limiting their discriminative power and reducing robustness in the presence of noisy geometric constraints. We propose an aggregator module that learns global descriptors consistent with both geometrical structure and visual similarity, ensuring that images are close in descriptor space only when they are visually similar and spatially connected. This corrects erroneous associations caused by unreliable overlap scores. Using a batch-mining strategy based solely on the overlap scores and a modified contrastive loss, our method trains without manual place labels and generalizes across diverse environments. Experiments on challenging benchmarks show substantial localization gains in large-scale environments while preserving computational and memory efficiency. Code is available at github.com/sontung/robust_scr.

title: PSA-MIL: A Probabilistic Spatial Attention-Based Multiple Instance Learning for Whole Slide Image Classification
authors: Sharon Peled, Yosef E. Maruvka, Moti Freiman
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Whole Slide Images (WSIs) are high-resolution digital scans widely used in medical diagnostics. Due to their immense size, WSI classification is typically approached using Multiple Instance Learning (MIL), where a slide is partitioned into individual tiles, disrupting its spatial structure. Recent MIL methods often incorporate spatial context through rigid spatial assumptions (e.g. fixed kernels), which limit their ability to capture the intricate tissue structures crucial for an accurate diagnosis. To address this limitation, we propose Probabilistic Spatial Attention MIL (PSA-MIL), a novel attention-based MIL framework that integrates spatial context into the attention mechanism through learnable distance-decayed priors, formulated within a probabilistic interpretation of self-attention as a posterior distribution. This formulation enables a dynamic inference of spatial relationships during training, eliminating the need for predefined assumptions often imposed by previous approaches. Furthermore, we introduce a diversity loss that promotes complementary spatial representations across attention heads and a spatial posterior-pruning strategy that reduces computational cost for long WSI sequences while preserving performance. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and tasks show that PSA-MIL outperforms current baselines and achieves state-of-the-art results with substantially lower computational overhead. Our code is available at https://github.com/SharonPeled/PSA-MIL.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Vision-Language Segmentation for Medical Imaging
authors: Aryan Das, Tanishq Rachamalla, Koushik Biswas, Swalpa Kumar Roy, Vinay Kumar Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: We introduce a novel uncertainty-aware multimodal segmentation framework that leverages both radiological images and associated clinical text for precise medical diagnosis. We propose a Modality Decoding Attention Block (MoDAB) with a lightweight State Space Mixer (SSMix) to enable efficient cross-modal fusion and long-range dependency modelling. To guide learning under ambiguity, we propose the Spectral-Entropic Uncertainty (SEU) Loss, which jointly captures spatial overlap, spectral consistency, and predictive uncertainty in a unified objective. In complex clinical circumstances with poor image quality, this formulation improves model reliability. Extensive experiments on various publicly available medical datasets, QATA-COVID19, MosMed++, and Kvasir-SEG, demonstrate that our method achieves superior segmentation performance while being significantly more computationally efficient than existing State-of-the-Art (SoTA) approaches. Our results highlight the importance of incorporating uncertainty modelling and structured modality alignment in vision-language medical segmentation tasks. Code: https://github.com/arya-domain/UA-VLS

title: Flex-Knee: A Low-Cost Passive Haptic Model for Injury Assessment Training
authors: Marco Moran-Ledesma, Robert Burns, Oliver Schneider, Mark Hancock
abstract: We present the design and development of a lowcost ($ 400 CAD), scalable, and modular haptic knee model to support training in manual musculoskeletal assessments of injuries. Our model aims to help inexperienced practitioners learn the Valgus test, a clinical test to assess medial collateral ligament (MCL) injuries. The valgus test is typically taught on classmates or patients, which limits available training time and exposure to anatomical diversity and injury conditions. Designed with a certified clinician, our model uses a generic mechanical joint, silicone approximating human skin look and feel, a metamaterials-based approach to reproduce MCL injuries, and pressure sensors to capture user-applied forces. We evaluated the model in three stages: internal calibration with our collaborating clinician to configure it for four conditions (healthy, grades 1-3) using the staircase procedure; acceptance testing with 6 additional clinicians; and a showcase with students in a kinesiology class. Certified professionals distinguished healthy, partially-torn, and fully-torn MCLs with sensitivities of 0.83, 0.75, and 1.00; students also recognized these differences, though refinements are needed for grades 1 and 2. Participants praised the model's skin texture and shape. This work highlights the potential of low-cost haptic simulators to deliver standardized and repeatable training experiences.

title: Mixed Diffusion for 3D Indoor Scene Synthesis
authors: Siyi Hu, Diego Martin Arroyo, Stephanie Debats, Fabian Manhardt, Luca Carlone, Federico Tombari
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Generating realistic 3D scenes is an area of growing interest in computer vision and robotics. However, creating high-quality, diverse synthetic 3D content often requires expert intervention, making it costly and complex. Recently, efforts to automate this process with learning techniques, particularly diffusion models, have shown significant improvements in tasks like furniture rearrangement. However, applying diffusion models to floor-conditioned indoor scene synthesis remains under-explored. This task is especially challenging as it requires arranging objects in continuous space while selecting from discrete object categories, posing unique difficulties for conventional diffusion methods. To bridge this gap, we present MiDiffusion, a novel mixed discrete-continuous diffusion model designed to synthesize plausible 3D indoor scenes given a floor plan and pre-arranged objects. We represent a scene layout by a 2D floor plan and a set of objects, each defined by category, location, size, and orientation. Our approach uniquely applies structured corruption across mixed discrete semantic and continuous geometric domains, resulting in a better-conditioned problem for denoising. Evaluated on the 3D-FRONT dataset, MiDiffusion outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive and diffusion models in floor-conditioned 3D scene synthesis. Additionally, it effectively handles partial object constraints via a corruption-and-masking strategy without task-specific training, demonstrating advantages in scene completion and furniture arrangement tasks.

title: TRACE: Confounder-free Adversarial Fine-tuning for Robust Object Detection
authors: Wonho Lee, Jisu Lee, Hyunsik Na, Sohee Park, Daeseon Choi
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Adversarial patch attacks critically endanger object detection systems by causing severe mispredictions with small, easily realizable perturbations in both digital and physical environments. Existing defenses such as certified methods or patch detection suffer from high latency, while conventional adversarial training often overfits to specific patches and lacks generalization, particularly in multi-object scenarios. To overcome high latency and poor generalization, we introduce TRACE (Tuning Robustness by Adversarial-patch Confounder Elimination), an adversarial fine-tuning framework that leverages Instrumental Variable Regression in the feature space. TRACE treats patch-related variations—including location, rotation, and brightness—as confounders, thereby eliminating spurious correlations and guiding the model toward causal features that sustain robust detection. Evaluations on YOLOv5 and YOLOv8 show that TRACE consistently outperforms conventional defense methods in both efficiency and robustness under adaptive and unseen patch attacks. Moreover, physical testbed experiments confirm its effectiveness beyond digital settings, highlighting TRACE as a practical solution for achieving generalized robustness in object detection.

title: CONSTANT: Towards High-Quality One-Shot Handwriting Generation with Patch Contrastive Enhancement and Style-Aware Quantization
authors: Anh-Duy Le, Van-Linh Pham, Thanh-Nam Vo, Xuan Toan Mai, Tuan-Anh Tran
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: One-shot styled handwriting image generation, despite achieving impressive results in recent years, remains challenging due to the difficulty in capturing the intricate and diverse characteristics of human handwriting by using solely a single reference image. Existing methods still struggle to generate visually appealing and realistic handwritten images and adapt to complex, unseen writer styles, struggling to isolate invariant style features (e.g., slant, stroke width, curvature) while ignoring irrelevant noise. To tackle this problem, we introduce Patch Contrastive Enhancement and Style-Aware Quantization via Denoising Diffusion (CONSTANT), a novel one-shot handwriting generation via diffusion model. CONSTANT leverages three key innovations: 1) a Style-Aware Quantization (SAQ) module that models style as discrete visual tokens capturing distinct concepts; 2) a contrastive objective to ensure these tokens are well-separated and meaningful in the embedding style space; 3) a latent patch-based contrastive (L<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">LatentPCE</inf>) objective help improving quality and local structures by aligning multiscale spatial patches of generated and real features in latent space. Extensive experiments and analysis on benchmark datasets from multiple languages, including English, Chinese, and our proposed ViHTGen dataset for Vietnamese, demonstrate the superiority of adapting to new reference styles and producing highly detailed images of our method over state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at GitHub

title: Game Optimization Scheduling Method for Regional Power Grid of 220kV Substation Based on Information Entropy
authors: Meng Ren, Junbao Zhang, Cunliang Wei, Defa Cao
publish: 2025 International Conference on Smart Energy and Control Engineering (SECE)
abstract: Multi-stakeholder game scheduling of power grid has high complexity, and the objectives of each stakeholder are very different. These different objectives make it difficult to find a solution that satisfies all parties completely in game scheduling. Therefore, this study takes 220 kV substation as the research object, and designs a game optimization dispatching method for power supply region. The power supply information mining is completed based on information entropy, and the power supply area data scheduling method is designed under the multi-mixed game, and the power supply area scheduling function is solved based on the Bat algorithm. The experimental results show that: Under the application of the proposed substation power supply regional network scheduling method, the power utilization rate is always above 95 %, and the power fluctuation is smaller, and the power generation cost in the power supply region can be significantly reduced, indicating that the research method can effectively optimize the interest game between the power grid multi-target entities.

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning with Decentralized AI in Autonomous Drone Swarms
authors: Vibhor Pal, Gaurav Sarraf, Manisha Bhende, Suvarna Patil
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The collaborative aspect of drone swarms endangers smooth functioning of services and security of national facilities. Multi-Agent Deep Learning (DL) Coordinating swarms of drones in dynamic systems are not simple tasks, but learning is proving to be a legitimate solution. The paper introduces a new end-to-end UAV swarm intelligence system that combines DL and multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to achieve autonomous and coordinated actions of drones. The system uses a new UAV swarm intelligence system that is based on YOLOv8 detection, DeepSORT-like tracking, and multi-agent PPO reinforcement. YOLOv8n model has the following performance: 0.91 precision, 0.83 F1-score, and 6.6 ms processing time per frame. The tracker is running at 151.5 FPS, ensuring the identities of the UAVs are the same throughout the movie. A specialized DroneSwarmEnv trains drones in formation control as well as in collision avoidance and cooperative navigation thus achieving an average reward of 1,886 with minimal collisions. In order to encourage generalization, real and synthetic UAVSwarm datasets are employed, consequently, training diversity and adaptability are multiplied. An in-depth analysis and visualization have indicated that the detection, tracking, and swarm behavior performance is outstanding and proves the policy convergence and policy stability. The system is of low weight, scalable, and is applicable for real-time deployment, thus providing a huge potential for uses such as autonomous surveillance, disaster monitoring, and aerial mission planning.

title: SentrySLA: SLA Breach Predictor for Incident Management Using Machine Learning
authors: Divya Gudibandla, Srithu Gaddolla, Jyothi Borra, Kadari Nandhini
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: In the realm of IT service management, adherence to service level agreements (SLAs) is essential for resolving incidents. However, the challenge is that you can never truly know when your call SLA may be breached. This project will develop a smart SLA breach prediction tool that predicts the likelihood of SLA breaches for ticket incidents using machine learning methods. Each time a ticket is submitted, the predictive tool will characterize the ticket incident using historical incident data while also including information about the ticket incident itself and resolution experience to predict whether a ticket incident is likely to breach SLA timelines. This project will include the processes of data cleaning, feature creation and classification processes. NLP techniques in motivation assessment of ticket descriptions help in improving your prediction accuracy. The solution involves easy-to- view dashboards and visualization that show SLA risk trends along with the likelihood of breaches, thereby helping IT teams take action in time. This predictive capability enhances functionality for service teams through notifications on which particular ticket might breach its SLA. Additionally, it would be able to provide helpful motivations, such as how risky that ticket may be, so the team could act swiftly-for example, routing the ticket to an expert or escalating it. It will shift teams from being reactive to a problem to being able to predict and solve problems before they become close to being big issues. Smoothening of operations and improvement in end-user experience will thus be the result.

title: Ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks with Transfer Learning for Remote Sensing Image Scene Classification
authors: Richa Kesharwani, Dhirendra Pandey, Pawan Kumar Chaurasia
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Remote sensing Scene classification is a challenging task due to the complex nature of high-resolution data scene data sets. Due to advancement in sensor technology, it has become very easy to collect huge amount of scene data related to the earth surfaces. One major problem in the area of remote sensing is the availability of labeled data. Deep learning techniques are state of art for the scene classification but these methods require huge amount of labeled data. In this work, an ensemble of deep learning method has been proposed which utilize transfer learning to reduce the requirement of labeled data from scene domain. The proposed method achieved 86.40% accuracy which is 2% greater than traditional method. The experimental results show that proposed framework have capability to improve classification accuracy in comparison to standalone deep learning models.

title: Quantum-Inspired Machine Learning for High-Dimensional Data Optimization
authors: Arpana Chaturvedi, Siddharth Shankar Mishra, S. Nagaraju, Amit Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: High dimensional data in machine learning (ML) however is not yet fully tapped due curse of dimensionality, sensitivity to local minima and computational burden. In this paper, we will introduce and test a new QIGS algorithm for such problems on classical computer. The efficiency of QIGS is demonstrated to be benefiting from the amplitude–phase hybrid probabilistic encoding and gradient-assisted update for optimizing entangling operations, which can effectively balance global exploration and local exploitation. We test this experiment on synthetic benchmark functions (Rastrigin, Rosenbrock, Ackley up to 5,000 dimensions) and real-world data sets (genomic profiles with 20,531 features and BERT-based sentiment embeddings with 768 features). It is also shown that QIGS achieves a classification accuracy of 94.2% on the genomic data (Adam: 91.5%, SGD: 90.7%, PSO: 89.8%) and reduces the number of iterations for convergence by 35–40% compared to fastest baseline on synthetic benchmarks. In addition, on the BERT representations QIGS got a training loss of 0.083 in 120 iterations (vs Adam's loss of 0.091 in 180 iterations). These results establish that QIGS is not only a faster converging algorithm, but also one which produces solutions of higher quality for many problems, thereby making it an accessible segue between classical optimization and the future of quantum-inspired machine learning.

title: Embedding-Enhanced Deep Neural Network for Hypertension Risk Prediction Using Mixed-Type Clinical Data
authors: Jeena Joseph
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Hypertension is primarily responsible for cardiovascular diseases and death all over the world. The early detection of such individuals is a must for the medical treatment and preventive care. A new hybrid deep learning model for predicting hypertension using structured clinical and behavioral data has been developed and elucidated in this article. Data from Kaggle has been utilized in this research, which consists of 1,985 records that are not only generated but also clinically reasonable to include features like age, body mass index (BMI), salt intake, sleep duration, stress score, blood pressure history, and lifestyle factors like smoking status and physical activity. The architecture of the proposed model is dual-input, which combines embedding layers for categorical variables with batchnormalized fully connected layers for numerical features. The model thus is capable to efficiently identify complex, non-linear interactions that involve such a variety of risks that are categorized as being diverse. Data were preprocessed through label encoding and standard scaling prior to the training conducted in 30 epochs using binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimization. The model obtained a classification accuracy of 90.1%, ROC AUC of 0.967, and balanced precision, recall, and F1-scores in both classes during evaluation. The results indicate that the model can be considered a very powerful and helpful decision support tool for hypertension risk screening. Furthermore, it highlights the possibility of applying deep learning techniques on health data of mixed types for predictive healthcare purposes.

title: Design and Implementation of an AI-Powered Autonomous Security Robotic Dog with Integrated Face Recognition
authors: Mohammed Mujeerulla, Shakkeera L, Zafar Ali Khan N, Hemanth G, Aditya Sharma, Dashami S, Harsha Vardhana T
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: Traditional security systems mostly use fixed surveillance cameras, which often result in persistent blind spots and provide little ability to respond in real time to security threats. To address these limitations, we present the design and implementation of an autonomous robotic security dog, which utilizes recent progress in mobile robotics and artificial intelligence. The robot was built on a specially designed, 3D-printed four-legged frame powered by 12 servo motors for flexible movement, all managed by a Raspberry Pi 5 computing unit. Its intelligence centers on a real-time face recognition system, for which we developed and compared two approaches: a lightweight option using Haarcascades with a Python library and a more advanced method based on the Insight Face deep learning framework paired with Facebook’s AI similarity search tools. In our analysis, the Haarcascade system achieved an average recognition rate of 92.4 % when running on a Raspberry Pi 5, whereas the Insight Face solution reached 95% accuracy on a high-end CPU workstation. This study confirms the potential of a modular robotic platform that strikes a practical balance between computational efficiency and recognition performance in active security environments, while also providing a solid foundation for future upgrades, such as autonomous navigation, more sensors, and responsive real-time behaviours.

title: DynaSplit: Latency-Aware Dynamic Model Partitioning for Large Language Model Inference in the Edge-Cloud Continuum
authors: Tejas Pravinbhai Patel, Sandeep Shivam, Amit Kumar Padhy, Bharadwaj Vulugunda, Chaitanya Kulkarni, Chandrashekhar Medicherla
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have unlocked new possibilities for interactive intelligence on mobile and edge platforms. However, achieving sub- 300 ms end-to-end latency in real-world mobile environments remains an unsolved systems challenge. Pure cloud inference suffers from unpredictable round-trip delays, while fully local execution on commodity edge hardware is infeasible for multi-billion-parameter models. Existing edge-cloud LLM partitioning systems rely on static policies that ignore dynamic bandwidth fluctuations, interlayer activation variance, and the non-linear interactions of quantization and tensor parallelism. As a result, their latency degrades by up to 47<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>1Static partitioning: 710ms p95 vs. DynaSplit: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$290 \text{ms}=(710-290) / 290 \approx 145 \%$</tex> latency increase or <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1/1.45 \approx 47 \%$</tex> performance degradation. under typical 4G/5G volatility. We present DynaSplit, a latency-aware runtime system that dynamically selects (i) transformer-layer partition points, (ii) per-layer quantization levels, and (iii) cloud-side tensor-parallel degrees at fine-grained 100 ms intervals. DynaSplit incorporates a sub-millisecond performance model, a hybrid greedy-beam search optimizer with a search space reduction of >99.8%, and an asynchronous execution engine that overlaps GPU compute and RDMA transfers with up to 91 % pipeline utilization. Evaluated on an RTX A4000 edge node paired with a cloud A100 GPU and real 4G/5G-Fi traces, DynaSplit achieves 2.1-3.8 reduction in tail latency over DeepSpeed-Inference, PowerInfer, Orca, and vLLM for LLaMA-3-8B, Mixtral-8×7B, and Qwen-14B. To our knowledge, DynaSplit is the first system to consistently meet <300ms p95 latency for 8-14 B models on commodity edge hardware.

title: Predictive People Analytics: Real-Time AI Insights for Engagement and Attrition using Workday Data
authors: Suresh Kurapati, Vinod Veeramachaneni, Mugunth Chandran
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: For companies that wish to maintain a high level of productivity and a stable level of employees, the topics of employee engagement and attrition remain of significant importance. The conventional HR analytics techniques can be said to be reactive in nature, as they rely on lagging indicators as well as batch-based reporting. The current research has proposed a real-time prediction-based people analytics platform that integrates Workday-based information with multimodal AI models that rely on time series signals, organizational graphs, as well as static tabular-based HR data. The methodology has utilized discrete time survival modeling for the prediction of attrition risk, as well as multi-task learning for improving engagement as well as productivity, along with fairness-based intervention optimization. Experimental assessment across several baselines indicates enhanced predictive capability. The suggested model has AUC = 0.88 (4w) and 0.86 (12w) for attrition, which is better than Logistic Regression (0.72) and XGBoost (0.80). With a 24% drop in RMSE and a R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.72, engagement and productivity prediction was better, showing that behavioural modelling was stronger. The calibration study showed that the Expected Calibration Error was smaller (0.032) and the uncertainty coverage was higher (96.1%). The findings of the intervention uplift showed that changing pay (7.2% uplift) and management check-ins (net benefit score = 0.71) were both cost-effective ways to keep employees. Bias audits showed that differences between men and women, people from different places, and those at different career levels were smaller. These results show that the framework can give quick, accurate, and fair information, which lets firms improve engagement and lower the risk of losing employees.

title: Zero-Shot Video Deraining with Video Diffusion Models
authors: Tuomas Varanka, Juan Luis Gonzalez, Hyeongwoo Kim, Pablo Garrido, Xu Yao
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Existing video deraining methods are often trained on paired datasets, either synthetic, which limits their ability to generalize to real-world rain, or captured by static cameras, which restricts their effectiveness in dynamic scenes with background and camera motion. Furthermore, recent works in fine-tuning diffusion models have shown promising results, but the fine-tuning tends to weaken the generative prior, limiting generalization to unseen cases. In this paper, we introduce the first zero-shot video deraining method for complex dynamic scenes that does not require synthetic data nor model fine-tuning, by leveraging a pretrained text-to-video diffusion model that demonstrates strong generalization capabilities. By inverting an input video into the latent space of diffusion models, its reconstruction process can be intervened and pushed away from the model’s concept of rain using negative prompting. At the core of our approach is an attention switching mechanism that we found is crucial for maintaining dynamic backgrounds as well as structural consistency between the input and the derained video, mitigating artifacts introduced by naive negative prompting. Our approach is validated through extensive experiments on real-world rain datasets, demonstrating substantial improvements over prior methods and showcasing robust generalization without the need for supervised training.

title: SAIL: Self-supervised Learning of Lighting-Invariant Representations from Real Images with Latent Diffusion
authors: Hala Djeghim, Céline Loscos, Désiré Sidibé
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Intrinsic image decomposition aims at separating an image into its underlying albedo and shading components, isolating the base color from lighting effects to enable downstream applications such as virtual relighting and scene editing. Despite the rise and success of learning-based approaches, intrinsic image decomposition from real-world images remains a significantly challenging task due to the scarcity of labeled ground-truth data. Most existing solutions rely on synthetic data as supervised setups, limiting their ability to generalize to real-world scenes. Self-supervised methods, on the other hand, often produce albedo-like maps that contain reflections and lack consistency under different lighting conditions. To address this, we propose SAIL, an approach designed to estimate illumination-invariant representations from single-view real-world images. We repurpose the prior knowledge of a latent diffusion model for unconditioned scene relighting as a surrogate objective for learning light-invariant estimates. To this end, we introduce a novel intrinsic image decomposition fully formulated in the latent space. To guide the training of our latent diffusion model, we introduce regularization terms that constrain both the lighting-dependent and -independent components of our latent image decomposition. Through our experiments, we demonstrate that SAIL produces stable albedo-like representations under varying lighting conditions and generalizes to multiple scenes, using only unlabeled multi-illumination data available online.

title: Multi-Staged Transfer Learning with Progressive Fine-Tuning for Citrus Fruit Disease Classification
authors: Isaac Angelo M. Dioses, Romero Dante Salum, Mark Joseph A. Asuncion, Carmelo Alejo Bisquera, Michael John R. Robles, Romeo Evangelista
abstract: Accurate and reliable plant disease detection is essential for improving agricultural productivity and supporting precision farming systems. This study proposes a real Multi-Staged Transfer Learning (MSTL) framework for automated rice leaf disease classification using a dual backbone feature fusion model based on MobileNetV2 and EfficientNetB4. The proposed approach employs progressive stage-wise fine-tuning, where network adaptation is performed incrementally by selectively unfreezing different backbones across successive training stages. This strategy enables controlled domain adaptation while maintaining training stability and minimizing overfitting. Experimental results demonstrate a clear performance progression across stages, with the final model achieving an overall test accuracy of 96.39%, along with balanced precision, recall, and F1-score across both disease classes. Confusion matrix analysis confirms low and symmetrically distributed misclassification errors, indicating strong generalization on unseen data. The findings validate the effectiveness of true MSTL with dual-backbone fusion and highlight its potential as a robust and scalable solution for real-world agricultural disease monitoring applications.

title: Object Tracking and Shooting Turret
authors: Kishore A T, Vishnu Murukan, Adarsh P, Akhil V M
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Automation is one of the most important factors in the modern defense and security systems, which in turn guarantee the rapid response and precision. This is a project of a Smart Target Detection and Shootable Turret System that aims to achieve the automatic detection, tracking, and shooting of a target via the use of computer vision and control technologies of an embedded nature. The system uses a YOLO (You Only Look Once) deep learning model integrated with OpenCV for real-time object detection and tracking. The live video is from a laptop camera that is processed to locate the targets and get their coordinates. The coordinates are then changed to angular positions and sent to an Arduino microcontroller.The Arduino drives two MG995 servo motors i.e. one for turret rotation and the other for firing mechanism stabilization thus the target can be fixed accurately by the detected target. When the target is locked, the system can be remotely activated to engage the firing module.The model is a fine example of how deep learning and the use of embedded hardware can be brought together to come up with a cheap, intelligent, and automated defense system of the future. The defense applications are only a stepping stone to the technology, which is being considered for use as an autonomous surveillance system in hard-to-reach areas or places that are dangerous for humans.

title: Electricity Generation from Waste Materials
authors: K Praveen, Jayasri Nerella, Vilasagaram Bhanuteja, Yogeshwar Mahadev, Karuppiah Natarjan, Karuppiah Natarjan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The pressure on utilizing renewable energy is ever increasing and new methods of using waste products to produce energy are becoming relevant. This paper has given a system that can be used to convert waste heat into useful electrical energy using very basic electronic parts like capacitors, resistors, heating panels, temperature sensors, rechargeable batteries and LED lights. The system starts with collection of waste heat in the form of heating panels. Thermal sensors are used to measure thermal energy to optimize the performance of the system. Capacitors and resistors are used in stabilizing the generated voltage and controlling the flow of current. The resulting electricity is stored in rechargeable batteries and applied to run LED bulbs. The system is cheap and environmentally friendly and can be used in the microgrids and the small-scale renewable energy installations. They are the system design, efficiency, and scalability.

title: Lightweight UNet++ Based Multi-Sensor Fusion for Automated Building Footprint Extraction
authors: Nagadi Leela Rao, Deepak Kumar, Shivani Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Building Footprints are two-dimensional boundaries that identify buildings on the ground-level surface of the Earth. These are useful for urban planning, infrastructure mapping, disaster management, etc. The extraction process can be auto-mated using deep learning methods, making it faster and more resistant to errors. Although optical images are widely used for this purpose, they are not as reliable in cloud-occluded, shadow-covered areas, and low-contrast regions. Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data, on the other hand, are immune to these problems, but can show an underwhelming performance when used alone, due to inherent speckle noise. This paper aims to explore the automatic extraction of building footprints by fusing the two modalities using a light-weight, early fusion-based UNet++ model. The SpaceNet 6 Multi-Sensor All-Weather Mapping dataset (MSAW) has been used for this purpose.

title: A Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning Framework for Adaptable Cross-Organizational Collaboration
authors: A Padmavathi, Nimish Kushwaha, Sandesh Varma
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: The role of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in medical imaging has improved automated disease detection and segmentation to provide faster and more accurate diagnoses. However, developing high-performance deep learning models requires access to large datasets with diverse cases, which is challenging because of privacy rules, ethical considerations, and some organizations’ unwillingness to share patient data in healthcare contexts. Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a promising paradigm that allows institutions to collaboratively train a shared model without transferring raw data. Although FL preserves privacy by keeping data local, existing FL architectures still face challenges such as non-IID data distribution across institutions, communication overhead, and reduced performance when clients intermittently participate or drop out. This work proposes a collaborative privacy-preserving federated learning framework for medical image segmentation across hospitals. The system has implemented secure aggregation, differential privacy, and communication-efficient optimization to increase robustness in heterogeneous client environments. Evaluating the proposed system on medical imaging datasets shows improved segmentation performance and generalization of the model. Furthermore, the model maintains privacy guaranties while reducing sensitivity to client dropout. The results confirm that privacy-preserving FL can support multi-institutional collaboration for medical imaging, improving diagnostic performance without compromising patient confidentiality, thus providing a scalable direction for real-world healthcare deployment.

title: Fault Detection in Smart Grids Using Machine Learning and Raspberry Pi for Condition Monitoring
authors: Nithin Narayanan S, Kamalapuri Dinesh, M. Venkateshkumar, Hari Bala Vardhan S, Praveenraj R S
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Intelligent smart grids have replaced traditional electricity networks, bringing with them new methods of fault detection, monitoring, and response. Conventional relay-based protection techniques, which rely on set thresholds or impedance values, frequently fail in noisy or rapidly changing environments, particularly when Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) are included. In this study, a comparison has been made between three machine learning networks for detecting faults. The 50000+ dataset includes demand forecasting and grid stability. To find out how well the algorithms—Decision Tree, Random Forest, and SVM—identify deviations from typical operation, they are trained and assessed. In Hardware, the trained models are installed on a Raspberry Pi 4 for real-time relay indication. Among the approaches studied, the Random Forest classifier yields the most accurate and consistent results. The entire system shows how machine-learning models in conjunction with low-cost embedded platforms can improve grid reliability and make way for autonomous condition monitoring and fault-protection features in conventional smart grids.

title: A Modular Edge-Assisted Deep Learning System for Real-Time Intrusion Detection in Smart Surveillance
authors: M. Saranya, S Fazil, R Muthumari, B Nivethithan, S Meiyarul, R Ranjithkumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The smart surveillance systems demand smart solutions that have the capability to detect unauthorized or suspicious access with maximum accuracy and minimal latency. In the research, it is proposed that a suspected entry alarm system based on AI can be developed that would incorporate object recognition, behavior, and notifications provided by the IoT to enhance the success of real-time monitoring. Using the assistance of edge computing, the system exploits deep learning approaches in intrusion recognition to deliver low-latency inference and cloud servers so that it can afford scalability. Experimental validation represents that the performance is vigorous with an IoU of 0.78, and ROC-AUC of 0.94 that denote good localization of objects, proper intrusion classification, as well as reliable occurrence of anomalies. In addition, the system took an average of 1.8 seconds to respond. It has been built with its architecture divided into modular architecture such as preprocessing, tracking and alert management that enables it to suit varied smart surveillance setups. Overall, the developed solution provides a scalable and efficient framework that improves security surveillance, reduces the false alarm, and makes proactive decision-making in the smart surveillance systems.

title: Vector: Canonical Intent Graphs and Mode-Aware Execution for State-Persistent Web Interaction
authors: Gautham Venkatesan, B Sharan, P Rama, J. Kalaivani, V. Angayarkanni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)

title: Dual-Active-Bridge Converter with ANFIS Controller
authors: Oakkar Min, Rishabh Dev Shukla, Shilpa Gupta
publish: 2026 1st International Electronics &amp; Packaging Technologies Conference: Bridging Skills &amp; Innovation for India’s Industry (EPT India)
abstract: Dual Active Bridge (DAB) Converter is one of the most commonly used configurations for handling DC output in Electric Vehicle (EV) chargers and energy storage systems due to its potential for converting and transferring power, as well as its ability to isolate components in both directions. However, the standard PI controller is not able to respond quickly enough and lacks enough stability to keep the voltage regulated while also reacting to reference inputs. This paper describes a model for an ANFIS based controller and compares its performance against a PI controlled version. The model developed in the study uses an ANFIS based control scheme for a dual active bridge DAB converter that converts 800 V input voltages into adjustable output reference voltages of 396-387 Vdc. The simulation performed with MATLAB/Simulink has shown a reduction of approximately $23 \%$ in settling time and $20 \%$ improvement in rise time over the PI control method which completely eliminates any overshoot associated with PI controller response and also achieves a decrease in output voltage ripple of nearly $30\%$ when compared to PI controller methods. The output voltage closely tracks the reference input voltage with minimal steady-state error. The ANFIS controller developed in this study has demonstrated better stability and dynamic performance than traditional PI and fuzzy controllers for other DAB converters used in EVs and renewable energy applications in currently available literature.

title: Architecting Hybrid Data Products Using AI/ML and Agentic AI for Group Insurance and Retirement Solution Platforms with Advanced Data Governance
authors: Ramesh Inala, Goutham Kumar Sheelam, Avinash Reddy Aitha, Aragala Udaya Lakshmi, Kushvanth Chowdary Nagabhyru, Avinash Reddy Segireddy
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Hybrid data products combine the content, applicative, and consumption components of data sharing in a singular consumable package that any data consumer can leverage with little or no effort. Architectures facilitating hybrid data products, like data mesh and data fabric, address the increasing complexity of data integration and sharing across silos. At the same time, demand for agentic AI solutions—those that act on behalf of the user—is on the rise. Hybrid data products have particular relevance for group insurance and retirement solution platforms, given the availability of predictive and treatment effect models, semantic simulated events for scenario testing, and personalized decision recommendations. Extra care should be taken to ensure that data products in the financial domain do not perpetuate model or sampling bias and that they adhere to industry regulations, from data privacy—where applicable—to risk provisioning. Although clearly Patterned for Financial Services, these concerns are secondary to the stability, accessibility, and usability of hybrid data products at scale.

title: Guide for the redesign of websites not accessible to blind people: MiUV study case_supp1-3690147.docx
authors: Jose Rafael Rojano-Caceres
abstract: This research proposes a guideline that outlines the redesign process of a web system by compiling a set of tools and techniques aimed at improving accessibility for users with visual functional diversity (blindness). It is worth mentioning that the study adheres to the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) standards using automatic evaluation programs and screen readers. The paper presents the selection process for these tools and techniques, which constitute the guideline composed of five stages: (1) training on accessibility issues, (2) initial system exploration, (3) evaluation with users and experts, (4) requirements analysis, and (5) recoding. It also specifies the artifacts to be produced at each stage and how they are used in subsequent phases. The guideline was applied to the institutional system “MiUV” which is used by the Universidad Veracruzana community, including faculty members, administrative staff and students—some of whom have visual functional diversity. The results showed that the proposed process was effective in guiding the redesign to improve accessibility. However, opportunities for improvement were also identified in each phase of the redesign process.

title: Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data
authors: Zhe Cao, Tao Zhang, Zihua Zhao, Haonan Xin, Rong Wang, Feiping Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Multimodal remote sensing (RS) clustering synergistically integrates multi-dimensional information, effectively addressing the representational limitations of single-modality data. This integration offers essential technical support for fine-grained recognition and accurate interpretation of ground objects in complex scenarios. However, existing methods still face several challenges, including insufficient utilization of spatial information, limited ability to extract consistent information due to inter-modal heterogeneity, and low efficiency when handling large-scale and complex datasets. To address these issues, we propose an Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering (PSSC) of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data model. The proposed method begins by constructing spatial bipartite graphs from multimodal data to fully exploit spatial information while reducing computational complexity. These graphs are then stacked into a third-order tensor, upon which a robust denoised representation is learned to suppress noise and preserve the core structural characteristics of the multimodal inputs. Based on this clean tensor, PSSC captures cross-modal consistency by minimizing the tensor nuclear norm within the low-rank space. To further enhance clustering efficiency and accuracy, a region homogeneity-constrained rapid label generation strategy is proposed, which leverages high-confidence pseudo-supervision information from homogeneous regions to iteratively refine clustering labels, thereby significantly reducing computational overhead. Extensive experiments on real-world multimodal datasets validate the effectiveness and superior performance of the proposed method.

title: Flight Dynamics to Sensing Modalities: Exploiting Drone Ground Effect for Accurate Edge Detection
authors: Chenyu Zhao, Jingao Xu, Ciyu Ruan, Haoyang Wang, Shengbo Wang, Jiaqi Li, Jirong Zha, Weijie Hong, Zheng Yang, Yunhao Liu, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Xinlei Chen
abstract: Drone-based rapid and accurate environmental edge detection is highly advantageous for tasks such as disaster relief and autonomous navigation. Current methods, using radars or cameras, raise deployment costs and burden lightweight drones with high computational demands. In this paper, we propose AirTouch, a system that transforms the ground effect from a stability “foe” in traditional flight control views, into a “friend” for accurate and efficient edge detection. Our key insight is that analyzing drone basic attitude sensor readings and flight commands allows us to detect ground effect changes. Such changes typically indicate the drone flying over a boundary of two materials, making this information valuable for edge detection. We approach this insight through theoretical analysis, algorithm design, and implementation, fully leveraging the ground effect as a new sensing modality without compromising drone flight stability, thereby achieving accurate and efficient scene edge detection. We also compare this new sensing modality with vision-based methods to clarify its exclusive advantages in resource efficiency and detection capability. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our system achieves a high detection accuracy with mean detection distance errors of 0.051m, outperforming the baseline method performance by 86%. With such detection performance, our system requires only 43 mW power consumption, contributing to this new sensing modality for low-cost and highly efficient edge detection.

title: Real-time Dual Sensing of cTnI and Myoglobin via Printable Graphitized Electrodes
authors: Suhaib Imtiyaz, Mohsina Afrooz, Parvathy Nair, Sonal Fande, Khairunnisa Amreen, Sanket Goel
abstract: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) has emerged as a serious global health concern. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), health authorities report approximately 100 million cases annually worldwide, with about 9 million associated mortalities. As AMI can affect individuals regardless of their age or prior health status, making early and pre-symptomatic detection is crucial for clinical intervention. Targeting AMI biomarkers such as cardiac troponin I (cTnI) and myoglobin is therefore essential for timely diagnosis and treatment. Hence, the development of ultra-sensitive and highly specific sensors is crucial. Herein, a laser-induced graphene (LIG)-based electrochemical biosensor capable of dual detection of two pivotal AMI biomarkers, cTnI and myoglobin, is presented, hence addressing the need for simultaneous biomarker monitoring.
