authors: Md. Emran Biswas, Tangina Sultana, Tanbin Yasmin Tanny, Jahidatunnaher Tani, Md. Tanin Hossain, Hasi Saha, Ashis Kumar Mandal, Md. Delowar Hossain
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The timely and accurate identification of tomato leaf diseases is crucial for ensuring global food security and sustainable agricultural productivity. Manual visual inspection is often slow and error-prone, creating a pressing need for robust automated solutions. This study proposes a novel deeplearning model, the Regularized Head-Normalized EfficientNet (RHN-EfficientNet), for the automated classification of tomato leaf diseases. The model leverages transfer learning by integrating a pre-trained EfficientNetB0 backbone as a feature extractor with a custom-designed, heavily regularized classification head comprising Batch Normalization and Dropout layers to enhance generalization and prevent overfitting. The model was trained and evaluated on a curated dataset containing four classes: Healthy, Leaf Mold, Mosaic Virus, and Septoria Leaf Spot. The proposed RHN-EfficientNet achieved a state-of-the-art test accuracy of 97.92%, significantly outperforming several benchmark architectures including VGG16, ResNet50, and MobileNetV2. Visual analysis via confusion matrix and ROC curves, where all classes attained a perfect AUC of 1.00, further confirmed the model's exceptional discriminatory power and robustness, with only a single misclassification observed. These results conclusively demonstrate that the RHN-EfficientNet architecture is a highly effective and reliable tool for automated tomato leaf disease diagnosis, with the potential to be deployed in real-world agricultural applications.

title: Real Time Sinhala Lecture Transcription for Political Science
authors: G.W.A.J.C. Prabhashwara, W.A.C. Weerakoon
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: This research aims to develop an optimized automatic speech recognition system for transcribing Sinhala language lectures in the subject of Political Science. Current Automatic Speech Recognition systems, designed to work with any type of audio, often do not perform well in capturing domain-specific vocabulary; therefore, the models currently available will not provide robust transcription for lectures presented in the field of Political Science. The research executed a comparison study of Automatic Speech Recognition frameworks and chose the Wav2Vec2-XLS-R-300M model as the reference point because of its good equilibrium of precision and computational efficiency on CPU hardware. A significant gap in performance was identified when comparing the model's results to a domain-specific dataset, leading to a multi-staged improvement effort to close that gap. This approach consisted of adjusting the acoustic model for a specific Political Science dataset along with the addition of a 5-gram KenLM language model for phonetic error correction as a result of decoding. Following thorough refinement of the system's decoding parameters and the configuration for beam search, the system's transcription accuracy increased significantly. Ultimately, the final optimized system produced an average Word Error Rate of approximately 10.58% and an average Real Time Factor of 0.85, indicating that this Automatic Speech Recognition system is suitable for use in live academic environments.

title: A Transformer-Based Aspect-Level Sentiment Analysis Framework for Analyzing Public Discourse on Generative AI
authors: Neltjie Mendy Martutty, Muhammad Nur Rizal, Guntur Dharma Putra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: The rapid adoption of Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI), particularly systems such as ChatGPT, has generated large-scale public discourse on social media, presenting both opportunities and challenges for computational analysis. From a machine learning perspective, modeling such discourse remains difficult due to the informal and short-text nature of social media data, class imbalance, and the need to associate sentiment with specific semantic aspects. Existing studies predominantly rely on global sentiment classification or coarsegrained topic modeling, while recent transformer-based approaches mainly focus on improving classification accuracy without explicitly modeling aspect-specific cognitive dimensions.To address these limitations, this study proposes a transformer-based aspect-level sentiment analysis (ABSA) framework for fine-grained modeling of GenAI-related public discourse using large-scale data from X (formerly Twitter). The framework integrates data-driven aspect extraction with supervised aspect and sentiment classification, enabling structured representation of cognitively relevant aspects. A subset of 2,000 tweets is manually annotated to construct a ground-truth dataset for training and evaluation. Multiple machine learning and deep learning models are benchmarked, among which a hybrid BERT-GRU architecture demonstrates superior performance by combining contextual semantic representations with sequential dependency modeling.Experimental results show that the proposed hybrid model outperforms baseline approaches, achieving an accuracy of 85.25% and a Macro F1-score of 0.8496 under class imbalance conditions. Aspect-level sentiment inference further reveals heterogeneous sentiment distributions across cognitive aspects, indicating that sentiment polarity varies significantly depending on the semantic context being modeled.Overall, the proposed framework demonstrates the effectiveness of transformer-based ABSA for fine-grained sentiment modeling on short and informal social media texts and provides a scalable machine learning approach for analyzing complex, aspect-dependent public discourse on emerging AI technologies.

title: Investigation of Coupled RRAM and MOSFET Degradation in 1T1R Memory Cell
authors: Yuhang Yang, Yingchen Ji, Ruiqing Xie, Lin Bao, Zongwei Wang, Yimao Cai
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: Resistive random-access memory (RRAM) implemented in one-transistor–one-resistor (1T1R) cells has been adopted in system-on-chips (SoCs) across multiple technology nodes (40 nm, 28 nm, and 22 nm) for high-density non-volatile memory. However, reliability becomes increasingly critical due to the coupled degradation of the RRAM and the access transistor, particularly in advanced nodes. Existing studies typically evaluate RRAM endurance/retention and MOSFET hot-carrier injection (HCI) degradation independently, overlooking their synergistic interaction in 1T1R cells. In practice, RRAM resistance drift during cycling amplifies the stress on the MOSFET, forming a degradation-accelerating positive feedback loop. Based on fabricated 28 nm TaO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</inf>-based 1T1R arrays, this work systematically characterizes standalone RRAM (linear Set/Reset voltage drift) and MOSFET HCI (power-law current degradation), and establishes a coupled degradation model. The proposed model accurately predicts accelerated transistor degradation under time-varying stress and enables co-design optimization, such as MOSFET gate-width tuning, to meet specific end-of-life power and reliability targets—providing a crucial framework for robust, high-density 1T1R memory integration.

title: A Deep Bi-LSTM-Based Motor Imagery Braincomputer Interface for Wheelchair Navigation
authors: M.T.A. Karunarathne, J.S. Aththanayaka, S.M.U.S. Samarakoon, M.I. Uvais, H.M.K.K.M.B. Herath, S.L.P. Yasakethu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) systems provide autonomy to individuals with severe motor impairments, yet practical adoption is hindered by a significant performance gap in reliability. This paper addresses these limitations by presenting a BCI system for wheelchair navigation. We propose a deep Bi-LSTM network optimized to classify four navigation commands and a rest state as a default fail-safe from motor imagery EEG signals. Using the public PhysioNet dataset, seven sensorimotor channels were filtered in the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$8-30 ~\text{Hz}$</tex> range to isolate <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{m u}$</tex> and beta rhythms, achieving a mean accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 4}$</tex> (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.018$</tex>). The evaluation rigorously assesses the model using subject-independent splits and precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> scores to ensure deployment readiness. The system employs a cloud platform for high-fidelity command generation, while an ESP32-based hardware layer ensures responsive execution via MQTT. Validated through simulation and a physical prototype, this framework bridges the gap between the complexity of deep learning and the constraints of embedded systems, providing a robust command-generation module for assistive mobility.

title: A Hybrid Machine-Learning-Based Framework for Geometry-Temperature-Aware General Compact Model Parameter Extraction
authors: Wenrui Zhang, Shuhan Wang, Zheng Zhou, Fei Liu, Jinfeng Kang, Xiaoyan Liu
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This work proposes a MAR (Masked-Autoencoder-Regressor) algorithm for the semiconductor device compact model parameter extraction. This algorithm architecture aims to address challenges in compact model parameter extraction for real devices characteristics measuring from experiment and it also can be used as general model parameter extractor. The algorithm architecture consists of data mask preprocessing and a self-normalized autoencoder-regressor hybrid parameter extraction model. This algorithm was verified by extracting the DSOI MOSFETs’ model parameters under various conditions, including multiple device’s sizes, multiple temperatures, and different backgate biases. The final results show that the error under all conditions is below 10%, meeting industry requirements for compact model parameter extraction.

title: Digital Leadership and Crisis Management: Lessons from Higher Education Institutions in the Kingdom of Bahrain During the COVID-19 Pandemic
authors: Ahmed Arbab, Osama El Abdein, Siddiq Babikir
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic profoundly disrupted higher education systems worldwide, compelling universities to adopt rapid digital transformation strategies to ensure educational continuity. This study examines the role of digital leadership in mitigating the educational risks of the COVID-19 crisis within higher education institutions in the Kingdom of Bahrain. Through a conceptual and analytical framework, the paper explores three core dimensions: the concept and principles of digital leadership, the nature of risks and crises with a focus on the COVID-19 pandemic, and the practical measures undertaken by Bahraini universities to sustain learning during the crisis. The findings reveal that the pandemic accelerated the transition from traditional and semi-digital leadership models to fully digital leadership approaches in Bahraini universities. Institutional leaders effectively utilized digital tools, risk management frameworks, and e-learning systems to maintain academic quality and operational resilience. Moreover, the Higher Education Council’s (HEC) policies and guidelines played a critical role in ensuring the effectiveness of digital adaptation. The study concludes that digital leadership has become an essential framework for managing crises, enhancing institutional agility, and ensuring the sustainability of higher education in the digital era. It recommends continued investment in digital infrastructure, leadership development, and the institutionalization of digital leadership practices across universities.

title: Dual-Band Dual-Polarized Shared Aperture Traveling-Wave Antenna Using Hybrid Dual-Mode Floquet Excitation for Satellite ISAC Systems
authors: Dongseop Lee, Wonbin Hong
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: This paper presents a new concept of a shared aperture traveling wave antenna (SATWA) using Hybrid Dualmode Floquet (HDMF) excitation for satellite Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC). A single periodic unit cell simultaneously excites the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{n}=-\mathbf{1}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{n}=-\mathbf{2}$</tex> spatial harmonics to enable dual-band operation over upper-mid (UM) band and Kaband with dual-linear polarization. Surface-wave suppression and optimized fringing-field coupling are achieved by a via-wall structure. To cover multi-target/multi-channel communication, the exemplified 4 × 4 array is designed and achieves twodimensional (2D) wide spatial beam scanning across a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2: 1$</tex> frequency ratio without additional feed-network structure. The proposed antenna concept is the first to demonstrate dual-band <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(12-17 / 24-32 \text{GHz})$</tex>, dual-polarized operation with a peak gain greater than 12 dBi, aperture efficiency exceeding 45%, and 2 D beam steering of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 50^{\circ} / \pm 43^{\circ}$</tex>, all within the compact footprint of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.105 \lambda^{3}$</tex>.

title: Multi-Band Patch Antenna Array for Out-of-Band Aided Millimeter Wave Communication
authors: Faruk Pasic, Jure Soklič, Robert Langwieser, Stefan Schwarz, Christoph F. Mecklenbräuker
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: Future wireless communication systems will integrate both sub-6 GHz and millimeter wave (mmWave) frequency bands within multi-antenna architectures to meet the increasing demand for high data rates. In such multi-band systems, reliable information obtained from the sub-6 GHz band can be exploited to support communication at mmWave frequencies. To ensure that both systems experience similar multi-path propagation effects, the sub-6 GHz and mmWave antenna arrays have to be colocated and precisely aligned. However, such a configuration may adversely alter the radiation characteristics of the arrays, potentially degrading their performance. In this paper, we investigate the impact of positioning a mmWave antenna structure in front of a sub-6 GHz antenna structure. Through both simulations and measurements, we evaluate how the presence of the mmWave structure affects the radiation pattern of the csub-6 GHz one. The results demonstrate that the influence of the mmWave structure on the sub-6 GHz performance is minor, indicating that co-located configurations are feasible with negligible degradation.

title: Zero-Knowledge Consent: Auditable and Private Data Permission Management via Blockchain
authors: Filippo Scaramuzza, Marco Tonnarelli, Damian A. Tamburri, Willem-Jan van den Heuvel
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: The challenge of achieving auditable, GDPR-compliant consent management while preserving true data subject privacy persists, as current blockchain-based solutions compromise anonymity through public ledgers. This paper addresses this by introducing a novel, privacy-by-design IT artefact built on the Ethereum platform that employs Zero-knowledge Succinct Non-Interactive ARgument of Knowledge (zk-SNARKs) to enable truly anonymous and irrefutable validation of data processing permissions. Implemented and evaluated through a Design Science Research (DSR) approach, the artefact demonstrated a high degree of functional and GDPR compliance, confirming its capacity to generate trustless, auditable on-chain proof of permission status. The proof of concept successfully implements core privacy-by-design principles through anonymity and encryption, with technical benchmarks indicating acceptable performance for the artefact's intended use despite the computational intensity of proof generation.

title: Classifying Lemon Leaf Diseases Using MobileNetV3-Large for Better Crop Health Monitoring
authors: Tanya Gera, Noopur Tyagi, Sharnjeet Kaur, Sonam Jain, Diya Agnihotri, Ashish Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This research explores the use of the MobileNetV3-Large model in classifying five diseases in lemons: Anthracnose, Citrus Canker, Deficiency Leaf, Healthy Leaf, and Spider Mites. It worked with a dataset of 795 images, with a testing set of 20% and a training set of 80%. Preprocessing steps like grayscale, resizing, Gaussian filtering, and histogram equalization were applied to enhance features and improve performance. The model was trained for 20 epochs and achieved a total test set accuracy of 86%. Precision, recall, and F1-scores indicated balanced performance with Spider Mites and Healthy Leaf being most commonly misclassified. From the confusion matrix, it was established that the model correctly classified most diseases with minimal errors in Citrus Canker and Deficiency Leaf. This study demonstrates that deep learning, in this instance MobileNetV3-Large, can effectively be deployed in automated disease classification in lemons and can be a valuable tool in monitoring and managing diseases in agriculture.

title: Compact Transparent Dual-Band Antenna with Artificial Magnetic Conductor for Wearable Devices
authors: Anxiu Zhang, Zhenzhen Jiang, Heming Yang, Yuanrui Wang, Arjun Kumar, Mark Leach
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: A dual-band coplanar waveguide (CPW)-fed transparent antenna integrated with a flexible artificial magnetic conductor (AMC) reflector is proposed for wearable applications. The antenna uses polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as a flexible, low-loss substrate and MT150 film as a transparent conductive layer. A compact 3 × 2 AMC array provides in-phase reflection, suppresses backward radiation, and enhances forward gain. The prototype achieves bandwidths of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$130 \text{MHz}(2.37-2.50 \text{GHz})$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1400 \text{MHz}(5.10-6.50 \text{GHz})$</tex>, covering the 2.45 and 5.8 GHz bands. Realised gains increase to 6.2 and 8.2 dBi at 2.45 and 5.8 GHz, respectively. The antenna maintains stable performance under bending and exhibits a low specific absorption rate, offering a compact, high-gain solution for wearable communication systems.

title: On the Energy Consumption of Continuous Integration in Open-Source Java Projects
authors: Robert Arntzenius, Xutong Liu, Andy Zaidman
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Continuous integration is essential for software quality, yet the energy footprint associated with its frequent execution has largely remained invisible. We provide the first comprehensive baseline of CI energy use through a largescale study of 204 open-source Java projects with repeated measurements under Maven and Gradle. Our results show that energy use is highly skewed: while most projects consume energy modestly, a small number of “CI-intensive” systems can reach annual CI energy footprints of hundreds of kilowatt-hours, which is comparable to a quarter of an average EU household's electricity use. We further show that immediate, practical savings are possible: simply enabling dependency caching cuts energy by 30 % on average in some Maven projects and by over 90% in some Gradle cases. These findings matter not only for individual developers, but also for large organizations that run thousands of builds. In those settings, even small inefficiencies can add up to very large energy costs. By exposing where energy is consumed and how to reduce it, our study establishes an actionable foundation for greener CI pipelines.

title: Evaluating and Enhancing Large Language Models for Islamic Question Answering
authors: Md. Naimul Haque, Mostahid Hasan Fahim, M. Reza Selim, Md. Minhajul Haque, MD. Fuad Al Amin, Md. Ataullha, M. Shahidur Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This study investigates how large language models (LLMs) perform when applied to Islamic question answering. We investigate two models, Mistral-7B and LLaMA-3.2-3B, assessing their ability to answer multiple-choice and short-answer questions derived from a curated dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 3, 0 0 0}$</tex> Islamic questions. Unlike general-purpose evaluations, our work emphasizes the unique challenges posed by religious content, including the necessity to align with authentic sources and avoid interpretive errors. The study applies prompt engineering, targeted fine-tuning and Retrieval Augmented Generation(RAG) to enhance output quality. Evaluation was conducted using manual and LLM-as-a-judge approach. Results show significant improvements with prompt engineering: LLaMA-3.2-3B improved from 38.78 % to 64.19 %, while Mistral-7B improved from 41.84 % to 70.05 %. Fine-tuning further raised Mistral-7B performance to 68.11 %. Still, the accuracy is not acceptable in such a sensitive field. Hence, we implemented a RAG pipeline to supply grounded context from authentic religious sources. However, retrieval quality, chunking limitations, and embedding mismatch led to frequent irrelevant or missing evidence. These issues also caused mistakes in nuanced religious concepts. As a result, RAG produced only a modest accuracy improvement, remaining far below expectations.

title: Proposed Framework of Sustainable Value Co-Creation in Agricultural Supply Chain: A Bibliometric Perspective
authors: Masayu Rosyidah, Elisa Kusrini, Widodo Brontowiyono
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Sustainable value co-creation (SVC) has emerged as a strategic approach for enhancing resilience and sustainability within agricultural supply chains. Drawing on a bibliometric review of 366 Scopus-indexed journal articles published between 2012 and 2025, this study identifies four major thematic clusters and 44 high-frequency keywords that shape the intellectual structure of SVC research in agriculture. Descriptive trends reveal a sharp increase in publication activity after 2019, reflecting growing academic and practical attention to integrating social, environmental, and digital dimensions in agri-food systems. The bibliographic coupling analysis (threshold: Total Link Strength = 26) highlights four key clusters—digital collaboration and agri-food innovation, cooperative-driven sustainability, circular innovation and industry 4.0, and IoT-enabled co-creation—that collectively represent the evolving research landscape. Based on this synthesis, a conceptual framework is proposed integrating Green Intellectual Capital (GIC), Green Transformational Leadership (GTL), Digital Transformation (DT), and Dynamic Capabilities (DC) as critical antecedents of SVC. This framework advances the Resource-Based View (RBV), Service-Dominant Logic (SDL), and Dynamic Capabilities (DC) theory, emphasizing how green resources, leadership, and digital enablers foster adaptive, multi-actor collaboration for sustainability. In practical terms, it offers guidance for agricultural stakeholders to strengthen knowledge-based resources, embrace digital transformation, and enhance adaptive capabilities to achieve sustainable performance aligned with SDG 8, SDG 9, SDG 12, and SDG 13.

title: Harmonizing Nature: Real-Time Plant Signal Analysis for Music Generation, Interactive Sound, and Moisture Monitoring
authors: V Shankar, Venkat Raghavendra A H, V Hemanth, Sushanth M. G., Ashwini Bhat
abstract: This project presents a cost-effective system for analyzing plant responses by integrating hardware and software. An Arduino measures plant capacitance, which is processed in Python to generate ambient music, while a Max-Patch transforms plants into interactive instruments that respond to touch. A notable feature is a water alarm that alerts users when moisture levels drop, supporting timely irrigation and plant health. This real-time monitoring setup enables precision watering and highlights creative applications in Plant Signal Extraction. Designed for affordability and scalability, the system provides an accessible tool for plant signal analysis, interactive sound generation, and moisture detection, suitable for both research and artistic exploration.

title: Non-Volatile Polarization-Reconfigurable SSPP Antenna
authors: Ziqi Ren, Xiaoyu Xiao, Zhirun Hu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: A non-volatile polarization reconfigurable spoof surface plasmon polariton (SSPP) antenna is presented. Twelve bistable and non-volatile Nafion memristive switches are placed across the single gap of each circular slot element patterned along the SSPP line. By controlling the switches into different resistive states, the antenna can realize circular, elliptical, and linear polarization states without any static bias power. Three switching configurations are implemented: all-OFF, all-ON, and an alternating complementary pattern. Simulated and measured <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$S_{11}$</tex> results confirm consistent impedance characteristics across the operating band, while the simulated radiation patterns verify stable beam directions and clear polarization reconfigurability. The proposed design provides an energy-efficient solution for adaptive and programmable microwave systems.

title: CodeScent: A Deep Ensemble and LLM-Based Framework for Python and Java Code Smell Detection with Android Integration
authors: Tasnia Haque Kheya, Shaikh Prothomaa Binte Minhaz
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Code smells are recurring design flaws that negatively affect software maintainability, readability, and overall quality. Automated detection remains challenging, particularly across heterogeneous programming languages. In this study, we propose a multi-stage framework of code smell detection in Python and Java with Android aggregation. This framework includes feature-based heuristics, deep learning, ensemble models, and LLM-assisted analysis. The system was designed for testing on both binary (smelly and non-smelly) and multi-class smell detection. It identified common smells like Long Method, Long Parameter List, Data Class, Large Class, Feature Envy, and Duplicate Code. Our experimental results show strong performance, with test accuracies around <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$94-96 \%$</tex>, ROC-AUC of 0.98, precision between 0.82 and 0.84, recall reaching 0.98, F1-scores around 0.90, and Matthews correlation coefficients (MCC) ranging from 0.83 to 0.87. To improve understanding, the framework generates confidence scores and human-readable explanations. A major contribution of this work is the development of an Android application that implements the detection pipeline. This app allows users to input source code and get instant feedback on detected smells, along with explanations and confidence levels. This deployment shows the practical use of our approach and actively demonstrates the potential for scalable, cross-language tools to improve software quality.

title: NextRAG: A Bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Financial Question-Answering
authors: Sayed Ashraful Islam Opin, Raiyan Ahmed Thakur, Ahsanul Karim Raiyan, Muhammad Rafsan Kabir, Shafin Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Financial institutions and regulators produce large volumes of complex, multilingual documents, making access to critical financial information challenging. Limited financial literacy further exacerbates this issue in countries like Bangladesh. This study introduces NextRAG, a bilingual Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework for financial knowledge retrieval and question answering in the Bangladeshi context. Unlike conventional RAG pipelines, NextRAG employs specialized preprocessing for noisy financial PDFs using OCR. The framework integrates sparse retrieval with BM25, probabilistic query modeling via Dirichlet smoothing, and fuses results using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), followed by cross-encoder re-ranking. It also supports Bangla and English interactions through languageaware prompting. Evaluation on a diverse bilingual financial Q&A dataset shows that NextRAG consistently outperforms conventional RAG, achieving a mean cosine similarity of 0.77 and an F1 score of 68.3%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of hybrid retrieval in handling complex financial terminology, reducing retrieval errors and hallucinations in a low-resource bilingual setting.

title: PoultryNet: A Lightweight CNN Student Model for Chicken Disease Classification via Knowledge Distillation from Vision Transformer
authors: Md Parvez Kabir, Rasedul Islam, Jhion Adbar Opi, Md Aminur Rahman Joy, Arefin Ahmed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Poultry diseases seriously endanger the poultry industry, leading to economic losses, as well as undermining food security. While there are various precise heavyweight disease detection models, there are few fast, lightweight, as well as deployable, solutions that are appropriate for real-world farm deployments. This research bridges this gap by introducing PoultryNet, a tailored lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) trained as a student network through Knowledge Distillation (KD) from a Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) teacher. While the teacher attains 98.83 % accuracy on a Kaggle chicken disease dataset containing four disease classes, PoultryNet achieves a reasonable test accuracy of 95.93%, presenting a fast and resource-optimized alternative. Extensive experiments confirm that PoultryNet not only surpasses the best classification performance on all four classes of diseases but also requires a much smaller model size (15.6 MB) as well as a smaller number of parameters (4 million) yet attaining over 95 % teacher- network compression. It is contrasted with baseline CNN-based architectures, including MobileNetV2, MobileNetV3, SqueezeNet, ShuffleNet, as well as EfficientNet, demonstrating higher efficiency as well as comparable or improved performance. Weighted precision, recall, F1-score, as well as Cohen's Kappa, also affirm its high strength. This work presents the capability of distillation-based knowledge aiding fast, precise, as well as deployable, poultry disease identification, offering an efficient farmer-deployed tool capable of scaling up to extensive health monitoring.

title: LED-Tuned HR-Si Monopole Antenna for K-Band Reconfiguration
authors: Fatma Elhouni, Yi Huang, Yaochun Shen
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: This paper presents a photoconductivity reconfigurable K-band monopole antenna based on high-resistivity silicon (HR-Si), optically controlled via high-power LED illumination. Using a material-informed model and measured optical intensity from high power LED, we estimate a photoconductivity range of 20–100 S/m. A 1.5 mm HR-Si monopole exhibits a resonant frequency shift from 27 GHz to 24 GHz as conductivity increases. This behavior is attributed to changes in effective permittivity <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$</tex>, which alters the guided wavelength and impedance matching. A photoconductive model is used to capture responses of the photoconductive layer. Simulated results improve from S11-3dB to −6dB across the conductivity range. To confine LED illumination to the active region and prevent parasitic photoconductive activation of the surrounding HR-Si wafer a contact optical mask is proposed, and future extensions include pixel-level optical reconfiguration using a DLP projector. This work demonstrates a compact, bias-free approach to tunable silicon antennas driven by optical intensity alone.

title: A Comprehensive Study of AI in Engineering Organizations’ Decision-Making: Capabilities, Ethics, and Performance
authors: Adel Mahmoud Al Samman
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This article explores the role of AI in decision-making within one of the construction companies (n = 297). Descriptive and regression models suggest that the AI capabilities and task automation have stronger impacts on decision effectiveness (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.288; β = 0.611, p < 0.001), whereas learning approaches and architectural techniques impact it weakly. The paper brings up ethical issues such as bias, transparency, job removal, and suggests XAI, mechanisms of fairness, and upskilling of the workforce. Limitations are single-industry scope and mediocre goodness of fit; future studies need to increase sample size and study other organizational moderators.

title: Resilient Communication Hubs Over LEO Satellite Networks
authors: Yue Cui, Tian Liu
abstract: In severe hazardous situations, reliable network communication is essential for conveying vital, real-time information from field-deployed sensors and devices. Critical communications must be received quickly to support life-saving decisions and situational awareness, yet maintaining continuous connectivity is challenging when terrestrial infrastructure is impaired. To ensure seamless, fault-tolerant, and secure communication, we propose a new system: Resilient Communication Hubs (RC Hubs) over Low-Earth-Orbit (LEO) satellite networks. In this architecture, reliability is improved by using Multi-Path TCP (MPTCP) across diverse satellite paths and optional terrestrial fallbacks, maintaining end-to-end connection semantics while rapidly recovering from subflow failures. LEO satellite networks provides beyond-line-of-sight coverage but network transmission can be expensive, so the hub supports cost-aware path selection and policy controls to minimize satellite airtime when alternatives exist. The system dynamically selects an edge node as the active satellite gateway, and MPTCP preserves session continuity across gateway switches by maintaining parallel subflows through the old and new gateways. We have implemented the RC hub with integrated LEO terminals and conducted experiments under varied mobility and impairment conditions. Results show that the proposed architecture is rapidly deployable and sustains reliable communications despite partial failure or loss of terrestrial infrastructure.

title: Decentralized Online Learning with Hard Constraints: A Doubly-Bounded Queue Approach
authors: Yituo Liu, Weiyi Qin, Wei Bao, Juncheng Wang, Jianxiong Guo, Min Zhou
abstract: We consider decentralized online convex optimization with time-varying constraints and conduct performance analysis using two stringent metrics: network dynamic regret with respect to the online global solution benchmark, and hard constraint violation that does not allow any compensated violation over time and learners. We propose an efficient algorithm called Constrained Online Learning with Doubly-bounded Queue (COLDQ), which introduces a novel virtual queue that is both lower and upper bounded, allowing tight control of the constraint violation without needing the Slater’s condition. We prove via a new Lyapunov drift analysis that COLDQ provides O(T 1+Vx/2 ) network dynamic regret and O(min{T 3+Vx/4 ; TVg }) hard constraint violation, where Vx and Vg capture the dynamics of the loss and constraint functions. For the first time, the two bounds smoothly approach to the best-known O(T 1/2 ) regret and O(1) violation, as the dynamics of the losses and constraints diminish, under both centralized and decentralized settings. For strongly convex loss functions, COLDQ provides O(log T) static regret and O(min{T1/2 (log T)1/2 , TVg }) hard constraint violation. Simulation results based on synthetic and canonical datasets demonstrate that COLDQ substantially outperforms the state-of-the-art approaches for both convex and non-convex loss functions.

title: A Grapheme-Aware LLM Integrated Hybrid Framework for Automated Text-to-Chart Conversion in Bengali Language
authors: Samia Rahman, Momtazul Arefin Labib
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: In domains like economics, health, and journalism, text embedded with numerical data is common, yet readers often struggle to derive insights. Converting such texts into charts enhances comprehension but is typically labor-intensive and domain-dependent. Despite progress in English, there is no prior dataset for Bengali. To fill this gap, we have introduced BETAR, a BEngali Text-to-chARt dataset comprising 3,519 annotated texts. We also propose BN-GraBERTNet, a grapheme-aware hybrid model that combines BanglaBERT, BiLSTM, and a fully connected layer to identify <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$x$</tex>-axis and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$y$</tex>-axis entities in text. To handle complex numerical reasoning, we selectively employ open-source large language models (LLMs) to simplify sentences when necessary. These simplified sentences are then processed by our sequence tagging model. The primary goal of this work is to develop a lightweight, user-friendly, and cost-free Bengali test-to-chart system that performs competitively. Although we also evaluated open-source, purely LLM-based approaches, our proposed architecture outperformed them, achieving a weighted average <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> score of 0.93 on the test set.

title: AI-Assisted IoT Framework for Water Quality Monitoring with Real-Time WQI Prediction and Cleaning Forecasting
authors: Tushar Sadanand Alande, Varsha K. Harpale, Aryan Anand Bharaswadkar, Siddhant Shantkumar Babanagar
abstract: Public health depends on the quality of their drinking water. But continuous access to systems that monitor quality of drinking water is not readily available widely. The common quality checking methods are based primarily on laboratory testing and manual sample collection. This is time consuming and does not ensure precise data. This leads to poor planning of maintenance and slow reaction to the contamination. To address these issues, we propose an IoT framework featuring a dual-node architecture that monitors water quality at the input and output nods of the storage system for increased assessment reliability. The system monitors the real-time values of necessary water parameters like pH, TDS, turbidity and electrical conductivity. The proposed framework uses an SVR model for predicting realtime WQI value and predictive scheduling of water tank cleaning. This combined monitoring-and-forecasting approach makes it suitable for optimal maintenance planning of water storage tanks. The results and real-time tracking are easily accessible using a mobile application and a web dashboard for remote supervision. The proposed solution provides a low-cost, scalable, and intelligent approach that is suitable for both urban and rural water storage environments.

title: Investigating Feature Selection Techniques to Enhance the Performance of Parkinson's Disease Detection Using Tablet Data
authors: Abu Saleh Musa Miah, Md. Maruf Al Hossain, Mohammad Hasan, Md. Abedur Rahman, Fahim Ashhab, Muhammad Nazrul Islam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is the second most common neurodegenerative disorder, particularly affecting the elderly. Handwriting analysis has shown promise in detecting PD, but effective classification requires extracting and selecting relevant features. In this study, we extracted 30 features using kinematic, temporal, and statistical methods, and applied six dimensionality reduction algorithms to select efficient features. Two classifiers—Random Forest and Decision Tree were then used to distinguish PD patients from healthy controls. Experiments were conducted on a spiral handwriting dataset containing samples from 62 PD patients and 15 healthy individuals. Our model achieved higher accuracy (by over 4 %) compared to existing methods, as demonstrated by confusion matrices, accuracy tables, and comparative analysis.

title: Comparative Analysis of Machine Learning Models for Accurate Solar Energy Forecasting
authors: Md.Iftakhar Ahsan Jarif, Abir Tirtha Das, Bishwajit Banik Pathik
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: To keep the grid stable, to improve storage and to help the energy market work, accurate solar power prediction is needed. The Calgary dataset was used in the study to compare machine learning and deep learning models—XGBoost, CatBoost, LightGBM, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN)—for short-term energy forecasting. The study also follows a method that includes preprocessing, feature engineering and model training to show the weather and time relationships. The coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex>), the Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error (sMAPE), the Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) were used to assess the models. The findings show that gradient boosting techniques regularly beat deep learning models in this setting. XGBoost got the highest accuracy, recording an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> of 0.997 and an RMSE of 3.64 kWh. It outperforms CatBoost and LightGBM. CatBoost and LightGBM which had higher error rates. Conversely, LSTM and TCN exhibited subpar performance, suggesting challenges in managing feature-sparse datasets with restricted temporal data. The findings indicate that gradient boosting techniques, especially XGBoost, are resilient, effective and ideally suited for enhancing renewable energy integration.

title: Steering the Beam of Smallest Aperture Horn Using Metallic Metasurfaces
authors: Foez Ahmed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The miniature phase gradient metallic metasurfaces (MMs) are presented to steer the beam of the smallest aperture horn antenna. The near-field phase transformation theory is used in the construction of the MMs to generate the necessary phase gradients at 12.5 GHz. The MMs are designed from low-cost, off-the-shelf thin copper sheets using a slot-based design approach. The lack of dielectrics in the design lowers the cost and allows them to be used in extreme environments, like high-power and space applications. A commercial electromagnetic simulator is used to model the antenna and verify its beam-steering concept numerically. Excellent beam steering performance is indicated by this preliminary investigation. With MM1, the beam can be steered at 43° as expected. When MM1 is fixed and MM2 is rotated at an angle of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$180^{\circ}, 90^{\circ}$</tex> and 80°, the beam-steering angles in the elevation plane are <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0^{\circ}, 55^{\circ}$</tex> and 42° at 12.5 GHz. Each MM is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.77 \lambda_{0}$</tex> thick and overall antenna profile is only <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.77 \lambda_{0}$</tex>.

title: Water Quality Monitoring and Detection Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning Techniques with IoT Sensor-Based Data
authors: Md. Jakir Hossen, Riadul Islam Rabbi, Sutiyo, Hilal H. Nuha
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Water quality monitoring is crucial for safeguarding public health and ensuring safe drinking water. Traditional methods for testing water quality are slow, expensive, and require manual labor, making them unsuitable for continuous monitoring. This study explores the integration of Internet of Things (IoT) technology with machine learning and deep learning techniques for automated water quality monitoring and anomaly detection. We compared the performance of six distinct algorithms: Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost, Deep Neural Network (DNN), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), and 1D Convolutional Neural Network (1D-CNN). The study utilized real water quality data, incorporating parameters such as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p H}$</tex>, temperature, turbidity, and dissolved oxygen. To address data imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was employed, and an 80:20 train-test split was used for evaluation. The results indicate that traditional machine learning methods outperformed deep learning approaches. Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy of 85 %, followed by XGBoost at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 2 \%}$</tex> and Decision Tree at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 5 \%}$</tex>. In contrast, the deep learning models exhibited lower performance: the DNN reached an accuracy of 67 %, the ANN attained 68 %, and the 1D-CNN recorded <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6 4 \%}$</tex>. The traditional machine learning models also demonstrated superior precision, recall, and F1-scores compared to the deep learning methods. These findings suggest that, for water quality monitoring applications, traditional machine learning algorithms are likely to be more suitable than complex deep learning models, particularly in IoT environments with limited computational resources. The study offers valuable insights into the selection of appropriate algorithms for realworld water quality monitoring systems.

title: Cyber Physical Hardening of BLE Microsensor Systems Through Systematic VAPT and IDS Based Mitigation
authors: Ramya K
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: BLE enabled microsensor platforms are increasingly integrated into cyber physical systems (CPS), yet their constrained architectures and wireless interfaces introduce cross-layer attack surfaces. This work presents a unified Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT) framework that evaluates the sensing element, embedded controller, and BLE telemetry as a single cyber-physical chain. A custom inkjet-printed electrochemical sensor interfaced with an ESP32-S3 BLE module is used as the experimental system and subjected to firmware, physical, and wireless-layer attacks. Results indicate that environmental and EMI disturbances induced up to ±23% variation in sensor output without triggering alarms, while controlled signal injection produced 100% acceptance of artificial values by the ADC. Firmware extraction and modification were successful via unsecured bootloader access in under 40 seconds, and BLE spoofing achieved unauthorized connection with a 92% success rate. Replay attacks consistently bypassed freshness validation with 100% packet acceptance, while BLE fuzzing induced intermittent instability in 31% of test cycles. Mitigations including secure boot, authenticated firmware updates, BLE encryption, and hardened pairing were applied, after which replay and spoofing attempts were reduced to 0% success, and firmware modification was fully blocked. These results demonstrate the need for holistic cyber-physical hardening of BLE microsensors. The proposed framework provides a repeatable methodology for securing microsensor CPS deployments.

title: Team Synergy-Aware Workforce Scheduling: Integrating Interpersonal Compatibility and Endogenous Turnover Dynamics
authors: Aijia Liu, Chaeeun Hong, Fumin Chang, Shizuka Ebisudani, Sejun Woo, Kiyotaka Shin, Hiroshi Takahashi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: In the labor-intensive service industry, workforce stability is paramount for sustaining competitive advantage, yet traditional scheduling models predominantly focus on cost minimization and demand coverage, neglecting the critical social dimension of employee-employee synergy. This oversight frequently creates a vicious cycle of dissatisfaction, turnover, and the subsequent erosion of service quality. Addressing this deficiency, this study proposes an extended optimization framework that quantitatively evaluates the impact of interpersonal compatibility on long-term organizational viability. A distinguishing feature of our approach is the endogenous modeling of workforce dynamics: we introduce a stochastic affinity generation mechanism to model realistic personality interactions and an endogenous turnover function that explicitly links cumulative team incompatibility to the attrition of experienced staff. Through rigorous multi-period dynamic simulations, we demonstrate that a synergy-prioritized strategy effectively disrupts the cycle of skill drain, maintaining superior service quality compared to traditional baseline methods. The quantitative results reveal significant revenue divergence between the two strategies, providing compelling evidence that optimizing for measurable team synergy yields substantial long-term financial benefits and transforms team composition from a mere operational constraint into a strategic asset for sustainability.

title: Unveiling Gate Leakage Dynamics in Nanosheet FETs at Cryogenic Temperature: A TCAD Study
authors: Prabhat Singh, Malvika, Mohd. Shakir, Sandeep Kumar, Sunil Rathore, Navjeet Bagga, S. Dasgupta
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: In this paper, we investigate the gate-leakage mechanism at cryogenic temperature (CT) in the Nanosheet FET (NSFET) using well-calibrated TCAD models. Using the Global TCAD Solution (GTS) tool, the carrier transport at cryogenic temperatures could be precisely captured by the Quasi-Fermi Transport (QFT) model. However, for evaluating gate leakage or OFF current, two possible models are available, i.e., the Tsu-Esaki and Fowler-Nordheim models, which require careful attention for accurate simulation. Thus, this study aims to explore the underlying behavior of the Cryo-NSFET characteristics with an appropriate modeling setup. As the temperature decreases to a deep-cryogenic range, the Fermi-Dirac distribution, i.e., the Fermi tail, becomes narrower, implying that fewer carriers have high energy, and thermal-assisted tunneling decreases. This results in a reduction in gate leakage current at all gate voltages. Our findings indicate that under CT, the absence of thermal excitation results in a shift in the transport mechanism to quantum tunneling. Additionally, gate tunneling is enhanced by a higher drain bias, which allows for the control of the potential barrier profile.

title: Educational Data Mining of VLE Interaction Logs for Explaining Student Assessment Outcomes: A K-Means Clustering Study
authors: Yasanthika Mathotaarachchi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Virtual Learning Environments (VLEs) generate extensive traces of learner interaction; however, educators and institutions often lack interpretable analytical approaches to determine which online learning activities meaningfully contribute to assessment success. This study applies Educational Data Mining (EDM) techniques to investigate the relationship between students' online learning behaviors and assessment outcomes using large-scale VLE interaction data. The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD), containing detailed student, assessment, and activity records across multiple course presentations, is used as the empirical basis of the analysis. A consolidated learner–activity–assessment dataset is constructed through relational integration and preprocessing of multiple OULAD tables. K-Means clustering is applied to identify distinct engagement–performance profiles without relying on predefined outcome labels. The analysis results in four clusters with heterogeneous engagement characteristics, among which one cluster emerges as dominant, capturing the highest proportion of meaningful learning interactions and demonstrating a strong association with higher assessment performance. Further analysis examines activity-type distributions across clusters to evaluate the relative influence of different online learning activities. The results indicate that engagement with structured learning resources and core content activities exhibits the strongest relationship with academic success, while activities supporting formative practice and interaction contribute positively when combined with contentfocused engagement. In contrast, excessive interaction with navigational or supplementary activities shows limited association with improved performance. These findings suggest that engagement quality and activity semantics are more informative indicators of learning effectiveness than overall activity volume. The study provides interpretable, cluster-based evidence to support data-driven instructional decision-making and highlights the importance of guiding students toward highimpact learning activities in higher education contexts.

title: Electronic Nose-Driven Classification of Sri Lankan Teas: An Ensemble Learning Approach Based on Aroma Profiles
authors: Bhashitha Kaveesh, Thosini Kumarika, Dakshika Wanniarachchi, Tharaga Sharmilan
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Geographical authentication of tea is critical for quality assurance, brand protection, and fraud prevention in the global tea market. However, conventional approaches based on sensory evaluation and chemical analysis are often subjective, labor-intensive, time-consuming, and costly, limiting their scalability for routine authentication. This study proposes an integrated electronic nose (E-Nose) and machine learning framework for the geographical origin classification of Sri Lankan tea using aroma profiles. Aroma data were collected from 367 dry tea samples representing seven major tea-growing regions using a custom-developed metal oxide semiconductor (MOS)-based Digi-Nose device equipped with seven gas sensors. The acquired sensor responses were digitally recorded and structured for supervised learning analysis. To ensure reliable and unbiased model training, the dataset was preprocessed and balanced prior to classification. Eight supervised machine learning models, such as ensemble-based (Extra Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost, CatBoost, LightGBM), distance-based (K-Nearest Neighbors), kernel-based (Support Vector Machine), and neural network-based (Multi-Layer Perceptron) approaches, were experimentally evaluated. Model performance was assessed using multiple metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, confusion matrices, learning curve analysis, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex>-fold cross-validation to verify robustness and generalization. Among all evaluated models, the Extra Trees classifier demonstrated superior performance, achieving an overall classification accuracy of 98.63 %, along with consistently high precision and recall across all tea regions. The findings confirm that the integration of MOS-based E-Nose sensing with ensemble learning techniques provides a reliable, non-destructive, and deployment-ready solution for aroma-based geographical authentication of tea, with strong potential for real-world industrial and regulatory applications.

title: Explainable AI (XAI) Driven Neural–Machine Learning Ensemble for Business Customer Churn Prediction
authors: Munshaibur Rahman Mahin, Abdullah Al Prince, Arif Rahman Samad, Md Rajib Ahmed, Shajedul Hasan Arman, Md Mahadi Hasan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Customer churn prediction is a critical challenge in the telecommunications industry, where retaining subscribers is far more cost-effective than acquiring new ones. Traditional models such as XGBoost, LightGBM, and Random Forest achieve competitive accuracy but often lack interpretability and struggle with complex non-linear relationships. To overcome these limitations, this paper introduces the proposed stacked ensemble framework, which integrates tree-based learners with a neural Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) as the meta-classifier. This design exploits the complementary strengths of machine learning and deep learning, combining robust decision boundaries with enhanced feature representation. To mitigate class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) is applied. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves superior performance, with an accuracy of 85.2%, precision of 71.7%, recall of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 5. 4 \%}$</tex>, F1-score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7 3. 5 \%}$</tex>, and ROC-AUC of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 9. 1 \%}$</tex>. Furthermore, the proposed model delivers explainable predictions via SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), enabling identification of key churn drivers. By unifying predictive strength with interpretability, the proposed framework provides both global and local insights, offering an accurate, transparent, and business-ready solution for customer retention in telecommunications.

title: Comparative Analysis of Configurable Logic Block Using Static CMOS, Transmission Gate and Gate Diffusion Input Techniques
authors: Md. Mahidul Haque, Md. Mahfuzur Rashid, Saquib Farhan, Md Tawfiq Amin
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Configurable Logic Block (CLB) is the core building element that determines the overall speed, power and area of a device. Conventional CLB implemented using Static CMOS logic offer robustness and reliability yet they occupy larger silicon area and consume more energy which limits efficiency in low power applications. The objective of this research work is to explore alternative logic styles for CLBs and to evaluate their impact on key performance metrics. The study investigates Static CMOS logic, Transmission Gate Logic (TGL) and Gate Diffusion Input (GDI) logic using a 90 nm process in Cadence Virtuoso with schematic design, layout implementation and post layout simulation analysis. Metrics such as state transition delay, setup time, clock to Q delay, maximum operating frequency, layout area, energy per cycle, and a composite figure of merit are analyzed. The results show that Static CMOS delivers the lowest delay of 55.49 ps but requires the largest area of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2176.52 \mu ~\mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex> and the highest energy of 171.6 fJ. By contrast, TG Logic reduces the area to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2025.08 \mu ~\mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex>, provides moderate delay, and achieves the lowest energy consumption at 139.1 fJ, while GDI offers the most compact design with a minimal area of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$804.824 \mu ~\mathrm{m}^{2}$</tex>. The findings highlight the trade offs among logic styles and provide guidelines for CLB design in future FPGAs where speed, area, and energy efficiency are critical.

title: A Capacitor-Less 22nm FDSOI-Based Event-Driven Pixel Utilizing Tunable Photoresponse Speed
authors: Nan Tang, Yi Xiao, Lianliang Wu, Jiaqi Li, Ao Shi, Zheng Zhou, Lifeng Liu, Xiaoyan Liu, Jinfeng Kang, Peng Huang
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate a capacitor-less pixel fabricated in a 22nm fully depleted silicon-on-insulator (FDSOI) process for event-based vision sensors (EVS). The proposed pixel leverages the photovoltaic-like effect of the negatively biased p-type well to enable electrical readout of light intensity directly from the MOSFET channel current. By engineering the exposure region of the FDSOI phototransistor, we achieve tunable photoresponse speed, as confirmed by both TCAD simulations and experimental results. Based on these characteristics, we designed and fabricated a capacitor-less event-driven pixel that generates voltage spikes only in response to changes in light intensity. Owing to the adjustable photoresponse speed and active nature of FDSOI phototransistors, the proposed design achieves spike output suitable for downstream circuit processing with a compact and simplified circuit structure.

title: Dual-Band Slot-Loaded Microstrip Patch Antenna for 5G mmWave and Ka-Band Applications
authors: Mehlaqa, Md. Farhad Hossain, Debprosad Das, Md. Azad Hossain
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper presents a dual-band slot-loaded microstrip patch antenna designed for 5 G millimeter-wave (mmWave) and satellite Ka-band applications. The antenna employs a quarter-wave transformer for effective impedance matching and integrates two F-shaped slots and one I-shaped slot on the radiating patch to realize dual resonances. The proposed design achieves resonant frequencies at 33.8 GHz and 45.53 GHz with reflection coefficients of -36.39 dB and -42.68 dB, respectively. It achieves fractional bandwidths of 6.78% and 9.14%, and peak gains of 4.59 dBi and 7.32 dBi. Radiation efficiencies of 76.2% and 79.34% confirm its effectiveness for high-frequency operation. Equivalent RLC circuit validation further supports the dual-band characteristics. Owing to its wide bandwidth, reliable gain, and stable radiation performance, the proposed antenna is well-suited for integration into nextgeneration high-speed communication systems.

title: A Hybrid Machine Learning Framework for Early Detection and Heuristic Severity Prediction of Autism Spectrum Disorder Using Q-CHAT-10
authors: Md. Mahbubur Rahman, Md. Mehedi Hasan Shihab, Mohammad Mahfuzul Islam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Today's renowned problem Autism Spectrum Disorder is not any typical disease. It's totally developmental and neurological disorder. This problem typically appears in early childhood. If autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is diagnosed early, the treatment can be less painful for the kids which also helps them to lead a near to normal life. This study proposes a hybrid ensemble framework which combines Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and KNearest Neighbors (KNN) with a LightGBM meta-classifier for ASD classification. Also shows an arithmetic calculation based way for severity prediction. The model was validated through stratified train-test splits, 15-fold cross-validation, and external testing. Severity was categorized into None, Mild, Moderate and Severe based on Q-CHAT scores. Results show 99.51% internal and 97.44% external accuracy for binary classification. 100% accuracy for severity prediction using arithmetic operation (not clinically validated). The approach demonstrates feasibility for resource-constrained and community-level screening.

title: SDNet: Interpretable Transformer-Based Captioning for Yield-Aware Nanoscale Defect Diagnosis
authors: Ruyue Jing, Yibo Qiao, Yuening Luo, Yining Chen
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: An accurate and interpretable diagnosis of wafer-level defects is essential for yield improvement in semiconductor manufacturing. Current practices still rely on manual scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image inspection, while automated semantic captioning remains limited due to the lack of domain-specific datasets. We propose the Semiconductor Diagnosis Net (SDNet), a lightweight and interpretable vision-language framework for nanoscale SEM defect diagnosis. SDNet employs a Vision Transformer encoder with a GRU decoder, enhanced by spatial and channel attention, to generate precise diagnostic captions. To support this task, we introduce SSID, the first expert-annotated SEM defect captioning dataset with 4,425 images and 7,375 sentences from a 55nm production line, enabling structured and process-aware defect understanding. The validation of SDNet on the SSID dataset has been achieved with 99.1% Top-5 Accuracy (Top-5 Acc), BLEU-1 of 0.9488, BLEU-4 of 0.8894 and Batch Time of 0.039s, demonstrating SDNet features high precision, low latency, and visual interpretability, which can achieve the information connection between low-level checks and production perception reports, thereby meeting the real-time deployment needs of wafer fabrication plants.
