abstract: Mobile applications evolve rapidly, forcing development teams to deliver updates at high frequency while maintaining reliable testing processes. In this context, GUI testing is essential for validating user-facing behavior; however, it is highly affected by fragility, as tests break across application versions not due to functional defects, but rather because of changes in the GUI structure, appearance, or properties. This leads to substantial manual effort to diagnose failures and repair outdated tests. This study aims to: (i) identify the most common causes of mobile GUI test breakages across application versions; (ii) assess how Large Language Models (LLMs) can reduce the effort required for repairing broken tests; and (iii) compare an LLMbased repair strategy with a state-of-the-art automated repair tool, Healenium-Appium. A total of 61 broken GUI tests from 19 real-world Android applications were analyzed to identify the underlying causes of breakage. We then developed an LLM-based repair approach, in which the model and user iteratively review test failures and progressively update test scripts until the repaired test passes. The approach was experimentally evaluated against HealeniumAppium. The LLM-based method successfully repaired 45 out of 61 tests <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(73.8 \%)$</tex> after a single interaction and 56 out of 61 tests (91.8 %) after multiple interactions, outperforming Healenium-Appium by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0. 8 \%}$</tex> and 68.8%, respectively. These results show that LLM-based repair effectively mitigates GUI test fragility, reducing maintenance effort while achieving higher repair success rates than existing automated tools.

title: Grammar-Aware Coverage-Guided Fuzzing with Grammarinator and AFL++
authors: Renáta Hodován, Ákos Kiss
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: In recent years, program code has been produced at an unprecedented pace, particularly following the rapid adoption of generative AI technologies. Despite this increase in volume, software systems must still be tested with the same level of rigor as before. Consequently, software testing requires an increasing degree of automation. Among automated approaches, randomized test generation—commonly referred to as fuzzing—has demonstrated strong bug-finding capabilities for many years. Nowadays, the majority of fuzzing approaches are based either on coverage-guided techniques that mutate existing test cases or on grammar-driven generation methods. While early coverageguided methods relied on relatively simple metrics and bruteforce mutation strategies to uncover numerous defects, grammarbased generators have gained significant popularity due to their ability to produce syntactically valid and complex inputs. However, these grammar-driven approaches typically cannot reuse existing test cases. Grammarinator is a grammar-based test generator capable of both generating and parsing, and thus also mutating, test cases using ANTLR grammars. This work integrates Grammarinator into one of the most widely used fuzzing frameworks, AFL++, and extends it with several additional features to further improve effectiveness. Experimental results show that the integrated approach achieves significantly higher coverage and discovers more bugs than state-of-the-art fuzzers.

title: From Threat Reports to Security Knowledge: Building an LLM-Based Pipeline for AI Systems
authors: Takuma Tsuchida, Yuya Fujiwara, Hironori Washizaki, Naoyasu Ubayashi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering - Companion (SANER-C)
abstract: Building structured and reusable security knowledge from unstructured threat reports is a challenging and predominantly manual task in the AI security domain. Although existing knowledgebases systematically organize adversarial threats, their reliance on manual curation by security experts limits scalability and responsiveness to new threats. Automating the structuring and maintenance of such knowledgebases not only improves scalability but also bridges the gap between threat intelligence and software engineering practices, facilitating the secure-by-design development of AI systems. We propose a pipeline based on a large language model that automatically extracts, refines, and classifies threat actions from incident reports into structured security knowledge. Our pipeline encompasses four critical phases: (1) threat-action extraction, (2) quality-guided refinement, (3) mapping to an established threat classification framework, and (4) structured knowledge construction with metadata generation. Evaluation shows 0.85 precision in extraction, with refinement further enhancing coverage. The classification phase effectively maps actions to known threat patterns while also identifying potentially novel threats that have yet to be documented, supporting the continuous evolution of the knowledgebase. These results lay the groundwork for scalable, automated construction and maintenance of AI security knowledge.

title: Blockchain-Enabled Framework for Secure and Transparent Land Administration in Bangladesh
authors: Ridwan Hamid Khan, Syed Sadman Salahuddin
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Current land administration systems mainly rely on fragmented, paper-based processes that hinder transparency, slow service delivery, and increase the risk of disputes. This paper proposes a secure and transparent land administration framework tailored to the operational context of Bangladesh. The design integrates a permissioned blockchain ledger, digital identity binding, smart contracts to automate core workflows (registration, sale, lease, mortgage, and recovery), and off-chain document storage with cryptographic anchoring to balance privacy and auditability. We present a prototype implementation using Hyperledger Fabric, Go chaincode, a Node.js middleware, and IPFS for document storage. Through controlled experiments we measure resource utilization, throughput, end-to-end latency, and IPFS cache behavior to evaluate functional correctness and runtime performance. The results demonstrate predictable operation under moderate to high loads while revealing CPU and off-chain interaction bottlenecks that motivate targeted optimizations. In general, the findings confirm the feasibility of the proposed system and highlight its potential to enhance transparency, security, and efficiency in land management.

title: Location Allocation for Parcel Lockers with P-Median Model: A Case Study from Türkiye
authors: Elifnaz Olgac, Ali Karasan, Ihsan Kaya
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: As delivery technologies have advanced, novel last mile delivery (LMD) channels have been emerged in recent years. Among these channels, parcel lockers stand out, as they reduce the number of failed delivery attempts in addition to offering cost-effective and efficient delivery experience. Location selection is a key concept for establishing a parcel locker network, as it directly influences ability of the demand coverage, required initial investment and compliance to any additional constraints. For this reason, the location allocation problem for parcel lockers should be addressed with a systematic and quantitative methodology. This study focuses on parcel locker location selection for Beşiktaş, a district of İstanbul, Türkiye which is densely populated and presents highly challenging urban conditions. Within this context, parcel locker allocation problem is solved with a modified p-median model, while the demand points are obtained from geographical information systems and the candidate service points are identified through k -means clustering algorithm. The results obtained provide a fundamental basis for establishing a parcel locker system in Beşiktaş. Moreover, traditional capacitated p-median model is enhanced with additional constraints to satisfy the demand coverage, providing a more effective initial solution than the traditional model.

title: Multi-Regime GRU-Scheduled PID Control for Greenhouse VPD Regulation with Real-Time PLC Deployment
authors: Natthanan Wanna, Kriengkrai Nabudda, Suphasit Sitthaphanit, Natakorn Thasnas
abstract: Vapor pressure deficit is a physiologically meaningful climate variable governing plant transpiration and water transport in greenhouse cultivation. However, accurate regulation in evaporative-cooled greenhouses is challenging due to nonlinear temperature–humidity coupling, time-varying dynamics, and rapidly changing solar disturbances. Conventional fixed-gain proportional–integral–derivative controllers, tuned for a single operating condition, often exhibit performance degradation under regime transitions. This study proposes a multi-regime admissible-region-constrained gain-scheduled control framework in which a gated recurrent unit network serves as a supervisory scheduler to generate smooth, near-optimal controller gains for real-time vapor pressure deficit regulation. Control-oriented first-order-plus-dead-time models are identified under representative operating regimes, and an interval-bounded plant ensemble is constructed to capture dynamic variability. Regime-wise optimal gains are obtained offline using hybrid evolutionary optimization and used as supervisory training labels. To support safe deployment, all scheduled gains are constrained within a pre-validated admissible stability region, such that each deployed gain configuration remains inside an offline-validated frozen-time stabilizing set. The controller is implemented on an industrial programmable logic controller and experimentally validated in a full-scale evaporative greenhouse under real environmental disturbances. Comparative multi-day experiments demonstrate improved tracking accuracy, reduced overshoot, smoother actuator behavior, and enhanced disturbance attenuation relative to fixed-gain strategies, while maintaining deterministic real-time execution. The results establish an industrially deployable learning-based gain-scheduling framework for practical greenhouse climate control.

title: Remote Sensing for Understanding the Vertical Dimension of Urban Landscapes: A review on data, products, methods, and prospects
authors: Haiming Qin, Weiqi Zhou, Zhe Zhu, Qihao Weng
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Magazine
abstract: Quantifying the vertical structure of urban landscapes is the prerequisite to understand its social and ecological impacts. Remote sensing provides an important tool for data acquisition and pattern analysis on the vertical structure of urban landscapes. Therefore, a comprehensive review of how remote sensing can facilitate the quantification of the vertical structure of urban landscapes is not only desirable for the remote sensing community but also for the increasing number of scholars who are broadly interested in 3D related issues. Here, we present a comprehensive review of the current statuses of data, products, methods, and applications for quantifying the vertical dimension of urban landscapes using remote sensing. We further discussed the challenges now facing and future research needed. Specifically, we first reviewed and compared the remote sensing data that could be used to extract the vertical information of urban landscapes, including passive optical data, LiDAR data, SAR data, and street view imagery. We then presented a detailed list of the digital surface model (DSM) and ground object height products derived from remotely sensed data that are readily used for quantifying the vertical structure of urban landscapes, ranging from global-scale products to local ones with finer resolution. We also reviewed key approaches for urban vertical structure analysis, which included urban land cover classification, analysis of the vertical structure of urban architectures, greenspace, and comprehensive landscapes, as well as analysis of the dynamics of urban vertical structure. Additionally, we synthesized applications of urban vertical structure in six core fields: urban heat islands and microclimates, disaster risks, biodiversity, energy and resource consumption, human settlements and social equity, and urban management and planning. Finally, we discussed current challenges and future opportunities, such as limitations and tradeoffs of remote sensing technologies for vertical analysis, extracting vertical information by fusing multi-source remote sensing data, deep learning for high-resolution and high-quality DSM generation, establishing a comprehensive index system for urban vertical structure quantification, monitoring the dynamics of 3D urban landscapes based on long-time-series data, exploring the human perception of 3D urban landscapes, and addressing uncertainty and robustness. To our knowledge, this paper is the first work that comprehensively integrates these elements around the urban vertical structure. It provides abundant information on the quantification of the vertical structure of urban landscapes using remote sensing and offers valuable insights for the remote sensing and 3D research community.

title: Organized Point Cloud Correspondence and Adaptive Weighting for Rotation-Robust LiDAR Odometry
authors: JunHyeong Jo, Chang-bae Moon
abstract: LiDAR odometry is pivotal for autonomous navigation, enabling precise position and orientation estimation through consecutive LiDAR scan analysis. However, existing methods encounter significant challenges, including accumulated drift and performance degradation during rapid yaw rotations. These limitations primarily stem from the reliance of conventional Iterative Closest Point (ICP) methods solely on geometric environmental features, which become unreliable during abrupt directional changes. To address this, we propose Yaw Rotation-Robust ICP (YR-ICP), a novel approach designed to enhance rotational stability. YR-ICP leverages the grid-based arrangement and indexing properties of organized point clouds to refine correspondence accuracy. Furthermore, we introduce an adaptive weighting scheme that dynamically balances geometry-based and organized point cloud-based associations. This hybrid approach ensures high accuracy even when geometric features are sparse due to rapid motion. Experimental evaluations on benchmark datasets demonstrate that YR-ICP achieves a 34% improvement in Absolute Pose Error (APE) compared to KISS-ICP. Moreover, in complex corridor environments requiring frequent rotational maneuvers, the proposed method exhibits superior consistency, showing a threefold reduction in the standard deviation of error compared to conventional methods. These results substantiate the effectiveness of YR-ICP in challenging real-world scenarios.

title: STAR-RIS Aided Fluid Antenna System: A Joint Optimization with Discrete Positions and Phases
authors: Rui Shi, Haisu Wu, Hong Ren, Cunhua Pan, Jiaju Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In this paper, we investigate a simultaneously transmitting and reflecting reconfigurable intelligent surface (STAR-RIS)-aided fluid antenna downlink communication system, where the antennas’ locations are limited to discrete positions. Specifically, the beamforming and the positions of fluid antennas at the base station (BS), the transmission and reflection coefficients of the STAR-RIS are jointly optimized to maximize the minimum signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) of the users, while satisfying the transmit power constraint at the BS and the discrete phase constraint at the STAR-RIS. To address the non-convex optimization problem, an alternating optimization (AO) algorithm is proposed. Within this framework, the non-convex SINR constraints for the BS beamforming subproblem are tackled using the successive convex approximation (SCA), while the STAR-RIS coefficients are optimized via a penalty-based method. Furthermore, to handle the discrete antenna position variables, an iterative approach based on the McCormick envelope technique is developed to find the optimal locations. Numerical results are provided to confirm the effectiveness of our proposed algorithm.

title: FaceLiVTv2: An Improved Hybrid Architecture for Efficient Mobile Face Recognition_supp1-3690540.pdf
authors: Chi-Chia Sun
abstract: Lightweight face recognition is increasingly important for deployment on edge and mobile devices, where strict constraints on latency, memory, and energy consumption must be met alongside reliable accuracy. Although recent hybrid CNN-Transformer architectures have advanced global context modeling, striking an effective balance between recognition performance and computational efficiency remains an open challenge. In this work, we present FaceLiVTv2, an improved version of our FaceLiVT hybrid architecture designed for efficient global–local feature interaction in mobile face recognition. At its core is Lite MHLA, a lightweight global token interaction module that replaces the original multi-layer attention design with multi-head linear token projections and affine rescale transformations, reducing redundancy while preserving representational diversity across heads. We further integrate Lite MHLA into a unified RepMix block that coordinates local and global feature interactions and adopts global depthwise convolution for adaptive spatial aggregation in the embedding stage. Under our experimental setup, results on LFW, CA-LFW, CP-LFW, CFP-FP, AgeDB-30, and IJB show that FaceLiVTv2 consistently improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off over existing lightweight methods. Notably, FaceLiVTv2 reduces mobile inference latency by 22% relative to FaceLiVTv1, achieves speedups of up to 30.8% over GhostFaceNets on mobile devices, and delivers 20-41% latency improvements over EdgeFace and KANFace across platforms while maintaining higher recognition accuracy. These results demonstrate that FaceLiVTv2 offers a practical and deployable solution for real-time face recognition. Code is available at https://github.com/novendrastywn/FaceLiVT.

title: Interference Sparsity-Aware Turbo Receiver for HF Skywave Massive MIMO
authors: Linfeng Song, Rui Sun, Ding Shi, Yanbo Yu, An-An Lu, Xiqi Gao, Geoffrey Ye Li, Xiang-Gen Xia
abstract: In this paper, we propose a low complexity turbo receiver for high frequency (HF) skywave massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. We first introduce the beam based channel model (BBCM) with uniform sampling for directional cosine. By leveraging the BBCM, we reveal the interference sparsity of HF skywave massive MIMO systems, which is defined as the asymptotic sparsity of the channel Gram matrix. Exploiting the interference sparsity, we provide a condition of extracting sufficient observation for signal detection. Motivated by this condition, we construct the interference user terminal (UT) set (IUS) and extract the observation vector from the received signal after matched filtering (MF) for each UT. Then, a low-dimensional interference sparsity-aware detector (ISD) is separately designed for each UT by minimizing the mean-squared error (MSE), and the interference sparsity-aware turbo receiver (ISTR) is subsequently formulated using ISDs. Under a relaxed version of the condition for sufficient observation selection, we prove the optimality of the ISTR. Further, we develop an efficient implementation of the ISTR, involving approximate computation of the ISD, the signal reconstructed by ISD and the channel Gram matrix. Moreover, an efficient construction of IUS using the statistical channel state information (CSI) is also proposed. Simulation results confirm that the proposed ISTR achieves excellent performance with relatively low complexity.

title: Monitoring-Based Prescribed Performance Control for Nonlinear Systems Against Actuator Faults
authors: Teng-Fei Fang, Huimin Wang, Guang-Hong Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This article investigates the prescribed performance control (PPC) problem for nonlinear systems subject to various degrees of actuator faults. The critical challenges lie in identifying performance deviations and achieving post-fault performance recovery. To address these issues, a performance-based monitoring strategy is first introduced, followed by the construction of a mapping function. Subsequently, by integrating these mechanisms with the actuator redundancy, a unified control scheme is presented, which is capable of handling both the degradation fault and the extreme fault effectively. Compared with the existing solutions, the proposed control scheme exhibits two distinctive features: 1) by incorporating shifting functions, it enables PPC for nonlinear systems independent of initial states; and 2) following the detection of the extreme fault and actuator switching, it facilitates accurate performance recovery within a prescribed time, while reducing dependence on the post-fault information. Finally, comparative simulations are conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Hybrid-Polarity Logic Design and An Automated Algorithm for Glitch-Optimized Arithmetic Units
authors: Yuxuan Du, Zhangrui Qian, Tuo Li, Changhong Wang, Zhihao Yan, Kaize Zhou, Zhuo Chen, Jun Yin, Yan Lu, Weiwei Shan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems II: Express Briefs
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) processors and Digital Signal Processing (DSP) chips require high-efficiency arithmetic units, while glitch-induced power has become a major obstacle to further efficiency improvement. To address this, a hybrid-polarity logic design method and an automated algorithm are proposed for low-power and glitch-optimized arithmetic units. The output inverters of full/half adders are removed to eliminate redundant switching and alleviate path imbalance. The logical duality of these inverted cells is exploited to enable both conventional logic (positive polarity) and its inverted form (negative polarity) to coexist and hybrid-propagate in arithmetic units. Then, a glitch-analysis-based inverter re-insertion strategy is used to ensure logic correctness with minimal power cost. The automated algorithm merges partial products from complex computations and explores hybrid-polarity circuit netlists with optimized power efficiency. Simulated in a 28 nm CMOS process, the proposed unsigned and signed multipliers achieve up to 54.40% and 55.92% power gains, 13.67% and 11.43% delay reduction, respectively, compared with their corresponding Design Compiler-generated baselines. When applied to dot-product modules in an AI accelerator, our design achieves up to 57.87% power reduction. The results verify the effectiveness of our hybrid-polarity design in mitigating glitches and enhancing efficiency for large-scale digital systems.

title: Metasurfaces-Integrated Wireless Neural Networks for Lightweight Over-The-Air Edge Inference
authors: Kyriakos Stylianopoulos, Mario Edoardo Pandolfo, Paolo Di Lorenzo, George C. Alexandropoulos
abstract: The upcoming sixth Generation (6G) of wireless networks envisions ultra-low latency and energy efficient Edge Inference (EI) for diverse Internet of Things (IoT) applications. However, traditional digital hardware for machine learning is power intensive, motivating the need for alternative computation paradigms. Over-The-Air (OTA) computation is regarded as an emerging transformative approach assigning the wireless channel to actively perform computational tasks. This article introduces the concept of Metasurfaces-Integrated Neural Networks (MINNs), a physical-layer-enabled deep learning framework that leverages programmable multi-layer metasurface structures and Multiple-Input Multiple-Output (MIMO) channels to realize computational layers in the wave propagation domain. The MINN system is conceptualized as three modules: Encoder, Channel (uncontrollable propagation features and metasurfaces), and Decoder. The first and last modules, realized respectively at the multi-antenna transmitter and receiver, consist of conventional digital or purposely designed analog Deep Neural Network (DNN) layers, and the metasurfaces responses of the Channel module are optimized alongside all modules as trainable weights. This architecture enables computation offloading into the end-to-end physical layer, flexibly among its constituent modules, achieving performance comparable to fully digital DNNs while significantly reducing power consumption. The training of the MINN framework, two representative variations, and performance results for indicative applications are presented, highlighting the potential of MINNs as a lightweight and sustainable solution for future EI-enabled wireless systems. The article is concluded with a list of open challenges and promising research directions.

title: Acceptance of Artificial Intelligence and Future Italian Physicians’ Perspectives: a cross-sectional descriptive survey
authors: Valentina De Nicolò, Davide La Torre, Ignazio Iacone
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly shaping clinical reasoning and healthcare delivery, yet evidence on physicians’ acceptance, trust, and ethical expectations remains limited. This nationwide cross-sectional study investigated the perceptions of 293 Italian physicians, the majority of whom were hospital-based specialists, regarding the integration of AI into clinical practice. Although 78.5% had previously used AI tools, only 4.8% had received formal training, revealing a significant educational gap. Respondents expressed a cautiously optimistic attitude toward AI, particularly recognizing its potential to enhance diagnostic accuracy and support therapeutic decision-making, while showing moderate agreement on its ability to reduce workload. Persistent reservations were linked to limited trust in autonomous AI outputs and fears of professional displacement. Factor analysis identified three clusters influencing acceptance: primary facilitators such as usability and clinical relevance; secondary enablers including trustworthiness, safety, transparency, and privacy protection; and minor inhibitors related to insufficient expertise, limited peer endorsement, and perceived loss of autonomy. Ethical expectations were notably high, with strong agreement on the need for defined medico-legal responsibilities, independent ethical evaluation, and validation of clinical reliability. Participants also emphasized the importance of equity in AI deployment, regulatory harmonization, and targeted professional education. Overall, Italian physicians appear open to the adoption of AI in medicine, provided that human oversight, fairness, and trust remain central. Bridging the current gaps in training, governance, and ethical regulation is essential to foster a healthcare environment in which AI complements—rather than replaces—human clinical judgement.

title: Deep Learning-Based Blood Cell Classification for Cancer Detection Using ResNet50
authors: Poonam Sharma, Priyanka Nanda, Meenakshi Sharma, Shikha Verma, Lakshay Kheterpal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This research focuses on ResNet50, a deep learning algorithm used for the classification of blood cells automatically, with the types including Basophil, Erythroblast, Monocyte, Myeloblast, and Seg_Neutrophil. The high-resolution 5000 images present in the used dataset belong to Kaggle and were pre-processed using resizing, normalization, and augmentation. Twenty epochs of training were done using the Adam optimizer and binary cross-entropy loss. The evaluation provided a total accuracy of 86% and precision, recall, and F1-scores of over 0.85 for each class. The number of misclassified results from Erythroblast to Monocyte and vice versa was very few due to overlapping morphological features in the confusion matrix. Analysis of the training and validation losses revealed stable learning with little over-fitting. The research establishes that deep learning can successfully automate the classification of blood cells and has potential in speeding up the process of manual diagnosis. Future improvement can be made with ensemble learning, more complete data collection, and integration in real-time with the clinical setting to improve the generalizability and explainability of the model, and make cancer detection with AI more reliable and accessible.

title: Urban Sustainability in Bangladesh: Satellite-Based Analysis of Land Use and Air Pollution Trends in Major Bangladeshi Cities
authors: Md Saib Hossain, Md Maynul Hossen, Shahariar Sarkar, Tasmin Sanjida, Iftekharul Abedeen
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Google Earth Engine (GEE) is the most powerful and open-source geospatial big data analysis platform. The study analyzes Land Use and Land Cover (LULC) dynamics from 2019 to 2025 across three major growing cities of Bangladesh - Dhaka, Khulna, and Rajshahi using the Sentinel-2 imageries of 2024. Three satellite indices such as the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), Normalized Difference Built-up Index (NDBI), and Normalized Difference Water Index (NDWI) are used to detect vegetation, water bodies, build-up, and barren land, which were classified by applying six machine learning(ML) algorithms like Random Forest (RF), Classification and Regression Tree (CART), K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LightGBM), and Naive Bayes (NB). The accuracy assessment showed that RF achieved the highest accuracies <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(99 \%, 99.3 \%$</tex>, 99.1%) across the study areas (Dhaka, Khulna, and Rajshahi), while Naive Bayes performed poorly in land classes. Additionally, time-series analysis revealed declining vegetation and water bodies (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.4 \%, 5 \%$</tex>) with increasing built-up and barren land (50%, 19%) in Dhaka, indicating urbanization pressures. Together, they are combined with air pollution analysis <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\left(\text{NO}_{2}, \text{SO}, \text{CO}, \mathrm{O}_{3}\right.$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{CH}_{4}$</tex>) to capture the impacts of urbanization on environmental quality. Moreover, this study can help in achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG), particularly SDG 6 (Clean Water and Sanitation), SDG 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), SDG 13 (Climate Action), and SDG 15 (Life on Land), by providing critical insights to balance urban growth with environmental sustainability.

title: Advancements in Image Captioning: A Comprehensive Survey on Techniques, Modalities, and Applications
authors: Ashokkumar Janarthanan, Arjun Paramarthalingam, Shanmugam Sundaramurthy, Yokeshvaran S
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Recent advances in image captioning come from deep learning methods. These methods connect visual understanding with natural language generation. Early CNN– RNN models had some success, but they struggled with making sense and reasoning in context. To fix these issues, researchers added multi-attention mechanisms, hierarchical designs, and multimodal pre-training. These changes greatly improve caption quality. In medicine, models like MedViLL use BERT designs to improve performance on various tasks. Also, using patient background information shows the value of specific medical knowledge. Generative adversarial and knowledge-enhanced frameworks, like RAGAN and EURAIC, improve feature representation. Also, fine-grained techniques such as ASP help achieve a deeper semantic understanding. This paper highlights recent developments that show the need to combine vision and language models. It shows we need more research on common-sense reasoning, fairness, and real-time captioning. These areas will help create more human-like and context-aware image descriptions.

title: Towards Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Robotic Manipulators Through Multi-Sensor Fusion with Machine Learning
authors: Khandokar Sakhawat Hossen, Sumaiya Kabir Shashin, Tawfiq Mahmud Khan, Ariful Islam Riad
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Anomalies within robotic applications can arise from hardware faults, environmental changes or unexpected operational deviations. Current research in this field has typically employed single-sensor monitoring or computationally intensive deep learning approaches, limiting the scalability of resourceconstrained low-power embedded systems in the industrial arena. To provide a more efficient and affordable solution in anomalous system detection, this study propose a multi-sensor fusion framework that combines multiple data streams like current, vibration, and temperature modalities with supervised machine learning models. The experimental dataset was collected from a 4DOF hobby servo-based robotic manipulator, where the proposed models were trained and validated for anomaly classification. A comparative evaluation of multiple classifiers is conducted, where the Random Forest (rf) model achieves the best performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8 \%}$</tex> accuracy and an f1-score of 97. The experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach provides a balance between computational complexity and detection reliability, making it well-suited for real-time application in industrial robotic systems.

title: An Integrated Socio-Technical Framework for Antifragile Project Organizations: A New Paradigm for Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
authors: Aawag Mohsen Alawag, Basheer M. Al-Ghazali, Saleem Ahmed Al-Azazi, Mohammed Abdulrazzaq Alaghbari, Fares Mohammed Shabaan, Maryam Althawadi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: In an era defined by persistent volatility, traditional project management paradigms focused on robustness and resilience prove insufficient. This paper introduces the concept of an anti-fragility system's ability to benefit from and improve in response to stressors and uncertainty as a necessary evolution for project-based organizations. We propose a novel Integrated Socio-Technical Framework that operationalizes antifragility by synthesizing principles from Complex Adaptive Systems (CAS), High-Reliability Organizations (HROs), and Socio-Technical Systems (STS) theory. The framework is built upon the joint optimization of two interdependent pillars: a 'social' system (comprising adaptive leadership, an antifragile culture, and strategic HRM) and a 'technical' system (including adaptive project management and digital enablers like the Digital Twin). By integrating these systems, organizations can develop the dynamic capabilities and organizational ambidexterity necessary to not only survive but also thrive in the face of disruption. This conceptual paper provides a practical roadmap for shifting from a process-centric focus to a value-delivery model, enabling project organizations to build a sustainable competitive advantage in an increasingly unpredictable world.

title: Design and Analysis of Button Shaped Microstrip Patch Antenna for Wireless Capsule Endoscopy in Biotelemetry
authors: Sumaya Sraboni, Fateha Samad
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper is designed based on a unique microstrip patch antenna for wireless capsule endoscopy (WCE). The dimension of the proposed antenna is <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20 \times 25 \times 0.9 \text{mm}^{3}$</tex> where the selected material of the substrate is FR-4 (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\varepsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=4.3$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\delta=0.025$</tex>). The patch of this antenna is prepared by the copper material. Moreover, a partial modified ground plane has been designed on the substrate. For WCE, a capsule is introduced including the dimension of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$26 \times 11 \text{mm}^{2}$</tex>. In this work, the capsule is made of polyimide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}=3.5$</tex>) material. CST Studio Suite software is used for designing and analyzing the antenna for free space, into the capsule shell, and inside phantom model. The proposed antenna gives resonant frequency of 5.576 GHz with - 10 dB, for the bandwidth of 3 GHz where the range is from 5.5 GHz to 6 GHz. For this antenna, omnidirectional radiation pattern has been observed and it shows circular polarization inside the phantom model. In addition, the maximum radiation efficiency is found as −28.4 dB. The specific absorption rate (SAR) in the gastrointestinal tract is also measured to ensure that it does not harm any body tissues during the operation.

title: AI-Based Flight Trajectory Optimization Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Achyutha S Nijigal, Balasubramanyam B S, Sripad Kulkarni S
abstract: Flight trajectory optimization is a major challenge in modern aviation due to increasing air traffic density, rising fuel costs, and strict operational constraints. Traditional trajectory planning approaches rely on static optimization techniques that are unable to adapt effectively to dynamic airspace environments. In this paper, an AI-based flight trajectory planning framework is proposed using Reinforcement Learning (RL). The trajectory optimization problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process where multiple RL agents are trained with different reward functions to optimize fuel consumption, arrival delay, and a balanced objective combining both. Experimental results show that the balanced agent achieves an effective trade-off between fuel efficiency and delay reduction. The proposed framework demonstrates the potential of reinforcement learning for adaptive and intelligent air traffic management systems.

title: Hierarchical Vision-Controlled Quadruped Robot for Autonomous Human Detection and Localization in Disaster Response
authors: Faiaz Ahsan, Najeeb Ahmed Bhuiyan, Md. Al Tas Did Ul, Md. Jubair Ahamed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Search-and-rescue (SAR) operations in collapsed buildings and rubble-strewn environments pose significant challenges for autonomous systems, where wheeled and aerial robots often fail due to limited mobility or sensing constraints. To address this gap, we present a compact spider-inspired quadruped robot designed for confined-space SAR. The robot combines multi-degree-of-freedom (DOF) legged locomotion with onboard computer vision and Global Positioning System (GPS)-based reporting. A hierarchical perception-control pipeline integrates real-time human detection and tracking using a lightweight object detection model on a high-level device with gait execution through inverse kinematics on a low-level device. The system autonomously alternates between search and track states, enabling both exploratory coverage and persistent victim following in cluttered spaces. Upon target lock, GPS localization is employed to provide situational awareness to rescuers. Experiments conducted demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of integrating adaptable legged mobility, vision-guided detection, and localization into a lightweight robotic platform. The proposed approach establishes a practical foundation for deploying quadruped robots as ground assets in disaster response missions.

title: Enhanced Grid Integration of PV, Wind, and Battery Systems: Leveraging UPQC and Intelligent Control for Power Quality Improvement
authors: Md Sharif Robbani, Bappaditta Saha Chowdhury, Md Khayruzzaman Abhi, Md Mobarok Hosen Omi, Afrin Tanzila Rabbani, Samiul Ahasan Sajid
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper presents a new approach that would improve the quality of power in grid-connected hybrid renewable energy systems, which combine photovoltaic (PV), wind, and battery storage systems. The increasing infiltration of renewable generation into the electrical transmission system has brought about various complications such as voltage instability, harmonic distortion and fluctuation of the current which negatively affect grid efficiency and reliability. The paper suggests implementing a Unified Power Quality Conditioner (UPQC) to reduce these challenges with the use of more sophisticated control measures, including an Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS), Proportional-Integral (PI) control, and fuzzy-logic controllers. The UPQC uses both shunt and series inverter to prevent disturbance by current and irregularities in voltage. In addition, both PV and wind subsystems have Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) techniques (i.e., Perturb-and-Observe or P&O) techniques that are used to optimize the power recovery. The results of the simulation support the effectiveness of the given scheme where the Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) was reduced to 8.02per cent and the effective stabilization of the voltage was achieved during sag and swell conditions. The ANFIS-based controller has better voltage regulation as compared to the conventional PI and fuzzy controllers, however, it has a greater THD. The presented results highlight the promise of the proposed approach towards further development of power quality, system stability, and enabling a more reliable introduction of renewable energy sources into the grid.

title: Generative AI Meets Responsible AI and Affective Computing
authors: Kamran Hassan Shomrat, Gazi Arman Islam, Atkea Fauzia Eva, Md. Saiful Islam, Jamilatun Subarna, Anika Tahsin, Md. Golam Rabiul Alam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly expected to interpret and respond to human emotions in real time, yet most existing solutions remain limited to unimodal or ethically unregulated approaches. In this research, we propose a unified, multimodal emotion recognition (MER) framework that integrates Generative AI (GenAI) and Responsible AI (RAI) with Affective Computing to enhance human-computer emotional interactions. We explore real-time emotion recognition and response by analyzing image, speech, and text, while also incorporating user gestures and behavioral signals. We construct a custom dataset of 1000 annotated video samples spanning seven emotion classes. We built our proposed approach using transformer-based models, including DistilRoBERTa, fine-tuned Wav2Vec2, and DeepFace to process text for sentiment classification, audio for emotion detection, and facial image modalities for facial expression analysis. A majority-vote fusion strategy combines the outputs of these models to identify dominant emotional states and gradually learns from user feedback, adapting to behave more human-like and empathetic. Additionally, our system also incorporates RAI layers to ensure ethical safeguards through bias and threat detection. Finally, a FLAN-T5-based generative module produces natural language summaries that reflect both the emotional content and ethical assessments. Our proposed method achieves an overall accuracy of 81%, with F1-scores of 0.94 and 0.90 for anger and disgust, respectively. Our approach suggests the potential for significant improvements beyond unimodal baselines, enabling ethically aware and emotionally intelligent applications such as virtual assistants, mental health support systems, and emotion-adaptive learning platforms.

title: Enhancing Network Security with a Two-Layered Machine Learning-Based Intrusion Detection Framework
authors: Zakia Sultana, Md. Saiful Islam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) face significant exposure to cyber-attacks because of their limited processing capabilities and distributed operating environment. Existing intrusion detection systems (IDS) typically offer either lightweight anomaly detection at the edge or depend on cloud based deep learning models that require substantial computational resources, which restricts their suitability for real-time use and for handling multiple attack categories. This study focuses on developing a hybrid IDS that can deliver fast detection at the edge while also achieving reliable multi-class attack classification in the cloud, all with reduced computational overhead. The proposed design includes a feedforward neural network at the edge for binary classification (Normal vs. Intrusion) and an encoder decoder model in the cloud for identifying different attack types, namely Flooding, TDMA, Grayhole, and Blackhole. Experiments conducted on the WSN-DS dataset show an overall accuracy of 99.57%, with macro-average precision, recall, and F1-score all above 0.94. The results indicate that the system is scalable, energy efficient, and well suited for real-time WSN applications that require both speed and high detection accuracy.

title: Malaria Detection from RBC Images Using Multi-Functional CNN with Explainable AI
authors: Md. Mahatab Sakib, Md. Omaer Goni, Md. Monjor Morshed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Deep Learning techniques are widely implemented to extract various features from image data. To detect malaria parasites from Red Blood Cell images we can leverage this technology. Several prior studies relied on generic CNNs and neighboring models to capture mainstream features from RBC. Whereas some works omitted attention mechanisms and interpretability. This led to the need for a specific architecture to integrate most of the important abilities of machine learning models i.e., decision explainability, accuracy enhancement by fine-tuning, omitting redundant data etc. through a novel framework. The primary objective is to capture multi-scale features from image data while keeping less complexity that uses multi-branch CNN architecture based on truncated ResNet18 & evaluate its performance using crossvalidation. Neighboring models such as plain ResNet18, CNN and ResNet50 lag in confirming multi-scale reasoning and attention mechanisms, vital for detecting a parasite in a cell. Total 26,161 accurate image samples were split into 80% for 5-fold training & 20% for testing over them. The proposed model reached average test accuracy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$99.61 \% \pm 0.06 \%$</tex> and a nearby precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex> score. A clinician can verify the model's logic by help of Grad-CAM as Explainable AI which generates heatmaps over the most important regions influencing the model's decision. It also offers a compact model consisting of 1.08 million parameters that requires a smaller number of resources. Socially, this automated model ought to fasten treatment in regions with scarce resources, thereby supporting global disease control goals.

title: Detecting AI-Generated Paraphrases in Bengali: A Comparative Study of Zero-Shot and Fine-Tuned Transformers
authors: Md. Rakibul Islam, Most. Sharmin Sultana Samu, Md. Zahid Hossain, Farhad Uz Zaman, Md. Kamrozzaman Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can produce text that closely resembles human writing. This capability raises concerns about misuse, including disinformation and content manipulation. Detecting AI-generated text is essential to maintain authenticity and prevent malicious applications. Existing research has addressed detection in multiple languages, but the Bengali language remains largely unexplored. Bengali's rich vocabulary and complex structure make distinguishing human-written and AI-generated text particularly challenging. This study investigates five transformer-based models: XLM-RoBERTa-Large, mDeBERTaV3-Base, BanglaBERT-Base, IndicBERT-Base and MultilingualBERT-Base. Zero-shot evaluation shows that all models perform near chance levels (around 50% accuracy) and highlight the need for task-specific fine-tuning. Fine-tuning significantly improves performance, with XLM-RoBERTa, mDeBERTa and MultilingualBERT achieving around 91% on both accuracy and F1-score. IndicBERT demonstrates comparatively weaker performance, indicating limited effectiveness in fine-tuning for this task. This work advances AI-generated text detection in Bengali and establishes a foundation for building robust systems to counter AI-generated content.

title: DynBrainNet: A Dynamic Lightweight Residual Network with Grad-CAM for Brain Tumor Classification
authors: Basharat Hussain, Basit Hussain, Saqib Hussain, Sania Zehra, Shah Faisal, Tasnimul Ferdous Tamim
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Brain tumor classification is crucial for prompt treatment and improved patient prognosis. Deep learning techniques have shown promising results in medical image classification; however, challenges remain in balancing the accuracy, computational efficiency, and model interpretability. This study proposes a novel lightweight deep learning architecture, DynBrainNet, designed for accurate and interpretable brain tumor classification using magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). DynBrainNet incorporates dynamic residual convolutional layers, Learned Group Convolutions (LGConv), early exit strategies, and hyperparameter optimization through grid search to achieve efficient and scalable performance. The model was assessed using a publicly accessible dataset consisting of 24,832 MRI images categorized into four categories: glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumor, and no tumor. DynBrainNet outperformed established transfer learning models, including ResNet50, InceptionV3, VGG16, EfficientNetV2B2, and Xception, achieving the highest classification accuracy of 95.3 % among the models tested. The Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (GradCAM) method was used to create heatmaps that clarify the model's predictions and can be used for clinical applications. The experimental results verified that the proposed DynBrainNet model provides a computationally efficient, interpretable, and robust framework for the diagnosis of brain tumors.

title: Design of a Cost-Effective Analog Solar Charge Controller Based on LM358 for Off-Grid Applications with Integrated Battery Protection
authors: Ratin Barua, Rakibul Hasan, Aditi Bhattacharjee, Mohammad Tazrian Taharin, Md. Imam Uddin Bhuiyan, Ratul Barua, Bivas Paul, Dolon Mutsuddi
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper presents the design, construction, and evaluation of a cost-effective analog solar charge controller optimized for off-grid photovoltaic (PV) systems. The controller employs the LM358 operational amplifier configured as a voltage comparator to regulate charging and discharging through relay actuation. Essential protections, including overvoltage cutoff, deep discharge prevention, and short-circuit isolation, are achieved using simple, reliable circuitry implemented on a custom PCB with cost-effective components. Experimental testing under real sunlight demonstrated stable charging performance, with a peak current of 3.5 A and effective regulation for a 12 V lead-acid battery. This work addresses the gap in providing an affordable, repairable alternative to available PWM and expensive controllers for mini standalone systems, especially for rural markets. To the best of our knowledge, it is among the first documented LM358-based analog Solar Charge Controllers (SCC) integrating multiple protections in one design. The unique application of LM358 in this context offers an accessible approach to solar energy management where simplicity, affordability, and durability are paramount.

title: XGception: Unveiling Mosquito Larvae Patterns with XAI
authors: Saif Mostasir Rohan, Sadman Samir Rafith, Md. Sayem Kabir, Maksudul Hasan Pranto, Touhid Bhuiyan, Kazi Tanvir
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Mosquito larvae, the aquatic life stage of mosquitoes, play a crucial role in the transmission of deadly diseases such as malaria, dengue, and Zika virus. Early detection and control of mosquito larvae are vital for effective disease management, yet traditional methods often struggle with inefficiencies, resistance, and limited scalability. Deep learning offers a promising alternative, but challenges such as limited annotated data, environmental variability, and class imbalance hinder many existing approaches. This study introduces a novel hybrid deep learning framework, combining the Xception model for feature extraction with XGBoost for classification. The architecture effectively captures both local discriminative features and complex relationships in mosquito larvae images. Evaluated on the MLMI-2024 dataset, comprising high-resolution microscopic images of Aedes aegypti and Culex quinquefasciatus larvae, the Xception-XGBoost model achieves state-of-the-art performance, attaining an accuracy of 99.33% and a Cohen's Kappa of 0.9883. Comparisons with existing models demonstrate its superior generalization capabilities and robustness across various mosquito species and body parts. This framework positions the Xception-XGBoost hybrid model as a scalable, non-invasive, and reliable AI-assisted solution for automated mosquito larvae detection, offering significant potential for enhancing vector surveillance and disease control efforts.

title: A Compensated Mini-Permeameter for Localized Magnetic Loss Measurements on Magnetic Laminations
authors: Olivier de la Barriére, Gulaly Khan, Carlo Stefano Ragusa, Frédéric Mazaleyrat, Luigi Solimene, Alessandro Prete
abstract: Accurate characterization of magnetic losses in permeameter-based systems is essential for reliable evaluation of magnetic materials. However, the magnetomotive force (MMF) drop in the flux-closing yokes can significantly affect the measurements, particularly in compact geometries where only a limited portion of the specimen is magnetized, leading to systematic overestimation of the losses. This paper presents a compensated mini-permeameter in which the MMF drop in the yokes is counteracted by an auxiliary winding driven by a feedback control scheme. The proposed approach enables the accurate determination of the magnetic field in the magnetized region directly from the magnetizing current. Experimental validation is performed through comparison with local tangential H-coil measurements on 0.35-mm-thick non-grain-oriented and 0.29-mm-thick grain-oriented laminations. Magnetic loss and hysteresis loop measurements at 50 Hz demonstrate that the compensation effectively mitigates systematic loss overestimation and enables close agreement with the reference method for both NGO and GO laminations. The proposed technique provides a compact and robust solution for localized magnetic loss characterization and can be extended to both standard and non-standard single-sheet testers.

title: Social Media Analytics for Sustainable Educational Decision-Making: A Case Study of TikTok’s Impact on Student Learning Behavior
authors: Majid Lateef Abdulrazaq, Ahmed Basil Suwaidan, Mustafa Maad Hamdi, Noor Raji Mohammed, Marwan Abdeldayem, Ibrahim Hamad Shabeeb, Saeed Hameed Aldulaimi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study aims to discover the reality of using the TikTok application by elementary, middle and high school students. A questionnaire was conducted to describe the reality of students’ use of the TikTok application (information, comments, and questions. The method used in this research is a quantitative method with a pre-experimental design. Therefore, this is often called a "quasy experiment. In this research, the researchers used a pretest and posttest approach with a non-equivalent control group design. This design requires two sample classes, namely the experimental class and the control class. The results indicate a double effect of gender and duration of use on the level of intellectual extremism, which is consistent with a number of recent studies. This study recommends increasing resources for raising awareness of the dangers of social networking sites, and methods for their optimal use for digital citizenship.

title: Low-Frequency Shock Dynamics in a Self-Gravitating Opposite-Polarity Complex Plasmas
authors: L. Rahman, S. Sultana, A. Mannan, A. A. Mamun
abstract: An investigation on the nonlinear dynamics of gravito-electrostatic dust-acoustic shock waves (DAShWs) propagating in a dusty plasma medium containing inertial positive and negative dust, as well as inertialess Maxwellian ions and electrons, has been carried out, incorporating external magnetic-field and dust-temperature effects. Adopting the reductive perturbation method, the Korteweg–de Vries–Burgers (KdVB) equation is derived to describe the nonlinear evolution of DAShWs. It is observed that the system supports stable propagation of dust-acoustic waves (DAWs) under gravity. The basic properties of DAShWs are found to be significantly affected by intrinsic plasma parameters, including the positive-to-negative dust charge, positive-to-negative dust density, negative-to-positive dust mass, dust temperatures, and the magnetic field, among others. A comparative analysis, considering and neglecting the self-gravitational field, is also presented to demonstrate the extent to which shock structures are modified by self-gravity. The results reported herein have potential implications for the characterization of the gravito-electrostatic dynamics associated with DAWs in laboratory plasmas and astrophysical environments (e.g., planetary rings and interstellar clouds).

title: Generative Rickshaw Art: Stylistic Fine-Tuning of Diffusion Models with Low-Rank Adaptation
authors: Susmita Mondal Sristi, Mohammad Shamsul Arefin
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Generative diffusion models such as Stable Diffusion have achieved remarkable success in producing high-quality images across diverse artistic domains. However, most existing applications remain centered on globally recognized styles, while regional folk traditions remain underrepresented. This work introduces Rickshaw Art, a vibrant Bangladeshi folk-art tradition, into diffusion-based generative pipelines through lightweight finetuning with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). A curated dataset of authentic Rickshaw Art paintings was used to adapt Stable Diffusion v1.5 and SDXL, enabling the selective transfer of stylistic motifs onto objects and scenes. Prompt engineering strategies were employed to control style placement, ensuring that elements such as garments, architecture, and decorative objects could be adorned in Rickshaw Art patterns without overwhelming entire scenes. Model performance was evaluated using both quantitative measures and qualitative human assessment, demonstrating strong stylistic fidelity and cultural authenticity. Beyond technical contributions, this study highlights the role of generative AI in cultural preservation, extending underexplored art forms into digital creative ecosystems. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to integrate Bangladeshi Rickshaw Art into diffusion-based generative modeling, contributing both cultural novelty and technical value.

title: DTKD: A Distance-Based Framework for Efficient Maintenance of Top-k Dominating Queries on Incomplete Data
authors: Kh. Khaleduzzaman, K. M. Azharul Hasan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Efficiently handling Top-K Dominance (TKD) queries on incomplete data is key for real-world applications where decisions rely on finding the best objects in large datasets with missing values. Traditional methods struggle because they rely heavily on pairwise comparisons, which increase complexity and lead to cyclic dominance problems, particularly with large sparse datasets. This often slows things down, reduces accuracy, and makes it difficult to manage large-scale datasets effectively. In this paper, we offer Distance-Based TKD (DTKD), a new framework that boosts both the speed and accuracy of TKD query processing. We use an Ideal Point to pick the Top-K dominant objects, with Euclidean Distance (ED) as a simple way to figure out the rankings. Unlike older approaches, DTKD skips pairwise comparisons, cutting down on computing effort and improving how well it scales with different data sizes and levels of sparsity. This work introduces a smart factor normalization technique that tackles the unevenness caused by missing data. This keeps distance calculations fair and consistent, even when some attributes are incomplete, ensuring steady and reliable results across various conditions. We tested this approach on real-world and synthetic datasets, showing that it saves a lot of processing time while still delivering accurate dominance outcomes. These findings highlight its potential for practical tools, such as decision support or recommender systems, where data gaps are a common challenge. The ability to handle diverse data scenarios makes the algorithm a promising solution for modern data-driven approaches, especially for incomplete data.

title: Multiphysics-Informed Adaptive Personalized Federated Learning for Battery State-of-Health Estimation
authors: Jiayin Xiao, Yujie Wang, Song Wang, Yin-Yi Soo, Zonghai Chen
abstract: Accurate state-of-health estimation is critical for the safe and reliable operation of Lithium-ion batteries, but developing robust data-driven models is often hindered by data privacy concerns and the distributed nature of the data. Federated learning (FL) offers a privacy-preserving solution, yet its performance is severely challenged by the statistical heterogeneity and data imbalance inherent in real-world distributed battery data. Furthermore, the opaque nature of conventional FL models impedes the cross-institutional trust required for collaborative training. To address these limitations, this article proposes a novel multiphysics-informed adaptive personalized federated learning (MP-APFL) framework. At the client level, we first develop a multiphysics-informed, multistage aging partial differential equation (PDE) that combines analytical models for key degradation mechanisms, such as solid electrolyte interphase formation, thermal effects, and lithium plating-induced “knee points,” with a data-driven term to account for complex unmodeled dynamics. Subsequently, this governing PDE is embedded as a soft constraint within a multiphysics-informed neural network via an adaptively weighted composite loss function that synergistically enforces physical consistency, maintains parameter realism, and ensures trend plausibility by penalizing unphysical jumps while permitting valid recovery, guiding each local model to learn physically plausible and interpretable aging dynamics. At the system level, we design MP-APFL to employ a privacy-preserving hybrid architecture with a shared encoder and personalized multiphysics components. This is orchestrated by an adaptive strategy featuring Information value-driven client prioritization for efficient client selection and physics-guided and stability-aware encoder aggregation for robust model fusion. Comprehensive experiments on laboratory-generated and public datasets demonstrate that MP-APFL significantly outperforms prominent FL benchmarks, particularly in highly heterogeneous and imbalanced settings.

title: Fully Data-Driven Inductance Reconstruction for Position-Sensorless Control of Switched Reluctance Motors
authors: Gyuwon Kim, Huibeom Youn, Minje Cho, Jaepil Ban
abstract: This study proposes a fully data-driven rotor position estimation method for sensorless control of switched reluctance motors (SRMs) under diverse operating conditions. The entire control process is performed online, spanning from initial startup to full-cycle position estimation without any prior motor information. For the initial startup and low-speed operation, a threshold-triggered slope drive (TTSD) method is proposed. While driving using TTSD, inductance extrema are computed and stored to construct an unsaturated inductance profile. Subsequently, an inductance vector method is used for estimating rotor position and is performed concurrently with the reconstruction of the energized phase inductance using an unsaturated inductance profile. Through this method, the rotor position is accurately estimated over the entire cycle, regardless of high-load operating conditions. Furthermore, an adaptive method for compensating the reconstructed inductance is also introduced to achieve robust estimation even under bus voltage fluctuations, without requiring a voltage sensor. The proposed method can be implemented through a simple online estimation process with minimal memory usage. Experimental results confirm its capability to achieve accurate rotor position estimation and robust operation without relying on model parameters, offline data, or additional hardware.

title: Day-Ahead Scheduling of Distributed Energy Resources in Net-Zero Energy Houses with Multi-Day Operation Using Soft Actor-Critic
authors: Chao-Hsien Hsiao, Wei-Chen Lin, Wei-Tzer Huang
abstract: This paper presents a reinforcement learning–based day-ahead scheduling framework for distributed energy resources in a net-zero energy house equipped with photovoltaic panels, a wind turbine, a battery energy storage system, and optional electric vehicle flexibility. The net-zero energy house is connected to a low-voltage grid (three-phase four-wire 380/220 V and single-phase three-wire 220/110 V). A soft actor–critic controller generates continuous 24-hour schedules at 15-minute intervals while enforcing converter power limits and state-of-charge constraints. A cross-day mechanism carries the terminal state-of-charge of each day into the next, ensuring physically consistent multi-day operation. The reward formulation is designed to reduce grid import and enhance the temporal matching between household load and distributed energy resource generation through netload shaping and state-of-charge-safe operation, with feasibility ensured via action projection under energy headroom constraints. The soft actor–critic policy is trained using measured 2023 operation profiles and evaluated on measured 2024 winter (January) and summer (July) data under two asset configurations: (i) a 30‑kWh battery energy storage system without electric vehicle and (ii) a 16‑kWh battery energy storage system with electric vehicle support. Across both seasons and configurations, the proposed scheduler increases the self-sufficiency rate and self-consumption rate to approximately 0.72 and reduces total grid-import energy by more than 50%, demonstrating robust month-long continuous operation suitable for practical net-zero energy house deployment. These results demonstrate the practical feasibility of soft actor–critic-based day-ahead scheduling with multi-day operation for intelligent residential energy management.

title: A Multifaceted Approach to Bangla Speech Emotion Recognition Using Hybrid Feature Fusion and Dimensionality Reduction
authors: Abu Omayed, MD. Asif Iqbal, Mohammed Moniruzzaman Bhuiyan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Speech Emotion Recognition (SER) is critical for Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), but robust SER systems for the Bangla language remain underexplored. This paper proposes a novel approach that combines extensive fusion of acoustic characteristics with statistical descriptors and a multifaceted approach based on dimensionality reduction to improve Bangla SER performance. We fuse diverse time-domain, frequencydomain, and time-frequency features (e.g., Amplitude Envelope, Zero-Crossing Rate, Mel-frequency cepstral coefficients, Spectral Centroid) by computing summary statistics. We apply Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Independent Component Analysis (ICA), and Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) to create a compact multifaced feature set. The approach is evaluated on two Bangla emotional speech datasets-SUBESCO (7 emotions) and KBES (9 emotions)-using four machine learning classifiers (kNearest Neighbors, Decision Tree, Random Forest, and XGBoost). The best model, Random Forest with the proposed featurereduction pipeline, achieves 97.78% accuracy on the KBES dataset and 87.14% on the larger SUBESCO dataset using only a fraction of the original features. These results demonstrate that the proposed strategy can significantly enhance emotion recognition accuracy while greatly decreasing feature dimensionality. This improvement will increase the efficiency of SER systems for under-resourced languages, such as Bangla.

title: A multi-task learning embedded parameterization method for downward shortwave radiation estimation under cloudy-sky conditions_supp1-3690513.docx
authors: Yunhao Chen

title: DSRF-SAR: Neural Radiance Field Representation Based on a Dual SAR Scattering Mechanism
authors: Yang Yang, Yueting Zhang, Jiayi Guo, Guangyao Zhou, Xia Ning, Yongchao Zhao
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) enables high-resolution imaging of the Earth's surface under all weather and illumination conditions. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the limited diversity of viewpoints restrict its application in data-intensive tasks. Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) can implicitly represent scenes and synthesize novel-view images from unseen viewpoints. Existing NeRF-based methods have achieved promising results in SAR image generation. However, they often fail to capture the complex electromagnetic scattering characteristics and sharp geometric transitions. This limitation often results in blurring of target regions and the omission of background textures. To address these issues, we propose DSRF-SAR, a neural radiance field representation based on a dual SAR scattering mechanism. Specifically, the scattering is decomposed into two components: isotropic scattering, which depends solely on spatial location, and anisotropic scattering, which depends on both location and observation geometry. Additionally, directional interpolation is reparameterized by using the reflection direction of radar wave instead of the incident direction to learn a more accurate SAR scattering distribution. And the target surface is represented using a Signed Distance Function (SDF), enabling stronger geometric constraint and higher-fidelity scene reconstruction. Qualitative and quantitative experiments, including sparse-view interpolation and few-shot extrapolation, are conducted on the Moving and Stationary Target Acquisition and Recognition (MSTAR) dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches and generates high-quality SAR images of novel views, providing additional support for SAR image interpretation and applications.

title: Training-Free Vision-Language Model for Substrate Glass Defect Detection
authors: Jiaxuan Liu, Hui Zhang, Hai Wang, Bingrui Zhao, Yang Zhao, Yingtian Yu
abstract: Substrate glass is essential for advanced applications, such as displays and semiconductors, where microscopic defects can critically degrade yield and reliability. Automated optical inspection for this task faces a tripartite challenge: 1) severe data scarcity, stemming from industrial confidentiality and evolving defect morphologies; 2) multiillumination complexity, which requires synthesizing information from complementary bright-field and dark-field imaging; and 3) multifocal sequential dependency, arising from defects located at varying depths within the glass. Conventional supervised methods are infeasible under such data constraints, while existing zero-shot vision-language models (VLMs) are ill-equipped to handle these domain-specific imaging characteristics. To address this gap, we propose Training-Free framework for Substrate Glass Defect Detection (TFSGD). Without requiring any weight updates, TFSGD introduces a novel pipeline that first leverages illumination-specific prompt engineering and clustering-based text embedding selection to generate robust classifiers from a frozen VLM. It then fuses multiillumination information through a novel structure-sparse low-rank fusion module. Finally, it resolves focal dependency using a clarity-guided sequence refinement strategy. Extensive experiments on a real-world industrial dataset demonstrate that TFSGD establishes a new state-of-the-art in zero-shot defect segmentation and localization, offering a practical, readily deployable solution that circumvents the prohibitive costs of data collection and annotation in industrial settings.

title: Fully Unsupervised Self-debiasing of Text-to-Image Diffusion Models
authors: Korada Sri Vardhana, Shrikrishna Lolla, Soma Biswas
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) diffusion models have achieved widespread success due to their ability to generate high-resolution, photorealistic images. These models are trained on large-scale datasets, like LAION-5B, often scraped from the internet. However, since this data contains numerous biases, the models inherently learn and reproduce them, resulting in stereotypical outputs. We introduce SelfDebias, a fully unsupervised test-time debiasing method applicable to any diffusion model that uses a UNet as its noise predictor. SelfDebias identifies semantic clusters in an image encoder’s embedding space and uses these clusters to guide the diffusion process during inference, minimizing the KL divergence between the output distribution and the uniform distribution. Unlike supervised approaches, SelfDebias does not require human-annotated datasets or external classifiers trained for each generated concept. Instead, it is designed to automatically identify semantic modes. Extensive experiments show that SelfDebias generalizes across prompts and diffusion model architectures, including both conditional and unconditional models. It not only effectively debiases images along key demographic dimensions while maintaining the visual fidelity of the generated images, but also more abstract concepts for which identifying biases is also challenging.

title: A Real-Time Smart Home Security System for Alert-based Intrusion Detection using YOLO and FaceNet
authors: Dip Bhattacharyya, Tanmoy Chanda, Nabanita Das, Tahamina Yesmin, Soma Chatterjee, Priyanka Das
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: In today's world, home security has become a critical concern, which is now the most prominent challenge due to increasing threats in safety and attempting unauthorized access. In this study, our main concern lies in detecting authorized individuals, identifying intruders, and analyzing maskwearing status through an integrated deep learning surveillance system. From multiple cameras, a large number of detection and recognition decisions are obtained. In real-time video streams, ambiguities arise from illumination variations, partial occlusions, and uncertain face angles. To overcome those challenges, a hybrid framework based on YOLO, FaceNet, MTCNN and MobileNetV2 is designed. The proposed architecture utilizes YOLO for realtime person face detection, MTCNN for face alignment, FaceNet for identity recognition, and a lightweight MobileNetV2-based Mask Classifier to distinguish masked and unmasked faces. For accurate identification under uncertain visual conditions, we incorporate optimized preprocessing techniques such as lightweight alignment, batched embedding extraction etc. An alert will also be triggered instantly when an intruder is detected and an email notification with intruder snapshots will be send to admin's email. Finally, few evaluations are performed to authenticate the efficiency of the proposed method through real-time experiments. Several observations can be drawn regarding the detection accuracy, recognition stability, and processing latency across diverse lighting and pose variations. Interestingly, it is noticed that the proposed hybrid model consistently maintains high recognition confidence for known individuals and accurately flags unknown persons for alert generation. Also, from a comparative study of multiple YOLO versions it can be concluded that YOLO version 12 (nano) offers the most balanced performance in terms of speed, stability, and detection precision which makes it the most suitable model for the proposed smart home surveillance system.

title: Enhancing Telegram Scam Detection Using OCR-Based Deep Learning Techniques
authors: Mithun Jayant M, Manikandan K, Gnanasoundharam J
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: As scams on messaging platforms like Telegram continue to rise, the detection of malicious content, particularly from images has become crucial, traditional keyword-based filtering, and Manual moderation is very insufficient since scammers embed harmful text embedded in images to evade detection. This paper proposes an Automated Scam Detection System using Tesseract-OCR with machine learning models for the extraction, preprocessing, and classification of text from image based Telegram messages. TF-IDF-based feature extraction and machine-learning classifiers (SVM/Random Forest) categorize messages into scam or non-scam classes and identify Scam types include crypto scams, gambling scams, and general fraud. Performance measures such as precision, accuracy, F1-score and recall show the effectiveness of the system. This low cost, scalable solution reduces manual workload and enables realtime detection, while being adaptable for future enhancements such as multilingual OCR and deep learning integration.

title: Generative AI-Driven Framework for Zero-Day Attack Prediction in Next-Gen Cybersecurity Using FusionGuardNet with DualInstinct Optimizer
