title: Bengali Cyberbullying Detection in Social Media Comments Using Machine Learning Algorithms
authors: Md. Abdul Hai, Sultana Jahan Soheli, Md. Tanvir Ahmed, Abu Rayhan, Ashikur Rahman Khan, Md Mahbubul Alam, M. Shamim Kaiser
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Facebook and Twitter are the most popular social media sites where cyberbullying is common. It is defined as the exploitation of digital communication to harass, intimidate, or degrade people, and this may cause serious psychological and social harm. Cyberbullying can occur at any time and is more difficult to avoid as opposed to traditional bullying. In this paper, we aim to identify cyberbullying in Bengali social media comments, a lowresource language, and to detect abusive content in depth. To address the problem, we trained and evaluated five models: Bangla-BERT, m-BERT, LSTM, Bi-LSTM, and SVM with a labelled dataset of Bengali social media comments. The models were trained to label the comments as abusive and non-abusive. The models were trained to classify comments as abusive or non-abusive. Experimental results show that Bangla-BERT achieved the highest accuracy (94 %), followed by m-BERT (91 %), Bi-LSTM (89 %), LSTM (87 %), and SVM (76 %). Although the Bangla-BERT is better than the other models, it can still be improved with bigger and more varied datasets. The study illustrates the usefulness of machine learning in cyberbullying detection in Bengali and also leads to safer online communication, although it also indicates future directions of low-resource language processing.

title: A Commercial-Grade Reliable 40nm eRRAM Macro Featuring a Dual-Oxide Resistive Switching Layer
authors: Zhenchao Sui, Chengxiang Ma, Kaimeng Liu, Xiangchao Ma, Jianshi Tang, Huaqiang Wu, Xing Zhang
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: This paper presents an embedded resistive random-access memory (eRRAM) macro fabricated on a commercial 40nm CMOS technology platform. Through a comprehensive product-level data validation, we first established a baseline HfO<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>-based RRAM that optimizes the trade-off between the forming and pre-cycling times during the chip probing (CP) stage. Subsequently, a novel dual-oxide resistive switching layer (RSL), enabled by an optimized atomic layer deposition (ALD) process, was developed. This dual-oxide RSL significantly enhanced the retention performance of the RRAM macro, achieving a 38.9% improvement in the post-bake memory window on the wafer level without degrading other performance metrics. The optimized 40nm RRAM macro demonstrates commercial-grade performance for the display driver integrated circuit (DDIC) application, including endurance >1K cycles, data retention >10 years at 85°C, read disturb immunity >10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">9</sup> cycles, and passes the solder reflow test at 260°C for 900s according to JEDEC standard [1]. Therefore, this work provides a highly competitive embedded non-volatile memory (eNVM) solution and promotes the commercialization of RRAM technology.

title: Federated Edge Learning MAC Cross-Layer Optimization in 6G Networks : The Case for Radial Basis Functions Optimization
authors: Spyridon Louvros, Anupkumar Pandey, Brijesh Shah, Yashesh Buch
abstract: This paper studies the impact of retransmission strategies on federated edge learning performance in wireless edge networks for 5G Advanced and emerging 6G systems. Hybrid automatic repeat request mechanisms are examined in multi device learning scenarios, where unreliable transmissions affect model convergence and latency. Retransmission selection is formulated as a global optimization problem balancing delay, energy efficiency and update reliability under uncertain channel conditions. A radial basis function-based optimization framework is proposed, showing improved learning robustness and communication efficiency compared to conventional approaches.

title: On the Limited Availability Group Model for Multirate Poisson Traffic
authors: Marinos Vlasakis, Maria Kourtesi, Iskanter-Alexandros Chousainov, Irene Keramidi, Dimitris Uzunidis, Orestis Zestas, Ioannis Moscholios, Michael Logothetis
abstract: Loss models for finite-capacity systems play a fundamental role in teletraffic theory, particularly for dimensioning multirate service-classes. Among them, the Limited Availability Group (LAG) model constitutes an important extension of the classical Erlang Multirate Loss Model for systems consisting of multiple separate resources that accommodate Poisson arriving calls. This paper presents a review of the LAG model with emphasis on clarifying the model’s assumptions and the intermediate steps required for the call blocking probability determination through analytical tutorial examples. Furthermore, the accuracy of the LAG model is evaluated against simulation results for small-capacity systems, demonstrating satisfactory performance.

title: Employing Machine Learning Algorithms for Intrusion Attack Detection
authors: Nadia Attiya Algaddar, Melad Mohamed Al-Daeef, Alhadi A. Klaib
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: As computer networks grow increasingly, complex and robust security mechanisms are essential and critically needed. This research is an attempt to evaluate four machine learning algorithms they are, Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbor, and Support Vector Machine for real-time intrusion detection using the publicly available CICIDS2017 dataset. Based on performance metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, Decision Tree and SVM are expected to achieve the highest accuracy, demonstrating strong efficiency and reliability for real-time intrusion detection.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Business: A Systematic Review of Applications in Leadership, Sales, and Management
authors: Sakher M.A Alnajdawi, Saed Adnan Mustafa, Mahmoud Alghizzawi, Hazem Alkasassbeh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly recognized as a transformative force in business, reshaping how organizations lead, sell, and manage in an era of rapid digital change. This paper presents a systematic review of 132 studies published between 2015 and 2025, examining AI’s applications in leadership, sales, and management through the PRISMA framework. The findings reveal that in leadership, AI supports predictive talent analytics, adaptive digital leadership, and inclusive decision-making. In sales, AI enhances demand forecasting, customer engagement, personalization, and dynamic pricing models. In management, AI strengthens human resource practices, improves supply chain resilience, enables advanced financial risk analysis, and supports strategic planning under uncertainty. Across domains, three key themes emerge: AI improves decision-making accuracy, facilitates personalization, and introduces new ethical and transparency challenges. While AI consistently augments efficiency and competitiveness, its effectiveness depends on being implemented as a complement to human judgment rather than a replacement. The review contributes to theory by extending leadership, marketing, and management frameworks into AI-driven contexts and provides practical guidance for organizations seeking to adopt AI responsibly. It concludes by highlighting future research directions, particularly in generative AI, explainable AI, and human–AI collaboration.

title: A Compact Circularly Polarized Array Enabled by a Slot-Coupled Feeding Network
authors: Jingjing Mao, Andrea Michel, Shigang Zhou, Jianying Li, Paolo Nepa
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: This letter proposes a compact circularly polarized (CP) antenna array enabled by a slot-coupled feeding network. By introducing open-ended branches and gradually widened terminals in inverted-F radiating elements, miniaturization of the antenna is achieved. The proposed CP feeding network exploits the inherent 180° phase difference of the non-resonant slot and incorporates 90° phase-delay lines to realize sequential CP excitation. When the feeding network is integrated with the radiating elements, an additional resonance is introduced, expanding the impedance bandwidth of the array from 5.5% to 14.9%. In addition, a Z-shaped slot is employed to effectively extend the axial-ratio bandwidth compared with a conventional straight slot. Simulated and measured results demonstrate that the proposed antenna achieves 4.5% CP bandwidth (845-884 MHz). The size of the antenna is only 0.231 × 0.231 × 0.064 λ03. The proposed design avoids the use of lumped elements or high-permittivity materials, providing a practical solution for compact CP arrays.

title: Generative AI Meets 6G and Beyond: Diffusion Models for Semantic Communications
authors: Hai-Long Qin, Jincheng Dai, Guo Lu, Shuo Shao, Sixian Wang, Tongda Xu, Wenjun Zhang, Ping Zhang, Khaled B. Letaief
abstract: Semantic communications mark a paradigm shift from bit-accurate transmission toward meaning-centric communication, essential as wireless systems approach theoretical capacity limits. The emergence of generative AI has catalyzed generative semantic communications, where receivers reconstruct content from minimal semantic cues by leveraging learned priors. Among generative approaches, diffusion models stand out for their superior generation quality, stable training dynamics, and rigorous theoretical foundations. However, the field currently lacks systematic guidance connecting diffusion techniques to communication system design, forcing researchers to navigate disparate literatures. This article provides the first comprehensive tutorial on diffusion models for generative semantic communications. We present score-based diffusion foundations and systematically review three technical pillars: conditional diffusion for controllable generation, efficient diffusion for accelerated inference, and generalized diffusion for cross-domain adaptation. In addition, we introduce an inverse problem perspective that reformulates semantic decoding as posterior inference, bridging semantic communications with computational imaging. Through analysis of human-centric, machine-centric, and agent-centric scenarios, we illustrate how diffusion models enable extreme compression while maintaining semantic fidelity and robustness. By bridging generative AI innovations with communication system design, this article aims to establish diffusion models as foundational components of next-generation wireless networks and beyond.

title: BERTopic for Disaster Response Needs Assessment: Insights from Twitter(X) Data
authors: Jessa O. Poe, Jeffrey S. Ingosan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Assessment of community needs is essential in disaster response, as it guides Local Government Units and responders in identifying priorities and delivering appropriate assistance to the affected communities. Social media platforms, particularly Twitter(X), were considered as a valuable tool for obtaining information on public concerns and needs during disasters. However, the large volume of data and noise found on social media posed challenges in accurately identifying relevant information. The emergence of unsupervised machine learning approaches, such as topic modeling, enabled the efficient analysis of large datasets. This study investigated the use of BERTopic, a topic modeling technique, to identify and analyze community needs expressed in Twitter(X) data. Historical data were preprocessed, embedded using SentenceTransformers, transformed with class-based TFIDF representations, and clustered using GPU-accelerated cuML to improve the efficiency of both UMAP and HDBSCAN. Several hyperparameter tuning experiments were conducted, and coherence scores were used to evaluate the performance and assess the quality of the generated topics. These results highlighted the importance of combining unigrams and bigrams in enhancing topic coherence. The identified categories were: “Shelter and Evacuation”, “Food and Water”, “Clothing”, “Health and Medical”, “Search and Rescue”, and “Power and Communications”. The study also integrated a Large Language Model (LLM) and manual validation to support topic labeling and enhance interpretability. These results highlight the potential of integrating BERTopic, a transformer-based topic modeling, into disaster response, providing valuable insights to support the assessment of community needs.

title: Metrology-Driven Comparison of Biomechanical Model of Fingers and Thumb: Evaluating Marker Configurations for Kinematic Measurements_supp1-3687323.docx
authors: Juri Taborri
abstract: Accurate recording of hand joint kinematics is crucial for fields such as clinical diagnostics, ergonomics, and sign language recognition. It relies on the precise placement of markers and the number of markers used to capture joint movements effectively. This study presents a simplified marker-based biomechanical model of the hand designed to compute 22 joint angles using only 24 passive retroreflective markers, following a one-marker-per-segment approach. Kinematic data was collected from ten participants performing predefined physiological movements. The proposed biomechanical model was compared with three established biomechanical models employing one, two, and three-markers-per-segment on the fingers. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this study is the first to directly compare these marker configurations simultaneously under identical experimental conditions. Range of motion (ROM) and joint variability were analyzed using repeated-measures ANOVA, while temporal trajectory differences were assessed via one-dimensional Statistical Parametric Mapping (SPM). Comparative analysis revealed no significant differences in ROM, variability, and joint angle trajectories across models, offering a robust and efficient approach for hand motion analysis suitable for functional, ergonomic, and AI-driven applications.

title: Learning-Enabled Elastic Network Topology for Distributed ISAC Service Provisioning
authors: Jie Chen, Xianbin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Conventional mobile networks, including both localized cell-centric networks (CCNs) and cooperative cell-free networks (CFNs), are built upon rigid network topologies. However, neither architecture is well-suited to flexibly support distributed integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) services, due to the increasing difficulty of aligning spatiotemporally distributed heterogeneous service demands with available radio resources. In this paper, we propose an elastic network topology (ENT) for distributed ISAC service provisioning, in which multiple coexisting localized CCNs can operate independently within their local boundaries or be dynamically grouped into CFNs with expanded boundaries for federated network cooperation. This topology elastically orchestrates the boundaries of localized CCNs and federated CFNs to balance signaling overhead and distributed resource utilization, thereby enabling efficient ISAC service provisioning. A two-phase operation protocol is then developed. In Phase-I, each CCN autonomously partitions its resources into dedicated and shared segments and classifies ISAC services as either local or federated. In Phase-II, each CCN employs its dedicated resources for local-type services, while the aggregated CFN consolidates shared resources from its constituent CCNs to deliver federated-type services cooperatively. Furthermore, we design a utility-to-signaling ratio (USR) to quantify the tradeoff between service utility and the operational signaling overhead. Consequently, a USR maximization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing the network topology (i.e., service classification and CCN grouping) and the allocation of dedicated and shared resources. However, this problem is challenging due to its distributed optimization nature and the absence of complete channel knowledge. To address this problem efficiently, we propose a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) framework with centralized training and decentralized execution. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed system design and algorithm development.

title: A Bounded-Update Commitment Protocol (BUCP) for Safe Edge–Cloud Adaptive Control of Networked Industrial Robots
authors: Yong Lin, Yadi Lin
abstract: Edge–cloud robotic systems increasingly rely on cloud-side learning or optimization to update controller parameters online. However, directly committing such updates to real-time industrial manipulators poses severe safety risks: network jitter, packet loss, and time-varying uncertainties can convert an apparently beneficial update into a destabilizing transient. This paper proposes a Bounded-Update Commitment Protocol (BUCP), a safety-gated mechanism for cloud-to-edge parameter commitment. BUCP introduces an edge-resident Safety Gateway that admits a cloud-suggested parameter vector only if it satisfies a verifiable three-layer contract: excitation sufficiency, bounded parameter jump, and predicted performance improvement. The tracking controller is instantiated as an inner-loop Slotine–Li adaptive law, and BUCP further employs a short validation window with automatic rollback to contain post-commit degradation. Under the protocol constraints, the closed-loop signals remain Uniformly Ultimately Bounded (UUB), and the update process is Zeno-free via an enforced minimum dwell time. Experiments on a SIASUN SR50A 6-DOF industrial manipulator deployed with ROS2 under diverse emulated network conditions corroborate the proposed design (servo cycle Tc = 2 ms, 500 Hz). Relative to a 10 Hz periodic update baseline, BUCP reduces the downlink commitment rate by approximately 88% (from 10 Hz to ≈1.0–1.2 Hz in our tests) while preserving near-periodic tracking accuracy in S1 and maintaining bounded degradation under harsher links, demonstrating its value as an auditable safety layer for industrial edge–cloud adaptation. Beyond conventional performance metrics, we report process-level safety indicators—unsafe-update rejection rate, rollback frequency, and recovery time—demonstrating BUCP as a pluggable and auditable safety layer for industrial edge–cloud adaptation.

title: Physics-Guided Deep Neural Networks for Radar-Based UAV Recognition in Different Environments With No Prior in Situ Data
authors: Sean Kearney, Sevgi Z. Gurbuz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Classifying UAV radar micro-Doppler signatures with deep neural networks (DNNs) suffers a degradation in performance when there is a difference in the distribution of training and test data due to the differences in clutter backscatter and signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) of different environments. This is further exacerbated in low SINR samples in which the HERM lines characteristic of UAV blade rotations become only faintly visible. As HERM lines are a critical feature enabling the recognition and discrimination of various types of UAVs, this paper addresses the two-fold challenge of 1) how to accurately synthesize HERM lines in training data for UAV classification, and 2) how to enhance recognition performance in previously unseen, different environments, especially when the SINR is low. Towards these aims, we propose a physics-guided approach in which a semantic segmentation network trained only using low-fidelity CAD-models of UAVs is integrated into the discriminator of a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) for training data synthesis, and on the front-end of a DNN for classification. Our results show that physics-guided semantic segmentation improves the representation of HERM lines in GAN-synthesized samples and enables improved classification of low SINR samples exhibiting HERM lines when no prior in situ data at that SINR is used during training. Consequently, our approach provides a way of enhancing UAV recognition performance in different operational environments, from which it is often difficult to obtain training data prior to deployment.

title: A Comprehensive Review of Controller Techniques for Wireless Power Transfer in Electric Vehicles Charging Applications
authors: M. Saranraj, Saravanan Kaliyaperumal, Ali Elrashidi, Kareem M. AboRas, R Narayanamoorthi
abstract: Wireless power transfer technology has emerged as a key enabler for efficient and convenient charging of electric vehicles, addressing the limitations of conventional plug-in charging systems. However, WPT systems face significant challenges, including power control, resonance frequency tuning, load regulation, and efficiency optimization. To overcome these challenges, various control strategies have been developed to enhance the stability, safety, and performance of these systems. This review provides a comprehensive analysis of different controller techniques, including proportional-integral controllers, sliding mode controllers, model predictive controllers, and adaptive control methods. It also explores optimization algorithms and hybrid approaches that aim to improve the dynamic response, robustness, and energy efficiency. By comparing the effectiveness of these control strategies, this study offers guidance to researchers and engineers in selecting the most suitable controller for specific applications in wireless charging of electric vehicles. The review concludes by highlighting future research directions and potential advancements in intelligent control strategies for next-generation wireless charging systems.

title: Development and Evaluation of Machine Learning Models for Diabetes Risk Prediction Using BRFSS 2023 Data
authors: Sarah Mokhtar, Jad Abbass
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Diabetes remains a major global health burden, with its rising prevalence demanding scalable approaches to early risk detection. Traditional clinical screening is resource-intensive, whereas survey-based modelling offers a complementary tool for population-level surveillance. In this study, we develop and evaluate machine learning classifiers on the 2023 U.S. Behavioural Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) to predict self-reported diabetes and prediabetes. Following a comprehensive computational comparison, three models—elastic net logistic regression, gradient boosting, and XGBoost—were selected for hyperparameter optimisation, probability calibration, and recall-oriented threshold tuning. Given that diabetes risk prediction prioritises minimising false negatives (recall) over false positives (precision), we emphasised PR-AUC as the primary metric, with AUROC as a secondary measure. XGBoost and gradient boosting achieved the strongest overall performance (PR-AUC ≈ 0.48, AUROC ≈ 0.83), and emerged as reliable models, balancing discrimination, calibration, and sensitivity for practical screening applications while elastic net maximised sensitivity at the cost of precision.

title: A Comparative Study of FPGA Debouncing Architectures for Emergency Stop Safety Channels
authors: Md Towhidul Islam, Shanjida Islam, Isha Das, Chaitaly Nandi, Monjurul Islam, Tasnim Jerin Labonno
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: When a human hits an emergency stop, the logic must not chatter. Mechanical contacts exhibit millisecondscale bounce that can produce multiple spurious transitions. For emergency-stop (E-Stop) channels, debouncing must provide explicit guarantees on latency and single-count interpretation. This paper implements and compares two FPGA debouncers on a Cyclone IV device at 5 0 MHz: (i) a window-qualified finite-state machine (FSM) that accepts a change only after a fixed stability interval, and (ii) a cyclelevel FSM that accepts the first stable edge and enforces a lockout to bound subsequent toggles. Both designs employ two- stage synchronization and close timing with large margin. We characterize resource cost, timing, power, latency distributions, and accepted-rate envelopes under shared bounce stimuli. The windowed design offers a simple audit trail tied to an explicit qualification window; the lockout design delivers near-instantaneous response with a clear throughput contract. Results indicate that either approach can supply deterministic, auditable behavior at negligible area and power, enabling straightforward integration into certified E-Stop functions. Parameterselection guidance and diagnostics suitable for proof testing are provided.

title: A Time-updated Fusion of Fast-varying and Slow-varying Signals for Health Assessment of Liquid Aerospace Engines
authors: Zhenyi Chen, Wenhao Zhang, Jianshu Yin, Jin Chen, Yanyang Zi, Hongxing Jia
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Many time-varying state monitoring methods, such as auto-regressive moving average (ARMA) and adaptive threshold algorithm (ATA), often analyze these data streams independently, lacking a joint analysis of the multi-physics signals. Furthermore, liquid aerospace engines (LAEs) operate under multi-source loads and complex conditions, leading to difficulties in system-level assessment throughout the entire working stage. To solve these issues, this paper proposes a dynamic fusion method for fast-varying signals (FVS) (e.g., vibration and pulsating pressure) and slow-varying ones (SVS) (e.g., flow, pressure) to comprehensively assess the system-level health state. First, the concerned instantaneous frequencies of FVS are obtained by second-order synchrosqueezing transform, providing fusion indexes of FVS. Second, a time-varying upper confidence limit algorithm and a confidence interval algorithm are proposed to map structural and functional health indicators onto a unified scale for system-level, whole-stage evaluation. Finally, a dynamic fusion algorithm, where the confidence boundaries of health indicators evolve with time rather than remaining fixed, is proposed for customized nonlinear time-tracking health indexes to translate the extracted features of FVS and key features of SVS into a standardized scale, quantifying dynamic deviation for each feature from its healthy baseline, and subsequently fusing these multi-source indicators into a comprehensive health index to achieve a holistic system-level assessment. To validate its performance, two ground-running tests of LAEs are conducted. The results show that the proposed method can not only cover the whole working stage for health assessment, but is also capable of assessing structural health, functional health, and system-level health, providing an effective tool for health monitoring and assessment for LAEs.

title: Impact of Vehicular Processing Capacity, Mobility Dynamics and Cooperativeness on R2V Task Offloading Performance In Vehicular Fog Networks
authors: Maurice Khabbaz, Hussein Hijazi, Ryan Atallah, Chadi Assi
abstract: In Vehicular Fog Computing (VFC), the per-vehicle processing capacity and mobility dynamics exert climacteric impacts on the task offloading performance of in-proximity RoadSide Units (RSUs) in terms of critical Quality-of-Service (QoS) metrics (e.g., task admission and Head-of-Line (HoL) blocking probabilities as well as the task expiry probability and mean buffering delay). Precisely, RSU buffer exhaustion, RSUto- Vehicle (R2V) denial of task release and, hence, resulting increases in the average system response time are undesirable but often inevitable whenever incoming vehicles into the RSU's range suffer from limited task processing resources and/or happen to be navigating at high speeds; hence, limiting their capability to successfully process and complete tasks within their relatively short residency within the RSU's coverage range. Existing work have already highlighted the possible occurrence of the abovementioned R2V Task Offloading (R2VTO) shortcomings. However, very few publications have quite marginally quantified the impact of vehicle processing resource availabilities and mobility dynamics on the R2VTO QoS performance. Also, the vehicles' non-cooperativeness has been largely overlooked. This work aims at closing these gaps through the establishment of a theoretical stochastic framework, throughout which the task release feasibility is derived from first principles (as opposed to being exogenously pre-defined in prior work) and the above-mentioned QoS metrics are expressed as a function of the vehicles's processing capacity, mobility characteristics and cooperativeness probability. Extensive simulations are then conducted to verify the correctness, validity and accuracy of the derived expressions and evaluate the R2VTO's performance as a function of each of the considered metrics as well as existing scheduling disciplines.

title: Fake News Detection in Bangla: A Transformer Ensemble Framework for Robust Classification
authors: Shubail Haque Turza, Akif Mahdi, Md. Ibne Shihab Shad, M. Hasnat Kabir, Ariful Islam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The exponential growth of fake news over digital media has seriously disrupted information and public trust, as well as threatened social stability. These challenges are even more severe for low-resource, morphologically rich languages like Bangla, where the scarcity of NLP resources and a high amount of code-mixed content make the automated detection even more challenging. To address this gap, the research proposes a transformer-based ensemble model for Bangla fake news detection, which combines BanglaBERT, IndicBERT, and XLM-RoBERTa via soft voting, along with a morphology-aware preprocessing pipeline. On the balanced IBFND benchmark, BanglaBERT achieved the best single-model result with 99.03% accuracy, and the ensemble achieved 98.93 % accuracy with more stable performance on evaluation metrics. Cross-dataset validation on the BanglaFakeNews corpus provided additional evidence of the approach's robustness but also demonstrated the effect of imbalance and domain coverage on evaluation. Overall, the results have shown that language-aware preprocessing combined with ensemble learning sets a high and consistent baseline for Bangla fake news detection, and offers a promising direction for under-resourced languages with complex linguistic structures as well.

title: Accelerating Distributed GNN Training by Attention Based Two-Level Dynamic Feature Caching
authors: Md Golam Morshed, Md. Murad Kabir Nipun, Raton Kumar Nondi, F A Sabbir Ahamed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The effectiveness of deep learning approaches on datasets such as images and texts has been extended to more complicated graph-structured data by evolving graph neural networks (GNNs). The current frameworks integrate mini-batch and sampling to train models efficiently on big graphs using GPU accelerators. Distributed GNN training has become more common as several concurrent workers learn on separate parts of a big graph, driven by the rapid increase in both the size of graphs and the dimensions of features. However, research has shown that the collection of graph features across servers takes up the majority of each worker's training time, which poses a serious problem for the training of distributed GNN. This paper presents a new and fast memory-aware, two-level attention-based dynamic feature caching method to accelerate GNN training. Our suggested solution effectively minimizes the time required for obtaining features by using the available GPU and CPU resources to store frequently accessed graph data in a cache. Additionally, it incorporates an efficient dynamic caching strategy that considers both the attention score and the structural information of the graph, as well as the data access patterns during the training of a sampling-based GNN. We found that it could achieve a performance speedup of up to 5x over the state-of-the-art baselines, all while eliminating the feature retrieval time from the distributed GNN training pipeline. We used six publicly available large graph datasets to show the efficacy of our method.

title: A multi-task learning embedded parameterization method for downward shortwave radiation estimation under cloudy-sky conditions
authors: Xuehua Li, Yunhao Chen, Kangning Li, Xiuyu Liu, Weizhen Ji
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Downward shortwave radiation (DSR), a key component of the surface energy balance, is commonly estimated using either physics-based methods or machine learning (ML) models. Recently, efforts to integrate the strengths of both approaches have shown great potential for enhancing DSR estimation. However, most existing hybrid methods focus on integrating ML with physics by training ML models on massive datasets simulated by radiative transfer models (RTMs), which is both computationally expensive and time-consuming. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel hybrid approach that embeds an MTL-based model into a physics-based cloud parameterization method, referred to as the multi-task learning embedded parameterization method (MEPM). By grounding the MTL-based component within a rigorous physical backbone, MEPM requires relatively fewer training samples while preserving strong generalization capability. Simultaneously, the powerful nonlinear mapping capacity of the MTL module optimizes the simulation of complex cloud-induced radiative attenuation processes, resulting in great improvements in overall model accuracy. There were three major findings: (1) MEPM demonstrated good performance under cloudy-sky conditions, achieving a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.88 and outperforming the original parameterization method; (2) when compared with representative models and mainstream DSR products, MEPM achieved notable improvements, with maximum reductions in relative root mean square error (rRMSE) of up to 23.80%; and (3) MEPM accurately captured the spatial distribution of DSR across a wide range of regions, time periods, and cloud cover conditions. By embedding ML directly into a cloud parameterization method, this study introduced a highly efficient and robust hybrid approach. This work provides valuable insights for advancing DSR estimation and contributes to research on land surface processes, climate change, and solar energy resource utilization.

title: The Influence of the Marketing Mix (4Ps) and Alumni Experience on Student Recruitment Decision: Evidence from Bahrain
authors: Eman Mahmood Ahmad, Meryem Masmoudi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: The focus of this study is to determine how marketing mix; product, price, place, and promotion, affect student recruitment in a university in Bahrain, and in this case, the mediating role of the alumni experience will be focused on. By applying a quantitative design and the data gathered on 323 students and alumni, the findings prove that product and the promotion are the most effective in predicting the student recruitment, price is moderately effective, and place has the least effect. Mediation analysis proves that the effect of the marketing mix is considerably greater when alumni are satisfied and engaged, which means that the credibility of the work of an institutional marketing is increased by satisfied and active alumni. The research will be of value to the academic literature in the marketing of higher education in two ways: it will empirically prove the role of alumni experience as a mediating factor, and it will indicate context-specific dynamics in the Bahraini sector. In practice, the results highlight the significance of incorporating alumni engagement in the marketing programs to reinforce the recruitment and institutional competitiveness.

authors: N/A

title: Low-Power and Robust SRAM Design: Comparative Evaluation of 6T, ST-9T, and SB-9T Architectures
authors: Md Faiyarul Islam, Md. Rakibul Hasan, Tahmina Akter, Shihab Ahmed, Md. Hasan Maruf, ASM Shihavuddin
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: High-Performance Computing (HPC) depends on multi-core processors and optimized memory architectures to speed up AI, large data, and other powerful applications. These applications require memory systems that combine high speed, energy efficiency, and robustness under aggressive scaling. To address this demand, this work compares three Static Random-Access Memory (SRAM) architectures conventional 6T, Schmitt-Trigger 9T (ST-9T), and Single-Bit line 9T (SB-9T) and analyzes read/write delays, dynamic and static power, Static Noise Margins (SNM), layout area, and Figure of Merit (FOM) to identify the most suitable cell. Simulation results show that the ST-9T cell consumes the lowest dynamic power of 58.1 nW with lowest static power, which is 2.66 nW lower than the other cells, while achieving the highest Read SNM (RSNM) of 261.1 mV. It also delivers a 72.87% faster write operation than the traditional 6T cell and a 38.29% faster write than the SB-9T cell, resulting in the best FOM. Overall, the ST-9T cell is the best option for obtaining low power consumption with robust stability and noise immunity. The simulations and detailed data analysis were executed using EDA design tools with 32 nm CMOS technology.

title: Covert Communication for UAV-Assisted Systems with an Active Warden
authors: Mingjie Yang, Linying Tian, Chan Gao, Bingbing Xiao
abstract: To address the inherent openness of wireless communication environments, covert communication has emerged as a promising approach to protect legitimate transmissions from unauthorized detection. This paper investigates a UAVassisted wireless covert communication system, where channel uncertainty is exploited to enable covert information transmission in the presence of active wardens acting simultaneously as jamming sources. We first model the indirect communication strategy between legitimate parties and characterize the detection behavior of an active warden. To achieve both covertness and communication efficiency, the transmit power of the devices and the hovering positions of UAVs are jointly optimized, subject to the dual constraints of maintaining a sufficient covert communication rate and ensuring a high detection error probability at the warden. Closed-form expressions for the wardens detection error probability and the achievable covert communication rate are derived. Furthermore, numerical optimization is employed to maximize the achievable rate under covertness constraints. Simulation results corroborate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed scheme in enabling covert communication in jamming-assisted active warden scenarios.

title: Smart Detection of Lemon Leaf Diseases Using InceptionV3 for Better Crop Care
authors: Neha Batra, Deepti Thakral, Pinki Sagar, Tanishpreet Kaur, Ratneshwar Kumar Ratnesh, Manoj Kumar Yadav
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Leaf diseases on lemons threaten agricultural production; therefore, early detection is essential for effective management. The study examines the use of the InceptionV3 model in the classification of diseases in lemon leaves, i.e., Citrus Canker, Deficiency Leaf, Healthy Leaf, and Spider Mites. The data comprised 795 images, and a test set was reserved to test at a ratio of 20% of the total. Preprocessing was done by converting images to grayscale, resizing them, and applying Gaussian filtering, which was used to improve the quality of images to be extracted as features. The model had an overall accuracy level of 88% and performed well in predicting Citrus Canker and Healthy Leaf. There was, however, a number that were misidentified, including Spider Mites and Anthracnose, which need further refinement. A confusion matrix was applied to show the capacity of the model to identify diseases correctly, and a classification report showed that there was a recall and precision balance among classes. This paper has shown that InceptionV3 is effectively applicable in the classification of plant diseases and provides an effective platform in enhancing plant disease management and agricultural activities.

title: Spatio-Temporal Hybrid Neural Network for Automated Classification of Batting and Bowling Actions in Cricket Videos
authors: Arnab Singha, Abir Hassan, Saadman Sakib, Kaushik Deb
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Automated cricket action classification is crucial for sports analytics, coaching, and performance evaluation. Existing methods often rely on handcrafted features or shallow models, limiting their ability to capture complex motion dynamics across diverse actions. In this work, we propose a deep learning framework that effectively classifies both batting and bowling actions by integrating spatial and temporal feature extraction. We extend the widely used CKT dataset to eight action classes by incorporating three additional bowling actions: Legspin, Offspin, and Fast Bowling, enhancing the dataset's diversity and representativeness. EfficientNetB0 is employed as a pre-trained backbone to extract robust spatial features from key frames, while a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) network captures temporal dependencies across sequences of frames. Evaluated on the extended dataset, the proposed system achieves a high classification accuracy of 95.89%, outperforming traditional approaches and demonstrating reliable classification of both batting and bowling action classes. The framework's efficiency and accuracy make it suitable for real-time applications in automated sports analysis, coaching assistance, and player performance monitoring.

title: IEEE Tech RXIV
authors: N/A

title: Bridging Educational Gaps: Predicting Retakes Among Bangladeshi Undergraduates with Machine Learning and Explainable AI
authors: Nusrat Zahan Nila, Md. Ali Mahmud Pritom, Fatema Tuz Johora, Aditya Rajbongshi, Md. Salauddin Sarker, Md. Ashrafuzzaman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: In higher education, course retakes present a significant challenge, disrupting academic progression, increasing financial burdens and psychological distress among students. This study presents a novel approach for early prediction of students' course retakes in the context of Bangladesh. The dataset was meticulously collected from different university students, consisting of 474 instances. Initially, the dataset was prepared using various preprocessing techniques, including data cleaning, transformation, handling missing values, one-hot encoding, column renaming, as well as feature scaling technique. Experimentation with several Machine Learning (ML) models revealed that the proposed Soft Voting classifier outperformed the baseline classifiers Logistic Regression (LR), Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB), Random Forest (RF), Support Vector Classifier (SVC), Perceptron, and K-nearest Neighbors (KNN) with an accuracy of 94.74%. To enhance the interpretability of ML model's two eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) algorithms were integrated to identify the key factors influencing the model's predictions. This approach provided a reliable and equitable prediction of course retakes or failures utilizing real datasets, contributing to enhanced student academic success and improved institutional efficiency.

title: GlucoNova: A Hybrid Model for Non-Invasive Blood Glucose Monitoring Using Near-Infrared Photoplethysmography
authors: Tanim Ahmmod, Rajat Sarkar, Mohammod Abdul Motin
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Diabetes mellitus is a growing global health crisis requiring frequent and accurate blood glucose (BG) monitoring. Traditional invasive methods, while effective, present challenges of pain, high cost, and poor patient compliance. This study presents a novel non-invasive BG monitoring system leveraging a low-cost photoplethysmogram (PPG) sensor. The core innovation of this work is a hybrid prediction framework that combines a global polynomial regression model with an optional personalized model. This approach effectively addresses the issue of intersubject physiological variability, which is a major limitation of many non-invasive techniques. The system's comprehensive pipeline includes a MATLAB-based signal processing stage and an automated data synchronization architecture that transmits real-time patient metrics to a secure, cloud-based platform for remote monitoring. Performance evaluation demonstrated the superior accuracy of the hybrid model, yielding an outstanding RMSE of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.602 \text{mmol} / \mathrm{L}$</tex>. This marked improvement confirms that our innovative hybrid methodology offers a precise and reliable solution for non-invasive BG monitoring, representing a significant step toward creating an accurate, affordable, and user-friendly system for personal diabetes management.

title: A Novel African Vulture Optimization Algorithm for Maximum Power Extraction from Different PV Array Arrangements
authors: Rabbiul Hasan Ankon, Munshi Jawad Ibne Azad, Md. Al Imran Fahim
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: With the increasing demand for the world's renewable energy, photovoltaic (PV) systems are an emerging and popular choice for producing sustainable electricity. However, they are strongly impacted by environmental factors such as temperature and sunlight. A plurality of local maxima (LM) can exist in the PV characteristic curve during partial shading (PS), and tracking of global maxima (GM) instead of LM is a big challenge. In this regard, the present work utilized a novel African Vulture Optimization Algorithm (AVOA) for GM tracking to overcome these issues. The AVOA-based maximum power point tracking (MPPT) algorithm is evaluated for multiple PV array configurations, and also, a comparison is made with the Horse Herd Optimization Algorithm (HHOA). Simulation results in MATLAB/Simulink are presented to show the effectiveness of the AVOA, and it is found that it is greater than that of the HHOA in tracking time, settling time, and tracking accuracy. Interestingly, AVOA achieves a 99.98% average tracking efficiency, with an average tracking time 70 ms faster and an average convergence time 60 ms faster. Along with that, AVOA consistently outperforms HHOA in error metrics, ensuring more dependable MPPT performance. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the AVOA-based MPPT approach to increasing the performance of PV systems under different PV array arrangements.

title: Toward Safer Workplaces: A Review of Digital Twin-Enabled Safety Training
authors: Shahed Chowdhury Omi, Musfiq Alam Chowdhury, Sabiha Alam Chowdhury, Mehedi Hasan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Rapid technological advancements in Industry 4.0 are reshaping industrial environments, introducing new operational risks alongside productivity gains. Traditional safety training methods often fail to adequately prepare workers for the complex, dynamic challenges of modern workplaces. This paper presents a comprehensive review of Digital Twin (DT) technology as a transformative solution for industrial safety training. By creating dynamic, high-fidelity virtual replicas of physical systems that are updated with real-time data, DTs offer immersive, risk-free and data-rich training environments. This review explores the limitations of conventional training, examines the role of enabling technologies such as the Internet of Things (IoT), Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Extended Reality (XR) and outlines the significant benefits of DT-enabled training, including enhanced realism, scenario repeatability and datadriven performance feedback. It also tackles the main obstacles to adoption, including infrastructure expenses, data standardization and legacy system integration. This paper makes the case for DT-based methods' potential to greatly increase workforce competency and lower workplace incidents by contrasting them with other digital training solutions and highlighting their distinct advantages in developing flexible and contextually relevant safety protocols.

title: Underwater Image Reconstruction Using a Swin Transformer-Based Generator and PatchGAN Discriminator
authors: Md. Mahbub Hasan Akash, Aria Tasnim Mridula, Sheekar Banerjee, Ishtiak Al Mamoon
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Underwater imaging is essential for marine exploration, environmental monitoring, and infrastructure inspection. However, water causes severe image degradation through wavelength-dependent absorption and scattering, resulting in color distortion, low contrast, and haze effects. Traditional reconstruction methods and convolutional neural network-based approaches often fail to adequately address these challenges due to limited receptive fields and inability to model global dependencies. This paper presented a novel deep learning framework that integrated a Swin Transformer architecture within a generative adversarial network (GAN) for underwater image reconstruction. Our generator employed a U-Net structure with Swin Transformer blocks to capture both local features and long-range dependencies crucial for color correction across entire images. A PatchGAN discriminator provided adversarial training to ensure high-frequency detail preservation. We trained and evaluated our model on the EUVP dataset, which contains paired underwater images of varying quality. Quantitative results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance with PSNR of 24.76 dB and SSIM of 0.89, representing significant improvements over existing methods. Visual results showed effective color balance restoration, contrast improvement, and haze reduction. An ablation study confirms the superiority of our Swin Transformer designed over convolutional alternatives. The proposed method offers robust underwater image reconstruction suitable for various marine applications.

title: A Lightweight Ensemble Network for Brain Tumor Detection from MRI Images
authors: Rizoan Toufiq, Zahirul Islam, Shovon Sarker
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Brain tumor detection from MRI images is a critical task in medical imaging, requiring a balance between model performance and computational efficiency. In this study, we propose a lightweight ensemble network that combines the outputs of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and MobileNet through two dense layers and a final classification layer. We evaluate the performance and parameter efficiency of several state-of-the-art (SOTA) deep learning models, including EfficientNetB0, DenseNet-121, MobileNet, InceptionV3, Xception, SqueezeNet 1.1, and a customized CNN.Experimental results showed that DenseNet-121 delivered the best performance among all evaluated models, achieving an accuracy of 97.54 %. However, this high performance came with a significant computational cost, as DenseNet-121 required <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7. 1 8}$</tex> million parameters (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{7. 1 8 ~ M}$</tex>). In contrast, the proposed ensemble method offered a better balance between performance and efficiency, reflecting a significant improvement in the performance-parameter tradeoff. The ensemble network achieved a very similar, or comparable, accuracy of 97.42 %. The ensemble method outperforms lightweight models such as SqueezeNet <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(90.05 \%)$</tex> and the customized CNN <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(94.6 \%)$</tex>. Its performance is also competitive with larger models like InceptionV3 and Xception. These findings highlight the effectiveness of the proposed ensemble method for resource-constrained applications that require high diagnostic accuracy.

title: Polarization Insensitive Metasurfaces Absorber for Visible Wavelengths
authors: Hayatul Islam Navin, Abu Ismail Siddique, Makshudur Rahman, Sharaf Tasnim
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Metasurface-based plasmonic absorbers leverage engineered metallic surfaces to enhance light absorption at specific wavelengths, which have opened new applications in nanophotonics. However, many existing plasmonic absorbers suffer from narrow bandwidths or reduced efficiency in the visible regime, which limits their applicability in practical photonic devices. This work presents a plasmonic light absorber (PLA) using metasurfaces to achieve a high absorption in the visible spectrum of light. The metasurface consists of an infinite array of unit cell with a cylinder surrounded by a ring, where the cylinder and the ring are made of aluminum oxide (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$</tex>) and titanium (Ti), respectively. Simulation results reveal an average absorption <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$>97 \%$</tex> covering the visible wavelengths of 380 to 700 nm and a maximum absorption around unity from 380 to 500 nm. Additionally, the metasurface is polarization-insensitive due to its structural symmetry, hence independent of incident light polarization and giving same absorption efficiency. The proposed metasurface absorber can be used for applications requiring broadband, polarization-independent absorption in the visible spectrum.

title: Explainable Hybrid Ensemble Models for Agricultural Soil Erosion Mitigation
authors: Afzalul Abid Nazir, Jahir Uddin Mohammad Babar, Islam Saiful, Md. Faruk Abdullah Al Sohan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Soil erosion is a major environmental and agricultural challenge, leading to land degradation, reduced crop productivity, and long-term ecological imbalance. Traditional assessment techniques often rely on empirical or rule-based models, which are limited in scalability and adaptability to diverse environmental conditions. This study introduces a hybrid stacking ensemble framework for soil erosion risk prediction, formulated as a binary classification task (HighErosion vs. LowErosion). Eight baseline machine learning models such as Logistic Regression, Naïve Bayes, Decision Tree, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost were systematically evaluated against the proposed hybrid ensemble. The methodology integrates preprocessing, hyperparameter tuning, and interpretability techniques such as feature importance and SHAP analysis. Experimental results demonstrate that the hybrid stacking ensemble outperformed all baselines, achieving 98.07% accuracy, an F1-score of 0.981, and an AUC of 0.997. Key predictors included rainfall, slope, and soil type, while vegetation cover contributed secondary but significant effects. The findings highlight the potential of ensemble-based machine learning for developing decision-support tools that enable policymakers and farmers to implement proactive erosion control strategies, ensuring sustainable land management.

title: Enhancing Wind Farm Operational Efficiency Through Power Output Classification Using MLP and Ensemble Models
authors: Md Fujael Ahmed, Rayhan Ahmed Opi, Rashedul Albab, Syed Nadim Mehdi, Abdul Hadee Tahsin, Md. Shahid Iqbal
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Accurately forecasting wind turbine power output is challenging due to the complex and non-linear patterns present in wind farm operational data. Conventional approaches largely adopt regression-based forecasting, which, while effective in predicting continuous values, often lacks the interpretability required for direct operational decision-making. In this work, we reframe the problem as a multi-class classification task, categorizing power output into discrete levels, low, medium, and high, to provide actionable insights for identifying underperforming turbines and optimizing maintenance schedules. A curated Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) dataset was processed through a rigorous pipeline, including the handling of physically impossible negative power values, outlier capping, feature normalization, and label encoding. Five Machine Learning (ML) models, including Logistic Regression (LR), Random Forest (RF), Gradient Boosting (GB), XGBoost, and a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), were trained and optimized using Grid Search hyperparameter tuning. The MLP model obtained the highest classification accuracy of 93.51%, marginally outperforming the top-performing ensemble models. Feature importance analysis revealed that while tree-based models relied heavily on a limited set of features, the MLP distributed its attention more evenly across all inputs. The proposed classification-based framework offers a robust and interpretable alternative to regression-based approaches, providing direct operational benefits for the renewable energy sector.

title: Reactive Current Profiles of Grid-Forming Converter and Power Plant During Voltage Sags
authors: Fangzhou Zhao, Tianhua Zhu, Yifei Li, Xiongfei Wang
abstract: As an internal voltage source, a grid-forming (GFM) converter is required to deliver a natural response of reactive current to grid voltage variations. However, this behavior differs from the current injection rule in existing grid codes, which were initially defined for grid-following converter. This article first introduces an analytical model quantifying the reactive current profile (RCP) of a GFM converter, defined as the relationship between the reactive current magnitude and voltage deviation. The model highlights the impacts of effective resistance, reactance and QV droop gain on RCP, offering clear insights into its connection and distinction from the k factor specified in existing grid codes. Then, an aggregation approach based on Thévenin equivalence is presented to describe the RCP of a GFM power plant consisting of multiple GFM converter strings. The approach provides a simple and efficient tool to evaluate the RCP at the plant level, considering the control effects and disparities within the power plant. The experiments of a downscaled converter and simulations of a nine-converter GFM power plant are presented for validation.

title: Editorial: Special Issue on the XPRIZE Rainforest Competition
authors: Matthew Spenko, Alexis Lussier Desbiens, José Reginaldo Hughes Carvalho

title: Advanced Stair-Climbing Delivery Robot with Multimedia Transmission and Client Verification
authors: Md. Al Tas Did Ul, Md. Muddassir Fuad, Dip Kumar Saha, Sarafat Hussain Abhi
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper presents a stair-climbing delivery robot designed to facilitate secure package delivery in multi-story buildings without elevator access. Current stair-climbing robots excel in mobility however, they don't have integrated client verification system which is important for secured handover of items. Beside this, existing delivery systems often miss terrain adaptability. Proposed stair-climbing robot addresses this gap by providing reliable stair navigation with secured and contactless delivery. These are achieved through a dual-tracked locomotion system with onboard face recognition and live video streaming to provide reliable stair navigation and secure, contactless delivery. Implemented on a cost-effective Raspberry Pi 4B and ESP32-CAM platform, the system leverages teleoperation supported by simulation-based mapping and localization. Experimental results demonstrate an 89% success rate in physical stair-climbing trials under payload, with secure face verification accuracy of up to 88% in typical indoor lighting. The streaming module delivers real-time video with low latency, ensuring transparency and user confidence. This integrated approach offers a practical solution for constrained indoor last-mile delivery, bridging the gap between mobility and security in robotic delivery.

title: Design and Development of a Multi-Modal Deepfake Detection System Integrated into the UnMask Mobile App
authors: Anonno Singha Ray, Md. Raihanul Haque, F. M. Rezwan Parvez, Md. Manirul Islam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: The rapid advancement of deep learning has made it possible for the generation of hyper-realistic synthetic media, commonly known as deepfakes, which pose significant threats to privacy, security, and digital trust. Most existing detection approaches available are computationally expensive and unsuitable for real-time use on mobile platforms. This paper presents UnMask, which is a lightweight and scalable multi-modal real-time deepfake detection application capable of processing image, audio, and video inputs with high accuracy. The proposed framework integrates optimized deep learning models such as DenseNet121 for image classification, a CNNbased architecture for audio analysis, and an EfficientNet-B0 with BiLSTM and attention mechanism for video detection into a FastAPI-powered backend. A corresponding lightweight mobile application, “UnMask,” was developed using dart programming language and Flutter, which enables both prerecorded and live media verification using those models. Companion website was also build for the users to get to know about the app's features and download the app on mobile devices. Experimental results demonstrate strong performance, achieving accuracy scores of 99% for images and audio and 73% for video detection on benchmark datasets. These findings highlight the effectiveness of the proposed approach and its potential for real-time deployment on resource-constrained mobile devices to enhance digital media integrity.

title: MobileNetV4-Small for Seven-Class Soil Classification on CycleGAN-Augmented Images
authors: V.Rekha, Uma Shankari Srinivasan, Neeraja Veeramachineni, Manikandaprabu P, P.Arthi Devarani, Divyanshu Sharma
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study introduces a lightweight and deployable pipeline for soil-type recognition based on MobileNetV4-Small and a composite dataset comprised of 5,097 images consisting of an original set and a CycleGAN-augmented CyAUG set. Seven soil categories (Alluvial, Arid, Black, Laterite, Mountain, Red, Yellow) were taken with the stratified ratio of 70/15/15 for training, validation, and testing. Images were resized to 224×224 pixels and normalized, with no heavy preprocessing needed. The model was trained for 50 epochs using Adam, with categorical cross-entropy, achieving 97.12% test accuracy, macro precision/recall/F1 ≈ 0.964/0.967/0.966. The confusion matrix exhibits strong diagonal dominance, with limited misclassifications mainly between the visually similar Red and Laterite soils. Results indicate that the MobileNetV4-Small successfully captures discriminative chromatic and textural cues while still being suitable for edge deployment in precision agriculture and environmental monitoring. The curated dataset, careful stratification, and well-balanced metrics contribute to a robust baseline on soil classification with compact CNNs.

title: Hybrid Classical-Learned Residual Lossless Image Compression with Affine Translation Augmentation and Entropy-Fidelity Tradeoff Analysis
authors: Sumaya Ishrat Moyeen, Md. Sadiqur Rahman Mabud, Md. Shahid Uz Zaman, Md. Al Mamun
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid residual compression framework that integrates classical spatial prediction with a convolutional autoencoder to improve lossless image coding. Established predictors—PAETH, MED and a simplified CALIC-GAP—are first applied to generate low-entropy residuals, with integer-pixel translation augmentation used during training to enhance predictor robustness. The residuals are then normalized and encoded by a lightweight autoencoder trained for minimal distortion. Evaluations on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CLIC datasets under both teacher-forced and predictor-driven decompression demonstrate consistent entropy reduction with high reconstruction fidelity. Comparative analysis highlights that PAETH predictor consistently achieves the best balance between compression efficiency and reconstruction fidelity, yielding the highest SSIM/PSNR and lowest MSE across all the datasets, while also maintaining competitive entropy levels; CALIC minimizes true entropy but inflates reconstructed entropy; and MED yields the lowest reconstructed entropy at the cost of significant quality loss. These results confirm that combining classical prediction with learned residual modeling provides an efficient and practical pathway toward deployable lossless image compression.

title: Highly Sensitive Novel HC-PCF Sensor for Detecting Harmful Food Additives in Terahertz Domain
authors: Md. Mahbub Hasan, Rabiul Islam, Md. Hasan Marouf Limon, Mahbubul Hasan Mehedi, Shak Mahmudul Hasan, Md. Motiur Rahman Tareq
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This research proposes a novel hollow-core photonic crystal fiber sensor featuring a central circular core and bi-symmetric semi-circular cladding, specifically engineered for sensitive detection of the food additives saccharin, sorbitol, and butyl acetate. In the terahertz (THz) range, the sensor's core and cladding air-region parameters are optimized to ensure stronger analyte-field interaction and effective suppression of confinement loss (CL). The sensor design is modeled using the finite element method in COMSOL Multiphysics software (version 5.6). Key optical parameters, including relative sensitivity (RS), CL, and effective material loss are evaluated to assess the fiber's suitability for accurate and efficient detection. To exemplify the values of the optimized design, the sensor achieves the values of RS of 99.70 % for saccharin, 95.25 % for sorbitol, and 96.26% for butyl acetate, with corresponding values of CL of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.36 \times 10^{-13} ~\text{dB} / \mathrm{m}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5.58 \times 10^{-15} ~\text{dB} / \mathrm{m}$</tex>, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3.01 \times 10^{-14} ~\text{dB} / \mathrm{m}$</tex>, respectively at 2.5 THz. The proposed geometry combines high RS, low CL, simple design and straightforward fabrication, making it promising for real-world deployment in food safety monitoring, offering a pathway toward safer consumption practices while supporting sustainable economic growth and improved food security in developing regions.

title: A Differential Resonant Ultrasound Pulser in 180 nm BCD: Fundamental Limits and Design Considerations
authors: Yaohua Zhang, Andreas Demosthenous
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems I: Regular Papers
abstract: This article presents the design of a differential resonant pulser implemented in 180 nm BCD process for PMUT-based ultrasound applications. By employing a precisely timed switching scheme together with differential resonant operation, a novel voltage step-up methodology is developed. A key contribution of this work is the first comprehensive, phase-by-phase analytical treatment of the pulser operation. This analysis reveals critical inter-phase coupling mechanisms that are not captured by conventional simulation-based approaches. It identifies two previously overlooked design limitations: 1) ac current leakage inherent to back-to-back DMOS configurations; and 2) an unavoidable timing offset caused by finite on-resistance (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${R}_{\mathrm {on}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) between the inductor current peak and the capacitor voltage zero-crossing during discharge. These effects establish fundamental performance boundaries for resonant pulsers. Measurement results validate the proposed design, achieving a 17.7 V output from a 5 V supply (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$3.5\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> voltage step-up) and a 71.7% reduction in power consumption. The measured performance represents state-of-the-art and shows excellent agreement with both theory and simulation.

title: Design and Analysis of Microstrip Patch Antenna with High Gain at 14 GHz for Satellite Communication
authors: Md Tushar Ahammed, Tofayel Karim, Mohammad Abdul Alim
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: This study represents an investigation and performance evaluation of a stacked microstrip patch antenna operating at 14 GHz within the broad Ku-band frequency range, designed and analyzed using CST Studio Suite 2019. The proposed antenna is designed on a Rogers RT/Duroid 5880 substrate with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\epsilon_{r}=2.2$</tex> and substrate thickness of 1.6 mm. An inclusion of quarter-wave transformer (QWT) and defected ground slot are incorporated to enhance the impedance matching, radiation performance and other related antenna parameters. Simulation results responses with a return loss (S11) of −37.12 dB, bandwidth of 1.014 GHz, a high gain of 7.403 dBi, directivity of 8.066 dBi, and radiation efficiency of 85.85%. These characteristics make the proposed antenna suitable for VSAT, CubeSat mission and Satellite communication.

title: A Computational and Empirical Study of ML and DL Models for Scalable DDoS Detection
authors: Md Saidur Rahman Kohinoor, Mehzabeen Azad Chowdhury, Suhrath Tahra
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks pose a persistent challenge to modern network security, requiring detection models that are both accurate and computationally efficient. This paper presents a comparative analysis of six machine learning and deep learning models-Logistic Regression, K-Nearest Neighbors, Random Forest, XGBoost, Long Short-Term Memory, and a feed-forward Neural Network-evaluated on two benchmark datasets: the DDoS Attack Detection Classification dataset and the DDoSdata-Botnet dataset. Models were assessed using accuracy, F1-score, training and prediction time, and memory usage to capture both empirical performance and computational trade-offs. Results demonstrate that XGBoost consistently achieved the highest accuracy (up to 98%) with balanced efficiency, while Neural Networks performed strongly (94%) but required significant resources. Logistic Regression offered reliable performance (91%) with minimal overhead, whereas KNN struggled with high-dimensional data <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(78 \%)$</tex>. The findings highlight the suitability of deep learning for complex attack patterns and the importance of model selection based on resource constraints.

title: When Future Communications Shift Toward Narrow Beams: A Forward-Looking Survey on Pointing Errors and Alignment Limits
authors: Meysam Ghanbari, Mohammad Taghi Dabiri, Osamah S. Badarneh, Mazen Hasna, Yazan H. Al-Badarneh, Mustafa K. Alshawaqfeh, Khalid A. Qaraqe
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Directional links in free-space optical (FSO), millimeter-wave (mmWave), and terahertz (THz) systems are a cornerstone of emerging 6G networks, yet their reliability is fundamentally limited by pointing errors and misalignment. Existing studies address this impairment using technology-specific definitions, models, and mitigation approaches, which hinders cross-domain comparison and transferable design insight. This survey provides a unified treatment of pointing errors across optical and high-frequency wireless communications. We establish consistent terminology and a cross-technology taxonomy of pointing errors, review angular misalignment and statistical distribution models, and analyze their impact on system performance. Mitigation techniques are systematically surveyed with emphasis on optical systems and their connection to underlying pointing-error models. The survey further provides a detailed examination of pointing-error effects in orbital angular momentum (OAM) links and quantum optical communications, and surveys the corresponding mitigation approaches tailored to mode-dependent impairments and quantum measurement constraints. The survey also outlines open challenges and future research directions. By consolidating fragmented literature into a coherent framework, this work supports consistent analysis and robust design of next-generation directional communication systems.

title: Efficient Approximation of Goldbach Partition Function Using Machine Learning Models
authors: Cheng-Zhe Wu, Shyong Jian Shyu
abstract: Goldbach’s conjecture, still unproven, posits that every even integer greater than 2 can be expressed as the sum of two primes. The Goldbach partition function G(n) counts the number of such prime pairs for even integer n > 2. While several analytic approximations exist, recent studies have explored machine learning approaches to estimate G(n). Building on prior architectures, we propose refined models that integrate Euler’s totient function φ(n) as a primary feature, combined with nonlinear logarithmic-exponential transformations, data exchange, residual learning, and deeper layers with increasing unit sizes. These innovations enable more accurate and stable approximations. Experimental results confirm that our models consistently outperform existing ones by achieving lower mean squared errors and Error rates. Our best-performing model not only delivers reliable accuracy but also demonstrates strong robustness across various test ranges.

title: A Super Low Frequency Transmitter for Ground-transparent Communication Across Four-layer Medium
authors: Xie He, Jianya Zhang, Qiang Zhou, Changfeng Liang
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Rotating magnet based mechanical antenna (RMBMA) is a novel super low frequency electromagnetic transmitting technology. It uses the mechanical rotary motion of permanent magnet to directly excite electromagnetic waves and is expected to achieve the miniaturization of the super low frequency ground-transparent transmitting system. To reveal the ground-transparent propagation characteristics of the RMBMA, this paper establishes a four-layer medium model of air - reinforced concrete - air - underground rock layer. By using the Helmholtz equations and the Sommerfeld integral, the analytical expression of the RMBMA in the four-layer medium is given. Based on the electromagnetic field simulation software FEKO, the correctness of the proposed four-layer medium propagation model is verified, and the directional characteristics of electromagnetic wave propagation are analyzed. Moreover, the effects of the reinforced concrete layer conductivity and operating frequency on the propagation characteristics of the RMBMA are investigated, and a method for extending the horizontal transmission distance is provided. A prototype of the RMBMA is set up to conduct ground-transparent propagation and communication experiments. This verifies the feasibility of the scheme and lays a foundation for the realization of efficient and miniaturized ground-transparent communication.

title: ilLog: Incremental Learning Based Anomaly Detection from Evolving System Logs
authors: Jiyu Tian, Mingchu Li, Zumin Wang, Liming Chen, Jing Qin, Jianyuan Gan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Log anomaly detection (LAD) is of paramount importance to enhance the reliability and stability of software systems. Current state-of-the-art LAD suffers a significant performance degradation when dealing with consistently evolving log events caused by system updates. To build a reliable LAD model under the context of log data evolution, we propose an incremental learning-based method for LAD, namely ilLog, to avoid catastrophic forgetting of previously learned knowledge while continuously updating the model for better detection when processing the evolving log events. In particular, we design a novel entropy-driven sorting algorithm for real log sample replay, which enables the preservation of old knowledge via storing representative samples with discrete sequence features from previous tasks. Additionally, we introduce a Halton-based low discrepancy sequence to better approximate the sliced Cram´ er distance between the probability distributions of two models, thus enhancing the model learning capability. Based on a standard incremental learning protocol setting, we evaluate the newly proposed ilLog method on three publicly available datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves the best performance compared to SOTA LAD methods and models by applying existing IL-based methods in evolving software systems.

title: Vibration Signal Generation for Straddle-Monorail Gearboxes Using a Physics-Conditioned VQ-VAE Model
authors: Ling Zhao, Hangjun Wu, Na Zhang, Gang Wu
abstract: Accurate fault diagnosis of straddle-monorail gearboxes is essential for operational safety. However, data-driven methods are often limited by the scarcity of labeled fault samples. Existing generative approaches, such as generative adversarial networks and diffusion models, tend to produce kinematically inconsistent signals and require substantial computational resources. To address these issues, this article proposes a physics-conditioned vector-quantized variational autoencoder. The model incorporates gearbox dynamics into the generation process via a lightweight, differentiable physical conditioner.
