authors: Nanda Ayudhea, Delsa Arrani, Vilena Nadianty Gunawan, Rini Kurnia Sari, Aisyah Qanita, Baghas Budi Wicaksono
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Employee satisfaction has long been acknowledged as a key determinant of organizational performance, productivity, and sustainability. Employee satisfaction is affected by multiple factors, such as female leadership, organizational culture, and work discipline by multiple determinants, such as female leadership, organizational culture, and work discipline, which collectively shape employees’ motivation levels, accountability, and engagement among employees. The purpose of this study is to explore how female leadership, organizational culture, and work discipline directly affect employee satisfaction in an engineering-based context, with PT XYZ in the cement industry as the case example. A quantitative research design was applied, utilizing purposive sampling involving 100 participants. The data collected were processed through multiple regression analysis using SPSS version 22. The results of the validity and reliability testing confirmed that all measurement items were both valid and reliable, while classical assumption tests demonstrated that the regression model satisfied all statistical requirements. The results indicate that female leadership, organizational culture, and work discipline each exert a positive and significant effect on employee satisfaction. Among these, female leadership demonstrated the strongest effect, underscoring the importance of inclusive and transformational leadership in motivating employees. These results highlight the strategic importance of leadership, culture, and discipline in fostering a motivated, productive, and competitive workforce within industrial organizations.

title: BanglaFeel: Enhanced Bangla Back-Transliteration
authors: Masfiq Mahmud, Sumaiya Akter Iqra, Muhammad Anas, Mohammed Ashikur Rahman
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Banglish, a Romanized form of Bangla, poses challenges for NLP models, especially sentiment analysis. The aim of this study is to develop an effective back-transliteration model that converts Banglish into native Bangla script, thereby enhancing the accuracy of downstream NLP tasks. Our model integrates a multi-encoder architecture, combining characterlevel CNN (CharCNN) and word-level LSTM (WordLSTM) encoders with a pre-trained T5 transformer decoder. This design helps capture both fine-grained spelling variations and broader contextual relationships. In the context of noisy Banglish, this multi-encoder design is necessary rather than arbitrary: the CharCNN encoder focuses on local character n-grams to model inconsistent and non-standard spellings, the WordLSTM encoder captures sequential word dependencies in often ungrammatical sentences, and the T5 encoder provides a strong semantic prior through transfer learning from large-scale pretraining, allowing the system to map noisy surface forms onto stable meaning representations. Trained on the BanglaTLit dataset, the model achieves a BLEU score of 68.03%, ROUGE-1 F1 of 83.34%, and a BERTScore F1 of 96.38%, demonstrating its superior ability to handle the variability inherent in Banglish. This approach offers a robust solution to the transliteration problem, improving performance across various Bangla NLP applications.

title: A Time-Domain Differentiator For Time-Mode Signal Processing
authors: Angeliki Bertsatou, Alexios Tsetsonis, Aikaterini I. Chronopoulou, Konstantinos P. Pagkalos, Spyridon Vlassis
abstract: In this work, a time-domain differentiator topology suitable for synchronous time-mode signal processing is proposed. The operation principle is based on the time-mode approach and the information is represented as the pulse width of rectangular pulses. The proposed topology uses a charge-based time register as a core circuit in order to implement unit delay tap and adder. Thanks to its modular, clock-synchronous structure, the differentiator can serve as building block in more complex time-mode systems. It is designed in TSMC 65 nm technology node, with 5 MS/s sampling rate. The power consumption is 137 μW under 1.2 V voltage supply. Magnitude and phase frequency responses match the corresponding response of an ideal delayed discrete-time differentiator.

title: Deep Learning-Based Classification of Pea Leaf Diseases Using EfficientNetB7: A Scalable Approach for Agricultural Diagnostics
authors: Srikanta Kumar Mohapatra, K. K. Manivannan, Dipannita Ray, A. Parvathi Priya, Kochumol Abraham, Disha Garg
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: Pea leaf diseases affect the yield of crops and thus require a proper and efficient identification system for management. This research leverages the EfficientNetB7 architecture to classify pea leaf samples into five categories: FRESH_LEAF, DOWNY_MILDEW_LEAF, LEAFMINER_LEAF, POWDER_MILDEW_LEAF, and OTHER. The data used in this work encompasses 1,432 images from the ghetto-d dataset; the images were pre-processed by resizing, normalizing, and applying augmentation. The dataset was split into training, validation, and test subsets in an 80:10:10 ratio to ensure balanced evaluation. The proposed model achieved 85% accuracy and a high level of precision, recall, and F1 score for all classes, thus confirming the model’s good generalization capabilities. Some methods, such as dropout layers, early stopping, and learning rate scheduling, were used to reduce overfitting to improve the performance. The confusion matrix and classification report provided the model’s effectiveness, and four minor classification errors identified further optimization opportunities. This work shows that EfficientNetB7 is capable of automating disease classification and offers a scalable approach for agricultural applications. Possible further studies could enhance the present dataset by including wider datasets or applying more effective feature learning systems, such as Vision Transformers. Such developments may also enhance the automatic control of sustainable agriculture to manage crops.

title: Assessing the Relationship Between Service Quality Dimensions and Customer Trust in Digital Banking Systems
authors: Florensia Irenna, Rio Andy Bong, Welly Gusnaidi, Stefanus Rumangkit, Christopher Joshua Leksana
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This study aims to investigate the impact of digital banking service quality on customer satisfaction and its subsequent influence on customer trust in the context of intelligent technologies. Specifically, the research examines the relationship between various service quality dimensions and customer satisfaction, and how satisfaction, in turn, affects trust. The study employs a quantitative research approach, utilizing primary data collected through an online questionnaire distributed via Google Forms. A total of 312 samples were analyzed, with the sample size determined using SPSS Sample Power. The purposive sampling method was used to select participants. Data analysis was performed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) with the SmartPLS software. The findings reveal that reliability not significant on customer satisfaction, while other factors such as service efficiency, service availability, responsiveness, security, privacy, and mobile application systems do not significantly affect customer satisfaction. Additionally, customer satisfaction was found to have a significant positive effect on customer trust.

title: Beam-Reconfigurable Quadrifilar Helix Antenna Based on a Bi-Stable Origami Metamaterial
authors: Dongxu Chen, Min Li, Chenye Xu, Wanchen Yang, Weixu Yang, Mengzhe Song, Qiubo Ye, Dian Wang, Wenquan Che
publish: 2026 IEEE International Workshop on Antenna Technology (iWAT)
abstract: This paper presents a novel beam-reconfigurable quadrifilar helix antenna (QHA) integrated with a bi-stable origami metamaterial structure. The design leverages the mechanical bistability of the origami structure to switch between two distinct radiation states without requiring continuous external power. The antenna radiates with righthand circular polarization (RHCP) in both states. In the unfolded state (State I), the antenna achieves a wide half-power beamwidth (HPBW) of 182° at 1.57 GHz, suitable for broad coverage. In the folded state (State II), it exhibits a narrow HPBW of 109° at 1.22 GHz, ideal for directional tracking. This provides a beamwidth reconfiguration range of 73°. Both simulation and measurement results validate that the design is potential for 6G adaptive communication scenarios.

title: Design and simulation of an electronic dice
authors: Kathleen C. Capalaran, Ma. Isabela D. Capalaran, Hazel Roi D. Coronel, Eunice Jane D. Landicho, Christine Raine B. Santos, Jobert C. Sapaden, Johnel Ian M. Songco, Eureka Neesha M. Lapuebla, Angelica D. De Sagun, Teofilo D. De Sagun, Selverino A. Magon
publish: 2025 International Conference on Decision Aid Sciences and Applications (DASA)
abstract: This paper presents the design and simulation of an electronic dice, offering a modern alternative to traditional dice. The electronic dice utilize integrated circuits and digital components to simulate the randomness inherent in conventional dice rolls. Key features include a decade counter for generating outcomes, a function generator for pulse generation, and a 7-segment display for visual representation of numbers. The study explores the circuit design, operational functionalities, potential enhancements, and various applications of electronic dice. The findings suggest significant implications for board games, educational tools, and recreational activities, providing a reliable, interactive, and versatile solution for generating random outcomes. Additionally, the paper discusses improvements such as multi-sided dice simulation and power source optimization to enhance user experience and sustainability. Overall, electronic dice offer a novel and effective approach to simulate randomness, catering to diverse user needs in gaming and educational contexts.

title: CPS-Guard: Defensive Architectures for Securing Large Language Models in Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Md. Mazharul Islam, Mubasshir Ahmed, Niaz Ashraf Khan
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Cyber-physical systems (CPS) are increasingly adopting large language models (LLMs) for log analysis, operator support, and anomaly triage. While LLMs enable powerful reasoning and flexible natural-language interaction, their probabilistic and opaque behavior introduces new attack surfaces, including prompt injection, adversarial manipulation, and privacy leakage. Existing CPS defenses largely assume deterministic models and do not account for LLM-specific threats, whereas current LLM safety work rarely targets real-time, safety-critical control environments. To bridge this gap, we propose CPS-Guard, a defense-in-depth architecture for LLM-enabled CPS that operates under advisory-only semantics. CPS-Guard combines secure prompt handling, explicit threat modeling, a deterministic safety validator with policy and bounds checking, and anomaly-based veto over plant telemetry. We implement CPS-Guard as middleware between CPS controllers and an aligned LLM, and evaluate it on 300 synthetically generated adversarial inputs spanning prompt injection, adversarial perturbations, and poisoning-style triggers. Compared to an unprotected LLM advisory baseline, CPS-Guard reduces failure rates by more than 85 % while incurring only 10 18 % additional latency across small to large CPS workloads. These results indicate that system-level hardening can make LLM-assisted CPS substantially more robust without violating real-time constraints.

title: A Knowledge-enhanced Hierarchical Graph Transfer Learning Framework for Large-scale Mobile Traffic Prediction
authors: Xing Wang, Zhiyan Song, Zhendong Wang, Kexin Yang, Chao Deng, Junlan Feng
publish: 2025 IEEE/ACM 33rd International Symposium on Quality of Service (IWQoS)
abstract: Large-scale mobile traffic prediction is critical for intelligent telecommunication networks, but remains challenging due to complex spatial-temporal patterns and network heterogeneity. Existing transfer learning-based prediction methods, tailored for grid data, inadequately handle graph-based mobile traffic data and ignore network heterogeneity. In this paper, we propose a Knowledge-enhanced Hierarchical Graph Transfer Learning (KHGTL) framework integrating graph-level and node-level knowledge transfer, for large-scale mobile traffic prediction. We also present a Spatial-Temporal Adaptive Graph Convolutional Network to capture the intricate spatial-temporal correlations. Experiments on real datasets show KHGTL reduces MAE by 44.7% compared to baselines, outperforms full supervision with 80% faster training, addressing scalability and efficiency challenges in large-scale traffic prediction.

title: CAL-Log: Calibration-Aware Logarithmic Cost Modeling for Active Learning in Low-Resource NLP
authors: Vihanga Supasan Kariyakaranage, Banuka Athuraliya
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Building robust NLP systems for low-resource languages remains constrained by the high cost of expert annotation rather than model architecture availability. This paper proposes CAL-Log, a cost-calibrated active learning strategy that jointly accounts for (i) human annotation time and (ii) model miscalibration in early rounds, extending our prior framework for costaware active learning [23] to genuinely low-resource multilingual settings. CAL-Log introduces a logarithmic annotation-cost model to avoid the “cost-collapse” behavior of linear utility-pertoken heuristics, coupled with a calibration-aware uncertainty signal. We evaluate on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex> low-resource text classification datasets spanning 8 languages (Sinhala, Tamil, Hindi, Telugu, Yoruba, Amharic, Swahili, Xhosa) over 3 independent random seeds. Results show that CAL-Log achieves an Efficiency Score of 0.281 F1 per 1,000 minutes (mean ± std: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.432 \pm 0.217$</tex> F1 at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1,535 ~{\min}$</tex>), maintaining robust performance across all datasets. Multi-seed ablation confirms that removing the cost model raises annotation cost by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 9 \%}(+597 ~{\min})$</tex> and lowers efficiency to 0.180, while removing temperature calibration yields a less stable learning trajectory. Our stability analysis demonstrates that CAL-Log prevents the seed-dependent performance collapse observed in linear cost models (cross-seed std up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 0.296$</tex> on MasakhaNEWS-sw for CAL-Linear) and achieves superior consistency on morphologically complex tasks such as Sinhala Sentiment <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0.547 \pm 0.033$</tex> vs. <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.422 \pm 0.061)$</tex>. To our knowledge, this is the first active learning framework to jointly formalize annotation cost collapse and integrate model calibration error into the acquisition function under realistic human-time constraints for low-resource multilingual NLP.

title: An Emotion-Aware Conversational AI with Intensity Scoring, Response Ranking, and Emotional State Visualization
authors: K.M.S.S. Kumarasingha, W.M.C.J.T. Kithulwatta, M.M S.M. Hansana, S.L.C. Hansika, P.A.T.C. Wijerathna
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: Various psychological distresses are leading global healthcare challenge. Therefore, this research study proposes a multi-component, emotion-aware solution for detecting and responding sensitively to users' emotional states during conversations. This study includes emotional intensity scoring, emotion classification, & emotional response generation. Firstly, analyzing user input using a BERT-based model fine-tuned with the Go-Emotions dataset to determine emotional intensity via the NRC emotional intensity dictionary was done. The user message is also processed through a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The system uses 5 possible responses from the model for ranking their suitability The most suitable response selection collects emotional metadata about intensity and category and stores it for visualization of user-model relationship patterns. The study indicates that the responses yielded a RAG relevance score of above 0.70 in each instance with a mean value of 0.77 and emotional intensity scores of between 0.0168 and 0.1344. It means that there is a controlled emotional expressiveness variation. Centered visualization shows a gradual increase in the emotional valence of the user between -0.4 and about 0.8 in all interactions, which is a positive emotional regulation. The study observed for a positive relationship between user emotional status and system responses. This research projects practical applications for mental health support, emotion recognition and socially intelligent Artificial Intelligence tools, which needed for conversation-based adaptive agents.

title: Skill Factors Influencing Low-Code/No-Code (LCNC) Readiness Among Sri Lankan IT Undergraduate
authors: Pasindu Jayasekara, Chalani Oruthotaarachchi
publish: 2026 IEEE International Research Conference on Smart Computing and Systems Engineering (SCSE)
abstract: This study examines the skill factors influencing Low-Code/No-Code (LCNC) readiness among 144 Sri Lankan IT undergraduates using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). The analysis evaluates the effects of Business Process Skills (BPS), Collaboration Skills (CS), Governance and Compliance Awareness (GCA), and Technical and Integration Skills (TIS) on LCNC readiness, with Self-Efficacy (SE) as a mediating construct. Results reveal that CS, GCA, and TIS significantly enhance LCNC readiness (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}<\mathbf{0. 0 5}$</tex>), while BPS demonstrates a comparatively weaker impact. Furthermore, SE partially mediates several relationships, highlighting the critical role of confidence in LCNC skill acquisition. These findings provide empirical evidence to guide the integration of LCNC-oriented learning into IT curricula, thereby fostering digital transformation initiatives in developing economies.

title: HiddenGuard: Real-Time Detection and Mitigation of Steganographic Prompt Injection Attacks in Multimodal Large Language Models
authors: Vamshidhar Reddy Parupally, Monisha Gottam
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) enable powerful cross-modal reasoning but expose new security risks. One such threat is steganographic prompt injection, where malicious instructions are hidden in images. Existing defenses either incur high latency or fail to detect sophisticated attacks. We propose HiddenGuard, a real-time detection framework integrating (1) cross-modal consistency analysis, (2) wavelet-based steganographic detection, and (3) context-aware threat assessment. HiddenGuard achieves 94.7% accuracy with under 50 ms latency, outperforming prior methods while reducing overhead by 67%.

title: Volleyball Lineup Recommendation using GraphSAGE with Knowledge Distillation and Feature-Aware Modeling
authors: Wei-Chi Lin, Jenn-Jier James Lien, Ya-Ning Chang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Effective lineup selection is crucial in competitive volleyball, where team synergy often outweighs individual talent. However, traditional lineup decisions largely rely on coaches’ intuition and lack objective, data-driven support. In this paper, we propose a graph-based lineup recommendation system that models player synergy through pairwise interaction learning. Leveraging five seasons of data from the Taiwan Volleyball League, we train separate GraphSAGE models for each team to predict collaboration scores. To capture role-specific dynamics, we apply ElasticNet regression to derive position-aware feature weights. To enable scalable, cross-team generalization, these teacher models are distilled into a single student model (FinalGraph). This knowledge distillation framework achieves an $83.4 \%$ reduction in parameters, making the system efficient and interpretable. Our final system achieves an average lineup overlap of $86.2 \%$ (with the highest accuracy in the women’s division reaching $91 \%$ and the men’s division achieving up to $\mathbf{9 8 \%}$) against actual starting rosters, demonstrating a strong alignment with coaches’ real-world decisions.

title: Comparing Deep Learning Frameworks for Air Quality Index (AQI) Prediction in Bangladesh: Static vs. Year-Wise Forecasting Evaluation
authors: Fariha Zaman Nishat, Saiful Islam, Jasiya Fairiz Raisa, Mosabber Uddin Ahmed
publish: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
abstract: Air quality has significant impacts on human health, ecosystems, and wildlife, making its monitoring and forecasting a critical concern. Bangladesh frequently experiences high levels of air pollution that exceed safe thresholds. This paper presents a harmonized Air Quality Index (AQI) dataset for Bangladesh (2012-2024), covering eight cities and ten monitoring stations, and a systematic comparison of static and forecasting deep learning (DL) models for AQI prediction. Building on this dataset, two complementary modeling frameworks were developed: (1) a forecasting pipeline employing residual learning with temporal lags, and (2) a static pipeline utilizing concurrent pollutant and station data. DL architectures including Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU), Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN), Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) were systematically evaluated under static and forecasting scenarios, with ensemble methods applied to enhance prediction accuracy. Stacking ensembles consistently outperformed individual models, achieving mean absolute error (MAE) of approximately 2.33, mean squared error (MSE) of 40.56, and coefficient of determination (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex>) of 0.98 in static evaluation, and improving from MAE of 4.1 to 1.99 in forecasting with extended temporal windows. CNN and TCN demonstrated strong performance for longer horizons, whereas recurrent models and MLP were less effective. These findings can inform more accurate AQI forecasting and support evidence-based air quality management in Bangladesh.

title: Hybrid Data Preprocessing of Office Actions in Patent Prosecution History
authors: Jieh-Sheng Lee
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: This study explores data preprocessing techniques for extracting relevant information from office actions in patent prosecution history. It utilizes resources available through the USPTO’s Open Data Portal, such as the Office Action Text Retrieval API and the Enriched Citation API. By combining traditional rule-based Natural Language Processing approaches with statistical language models, this research addresses challenges in parsing complex patent texts. Specifically, a hybrid approach is implemented to integrate language models for named entity recognition and deterministic rule-based techniques for identifying claim numbers and reference locations. This combination enhances the accuracy and efficiency of data extraction processes. This study also highlights both practical applications and areas requiring further development, particularly concerning document structure and segmentation challenges within office actions. In summary, this research offers valuable insights into improving data preprocessing techniques for patent prosecution history, emphasizing the potential of hybrid approaches to effectively handle diverse and complex patent texts.

title: Multi-Agent AI System for Personalized Medication Adherence in Retail Pharmacies
authors: Roopam Dhanraj Choudhari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Medication non-adherence remains a critical public health challenge, contributing to preventable hospitalizations and healthcare costs exceeding ${\$}100$ billion annually in the United States. Traditional adherence interventions rely on static reminder systems and single-model prediction approaches that fail to address the complex behavioral patterns underlying nonadherence. This paper proposes a novel agentic artificial intelligence framework designed to transform medication adherence support through a multi-agent pharmacy ecosystem. The system integrates six specialized AI agents: Risk Prediction, Reasoning, Intervention Planning, Personal Health Companion, Pharmacist Assist, and Digital Twin Simulation. Combining gradient boosted decision trees with large language models, the framework provides explainable risk prediction and autonomous intervention generation using reinforcement learning. We present the system architecture, agent specifications, and validate the approach through MEPS dataset with augmented behavioral features and three detailed case studies demonstrating practical applicability across diverse patient populations. The proposed framework addresses fundamental limitations of existing systems by providing explainable predictions, contextual reasoning about adherence barriers, and adaptive intervention strategies tailored to individual patient needs.

title: Trajectory-centric Probing of Frozen Chest CT Foundation Embeddings for Multi-endpoint Lung Cancer Phenotyping
authors: Kuan-Ting Wu, Hsu-Ching Huang, Wei-Kai Lee, Wei-An Chen, Chien-Ying Huang, Heng-Sheng Chao, Yung-Hung Luo, Jing-Yao Weng, Yi-Chen Yeh, Yuh-Min Chen, Yu-Te Wu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Serial chest CT is routinely acquired during the preoperative workup of lung cancer, yet most imaging AI studies treat each scan as an isolated snapshot. We study whether frozen chest CT foundation embeddings can support clinically meaningful two-timepoint reasoning without end-toend fine-tuning [1]. Using a paired preoperative cohort with two CT exams per patient (prior exam $t_{S}$ and the preoperative exam $t_{0}$), we construct three longitudinal feature types from each encoder [2], [3]: the single-scan embedding $e\left(t_{S}\right)$, the change vector $\Delta e=e\left(t_{0}\right)-e\left(t_{S}\right)$, and the annualized velocity $v=\Delta e / \Delta t_{\text {years }}$. To mitigate acquisition/protocol heterogeneity across timepoints, we apply fold-wise CORAL alignment [4] and L2 normalization prior to probing. We evaluate a nonlinear probe (RBF-SVM) [5], [6] under 5-fold stratified crossvalidation with class-balanced training, and quantify uncertainty by bootstrap confidence intervals computed from out-of-fold predictions [7]. Across six binary endpoints spanning reportderived progression and pathology/molecular phenotypes, our framework demonstrates that longitudinal information provides statistically significant additive signal beyond single-timepoint embeddings, particularly for progression phenotypes, even without task-specific fine-tuning.

title: Evaluating Agentic AI Systems in Manufacturing: A Review of Taxonomy, Challenges and Future Directions
authors: Nastaran Moradzadeh Farid, Sara Shafiee
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems are rapidly transforming the landscape of intelligent automation by enabling autonomous decision-making, planning, and interaction with complex environments. As these systems grow in capability and complexity, evaluating their performance becomes increasingly critical, yet remains a substantial challenge. In manufacturing, where agentic AI is increasingly applied to tasks such as predictive maintenance and quality control, standardized evaluation frameworks are still lacking. Prior research has proposed structured categories for this purpose in general scope, such as analyzing submodules like tool use, reasoning, memory, and self-learning, or evaluating overall high-level performance. In this paper, we aim to review the evaluation approaches of agentic AI systems, with a specific focus on applications in manufacturing and studies released between 2024-2025 and proposes a three-dimensional taxonomy spanning scope, methodology, and analysis. Reviewing 31 studies, we find evaluations are mostly small-scale proofs of concept with limited metrics, while reliability, safety, and large-scale benchmarking are overlooked. We propose a manufacturing-oriented taxonomy and reporting checklist that synthesize existing evaluation practices into a standardized framework for reproducibility and transparency. Our synthesis highlights gaps and offers guidelines for future industrial pilots.

title: Advancing Security in Android Devices for malware detection using Machine Learning and Deep Learning Techniques
authors: Monika Sharma, Ajay Kaul, Naveen Kumar Gondhi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Advances in Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AAIML)
abstract: Intelligent and adaptable security measures have become necessary due to the swift increase in the frequency and complexity of cyber-attacks. Among these, machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) have shown promise in improving cybersecurity frameworks. Due to their dominance in the mobile ecosystem, Android smartphones are increasingly being targeted by malicious applications for attacks. The complexity of the Android ecosystem and the dynamic nature of attack routes make it difficult to detect such malware. This study examines the effectiveness of machine learning methods in categorizing apps using extracted feature patterns in order to detect Android malware. The study makes use of two benchmark datasets, Android MalGenome and Drebin, from which three subsets are obtained: (i) the API-permission dataset, (ii) the only API dataset, and (iii) the only permission dataset. These subsets are used to evaluate and compare the classification performance of six supervised machine learning algorithms: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest (RF), Artificial Neural Network (ANN), AdaBoost, and XGBoost. The comparison analysis offers empirical information about the performance of various algorithms across feature subsets. The findings show that ensemble-based models, like Random Forest and XGBoost, are more efficient at identifying new malware variants, which helps make Android security systems smarter and more effective.

title: Analysis of Atomic Structures in Ultrathin Crystalline/Amorphous Alumina Films Using STEM-EELS and Virtual Reference Spectra
authors: Yusuke Yuzawa, Takanori Asano, Kenji Nakamura, Yuta Aiba, Daisuke Hagishima, Masumi Saitoh, Hiroki Tanaka
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: We investigated the atomic structures of ultrathin alumina films using scanning transmission electron microscopy combined with electron energy-loss spectroscopy (STEM-EELS). To analyze the effects of electron-beam damage causing crystalline-to-amorphous transitions, we used multiple virtual reference spectra calculated from amorphous atomic structure models via first-principles molecular dynamics simulations. We found that the crystalline-to-amorphous transition occurs during the repeated STEM-EELS measurement, which is consistent with nano-beam diffraction results. This approach is effective for the assessment of the atomic structures before damage in ultra-thin films in semiconductor devices.

title:
P-GaN-Gate GaN-on-Si HEMTs with BV>1200 V and Excellent Dynamic Performance Realized Through Low Power NH
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Plasma Acceptor Re-Passivation
authors: Zhaofeng Wang, Zhihong Liu, Weichuan Xing, Weihang Zhang, Shenglei Zhao, Xiangdong Li, Shuzhen You, Jincheng Zhang, Yue Hao
publish: 2026 10th IEEE Electron Devices Technology &amp; Manufacturing Conference (EDTM)
abstract: In this letter, we report p-GaN gate enhancement-mode (E-mode) GaN-on-Si high electron mobility transistors (HEMTs) fabricated using an Mg acceptor re-passivation technique (ARP HEMT) via NH<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">₃</inf> plasma treatment. The gate-to-source and gate-to-drain access regions were subjected to hydrogen ion implantation through NH₃ plasma treatment in a plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition (PECVD) system, forming a high-resistance cap layer. The fabricated device demonstrates a high threshold voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">TH</inf>) of 2.1 V, a low gate reverse leakage current (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">gr</inf>) of 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">⁻¹¹</sup> A/mm, and a high ON/OFF current ratio (I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ON</inf>/I<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OFF</inf>) of up to 3×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">⁹</sup>. The dynamic on-resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on,d</inf>) measured under a quiescent drain voltage (V<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds,q</inf>) of 600 V with a stress time of 1200 s is only 1.03 times the static on-resistance (R<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on,s</inf>) at 25°C. A breakdown voltage (BV<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">OFF</inf>) of 1298 V and a specific on-resistance of 0.85 mΩ•cm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> were achieved in a device with Lgd=6 μm after additional SiN passivation. The developed fabrication process shows significant potential for application in GaN-based power transistors.

title: Learn to Solve Constrained Routing Problems in V2V Social Vehicle Networks
authors: Wei He, Junliang Ye, Lijun Wang, Xiaohu Ge, Tao Han
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: In vehicle social networks (VSN), multi-hop vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) routing must minimize end-to-end delay while satisfying a social trust budget. This trust-constrained routing can be formulated as an NP-hard resource-constrained shortest path problem (RCSPP), while the per-link delay is analytically intractable because the global co-channel interference is unknown. To this end, this paper proposes a proactive infeasibility pruning (PIP) mechanism within a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) framework. PIP constructs a budget-consistent action mask from a shortest-path-to-go prior, providing a conditional guarantee that any policy respecting the mask produces trust-feasible paths. The empirical conditioning rate of this guarantee matches the feasibility ceiling of an exact label-setting (LS) oracle to within 0.8 percentage points across the full trust-budget range. A graph neural network delay surrogate learns the interference-inclusive delay from local observations. System-level evaluation on the Wuhan Optics Valley, China, traffic demonstrates that the proposed method approaches the routing feasibility upper bound and delivers 5.3× more packets than the deep reinforcement learning baselines using only local one-hop information.

title: Interactive Residual Domain Adaptation Networks for Partial Transfer Industrial Fault Diagnosis
authors: Gecheng Chen, Zheng Luo, Zeyu Yang, Kai Wang, Xinkai Chen, Jianqiang Li, Chengwen Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The partial domain adaptation (PDA) challenge is a prevalent issue in industrial fault diagnosis. Current PDA approaches primarily rely on adversarial learning for domain adaptation and use reweighting strategies to exclude source samples deemed outliers. However, the transferability of features diminishes from general feature extraction layers to higher task-specific layers in adversarial learning-based adaptation modules, leading to significant negative transfer in PDA settings. We term this issue the adaptation-discrimination paradox (ADP). Furthermore, reweighting strategies often suffer from unreliable pseudo-labels, compromising their effectiveness. In this work, we propose a novel PDA framework called Interactive Residual Domain Adaptation Networks (IRDAN), which introduces domain-wise models for each domain to provide a new perspective for the PDA challenge. Each domain-wise model is equipped with a residual domain adaptation (RDA) block to preserve the discriminative structure of each domain and mitigate the ADP. Additionally, we introduce a confident information flow via an interactive learning strategy, training the modules of IRDAN sequentially to avoid cross-interference. We also establish a reliable stopping criterion for selecting the best-performing model, ensuring practical usability in real-world applications. Experiments have demonstrated the superior performance of the proposed IRDAN.

title: Rethinking the Role of Vector Quantization for Blind Image Restoration_supp1-3688406.pdf
authors: Jun Xu
abstract: Vector-Quantization (VQ) generative models like VQGAN have been widely employed in blind image restoration (BIR) methods to learn powerful high-quality (HQ) priors. However, the inconsistency between the generation-oriented VQ and the reconstruction-oriented BIR tasks would lead to suboptimal restoration performance, which has rarely been discussed. In this paper, we first investigate the role of VQ in VQ-based BIR methods and demonstrate that the HQ feature extracted from the HQ image achieves much better reconstruction results than the correspondingly VQ-quantized HQ feature. This inspires us to use the HQ feature as a better learning target than the VQ-quantized feature, which potentially improve the restoration of low-quality (LQ) images. For flexible approximation of the HQ feature, we propose a Vector Regression (VR) module to learn regression weights over the HQ codebook, providing more accurate feature representation than the VQ operation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our VR module consistently improves four VQ-based BIR methods on different BIR tasks. The code and pretrained models will be publicly released.

title: Advanced 3-ω Method for Evaluating Anisotropic Thermal Conductivity in Carbon Nanotubes
authors: T.-W. Chen, M.-H. Liao, C.-W. Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: As the limitations of Moore’s law become increasingly apparent, three-dimensional integrated circuits (3-DICs) have emerged as a viable solution to sustain the scaling and performance of semiconductor devices. However, the vertical integration of components in 3-DICs introduces significant thermal management challenges due to increased power density and restricted heat dissipation pathways. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been proposed as a promising alternative to copper for filling through silicon vias (TSVs), owing to their superior thermal conductivity, electrical performance, and mechanical stability. Notably, CNTs exhibit anisotropic thermal transport properties, which can be exploited to enhance heat dissipation efficiency in both the cross-plane and in-plane directions within 3-DIC architectures. Despite their potential, limited research has focused on characterizing the directional thermal behavior of CNT films. In this study, we present a refined measurement technique based on the conventional third harmonic (3-ω) method, specifically designed to quantify the thermal conductivity of CNT films along both principal directions. The methodology employs two metal lines, one functioning as a heater and the other as a temperature probe, to detect thermal fluctuations and facilitate accurate κ-value extraction. This work offers a comprehensive framework encompassing the measurement principle, fabrication process flow, and experimental design, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of CNTs’ anisotropic thermal characteristics and their applicability in advanced microelectronic systems.

title: Distilling Large Language Models for Network Active Queue Management_supp1-3690076.pdf
authors: Shiva Raj Pokhrel
abstract: We propose AQM-LLM, a framework that distills Large Language Models for Active Queue Management in modern networks. Unlike conventional learning-based AQMs that require extensive feature engineering and struggle with dynamic conditions, our approach leverages the contextual reasoning capability of LLMs to enhance the Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput (L4S) architecture with minimal manual intervention. The L4S-LLM design introduces three key components: (i) a state encoder that transforms heterogeneous network telemetry into token embeddings, (ii) a specialized L4S-LLM head that produces congestion actions in a single inference step, and (iii) a data-driven Low-Rank Adaptation scheme that drastically reduces trainable parameters while preserving accuracy. Our open-source FreeBSD-14 implementation demonstrates improved queue delay stability and higher bandwidth utilization for both DCTCP and UDP Prague traffic. We emphasize that this work demonstrates architectural feasibility through controlled experiments; deployment on router-class hardware will require additional model optimization (e.g., compression, pruning, or quantization) and is left for future work.

title: Gradient Boosting for Automatic Epileptic Seizure Detection in EEG Signals: A Comparative Study with Classical Models
authors: Ignacio D. Lezcano, Edgar H. Ayala-Britez, Diego P. Pinto-Roa
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The automatic detection of epileptic seizures from EEG signals is a clinical and technical necessity for improving monitoring and medical decision-making. The literature has reported on classical machine learning and deep learning approaches, the former being efficient and the latter requiring significant computational resources. In this regard, it is necessary to develop solutions that achieve a good balance between performance and low computational training costs. The objective of this study is to evaluate an ensemble classifier based on Gradient Boosting, comparing it with classic machine learning methods (SVM, KNN, GNB, DT, LDA, and LR) for binary seizure/non-seizure detection on EEG segments in accordance with the technical recommendations of the ILAE. From 10-second segments of raw signals from the CHB-MIT Scalp EEG Database, feature extraction obtained thirty linear temporal features per channel to capture statistical, shape, and dynamic information in the time domain. The models were trained using stratified crossvalidation with optimal hyperparameters. Gradient Boosting achieved the best overall performance, outperforming classical classifiers in all evaluated metrics, with the following results: Accuracy = 0.9793, Precision = 0.9868, Recall = 0.9720, F1-Score = 0.9792, and Specificity (TNR)=0.9866. The results demonstrate that the Gradient Boosting classifier, when tuned and evaluated using stratified procedures, is a promising alternative for the automatic detection of epileptic seizures in EEG signals. These findings support the use of ensemble approaches as a complementary tool in monitoring systems and clinical decision support.
